title: Preamble of India
verbatim_text_excerpt: WE, THE PEOPLE OF INDIA, having solemnly resolved to constitute India into a SOVEREIGN SOCIALIST SECULAR DEMOCRATIC REPUBLIC and to secure to all its citizens: JUSTICE, social, economic and political; LIBERTY of thought, expression, belief, faith and worship; EQUALITY of status and of opportunity; and to promote among them all FRATERNITY assuring the dignity of the individual and the unity and integrity of the Nation
simplified_explanation: The Preamble is the introduction to the Constitution. It declares India to be Sovereign (independent), Socialist (state-controlled mixed economy), Secular (no state religion), Democratic (people elect representatives), and Republic (elected head of state). It guarantees Justice, Liberty, Equality, and Fraternity to all citizens.
keywords: Preamble,Sovereign,Socialist,Secular,Democratic,Republic,Justice,Liberty,Equality,Fraternity
landmark_case_1: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
lc1_holding_summary: Preamble is part of the Constitution and can be amended but its basic structure cannot be altered
landmark_case_2: SR Bommai v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Secularism is a basic feature of the Constitution; misuse of religion in politics justifies President's Rule
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

part_chapter: Part I - The Union and its Territory
title: Name and Territory of the Union
verbatim_text_excerpt: India, that is Bharat, shall be a Union of States
simplified_explanation: India is officially called India or Bharat. It is a Union of States — not a federation — meaning states cannot secede. The term 'Union' was deliberately used to show the indestructible nature of the country.
keywords: Union of States,Bharat,India,Territory,Indestructible
landmark_case_1: In re Berubari Union
lc1_holding_summary: Ceding territory to a foreign country requires constitutional amendment under Article 368
landmark_case_2: Rameshwar Prasad v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: The expression Union of States reinforces that no state can claim the right to secede
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

part_chapter: Part I - The Union and its Territory
title: Formation of New States
verbatim_text_excerpt: Parliament may by law form a new State by separation of territory from any State, or by uniting two or more States or parts of States or by uniting any territory to a part of any State
simplified_explanation: Parliament alone has the power to create new states, alter boundaries, or rename states. The concerned state legislature's opinion must be taken but is not binding on Parliament. No state's consent is required.
keywords: New States,Bifurcation,Reorganisation,Parliament,State Legislature
landmark_case_1: Babulal Parate v. State of Bombay
lc1_holding_summary: State legislature's view is not binding on Parliament while reorganising states
landmark_case_2: In re Article 3 of the Constitution
lc2_holding_summary: Parliament has plenary power to alter boundaries and form new states
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Citizenship at commencement of Constitution
verbatim_text_excerpt: At the commencement of this Constitution, every person who has his domicile in the territory of India and who was born in the territory of India shall be a citizen of India
simplified_explanation: At the time the Constitution came into force (26 Jan 1950), persons domiciled in India and born here, or whose parents were born here, or who had been resident for at least 5 years, became Indian citizens automatically.
keywords: Citizenship,Domicile,Birth,Commencement
landmark_case_1: Izhar Ahmad Khan v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Citizenship determined by domicile and birth as prescribed under Part II
landmark_case_2: State of UP v. Shah Mohammad
lc2_holding_summary: Voluntary acquisition of citizenship of a foreign state terminates Indian citizenship
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Definition of State
verbatim_text_excerpt: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires, 'the State' includes the Government and Parliament of India and the Government and the Legislature of each of the States and all local or other authorities within the territory of India or under the control of the Government of India
simplified_explanation: 'State' for Fundamental Rights purposes includes not just Central and State governments but also local bodies, statutory corporations, government companies, and any authority exercising governmental functions. This broad definition ensures Fundamental Rights can be enforced against all arms of the state.
keywords: State,Fundamental Rights,Government,Authority,Enforcement
landmark_case_1: Ajay Hasia v. Khalid Mujib
lc1_holding_summary: A body substantially financed and controlled by government is 'State' under Article 12
landmark_case_2: Pradeep Kumar Biswas v. Indian Institute of Chemical Biology
lc2_holding_summary: Functional and financial test used to determine if an entity is 'State'
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights
verbatim_text_excerpt: All laws in force in the territory of India immediately before the commencement of this Constitution, in so far as they are inconsistent with the provisions of this Part, shall, to the extent of such inconsistency, be void
simplified_explanation: Any law (pre-constitutional or post-constitutional) that violates Fundamental Rights is void to the extent of inconsistency. Article 13 also includes ordinances, bye-laws, regulations, and notifications under its definition of 'law', ensuring comprehensive protection of Fundamental Rights.
keywords: Void,Inconsistency,Judicial Review,Law,Pre-constitutional Laws
landmark_case_1: Shankari Prasad v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Constitutional amendments are not 'law' under Article 13 and hence can abridge Fundamental Rights
landmark_case_2: Golak Nath v. State of Punjab
lc2_holding_summary: Parliament cannot amend Fundamental Rights as amendments are 'law' under Article 13
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Right to Equality
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or the equal protection of the laws within the territory of India
simplified_explanation: Every person (citizen or non-citizen) is equal before the law and entitled to equal protection. Equality before law (British concept) means no one is above the law. Equal protection (American concept) means equals must be treated equally. Reasonable classification is permitted if based on intelligible differentia with rational nexus to the object.
keywords: Equality,Intelligible Differentia,Rational Nexus,Reasonable Classification,Rule of Law
landmark_case_1: E.P. Royappa v. State of Tamil Nadu
lc1_holding_summary: Article 14 strikes at arbitrariness; equality and non-arbitrariness are twin facets
landmark_case_2: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Articles 14, 19, and 21 form a golden triangle; law must be non-arbitrary, fair and reasonable
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Prohibition of discrimination
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall not discriminate against any citizen on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth or any of them
simplified_explanation: The State cannot discriminate against citizens on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth alone. However, special provisions for women, children (Clause 3), and socially/educationally backward classes or SC/STs (Clause 4 & 5) are permitted as exceptions.
keywords: Discrimination,Religion,Race,Caste,Sex,Place of Birth,Protective Discrimination
landmark_case_1: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Section 377 BNS struck down; sexual orientation is a ground covered under Article 15
landmark_case_2: Naz Foundation v. Govt of NCT of Delhi
lc2_holding_summary: Discrimination based on sexual orientation violates Article 15
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry
exceptions_and_limitations: Permits special provisions for women and children, socially and educationally backward classes, Scheduled Castes/Scheduled Tribes, and economically weaker sections where constitutionally valid.

title: Equality of opportunity in public employment
verbatim_text_excerpt: There shall be equality of opportunity for all citizens in matters relating to employment or appointment to any office under the State
simplified_explanation: Every citizen has equal opportunity for government jobs. The State can provide reservations for backward classes if they are not adequately represented. Clause 4A allows reservations in promotion for SC/STs. The creamy layer concept has been applied by courts to OBC reservations.
keywords: Public Employment,Reservation,Backward Classes,SC/ST,Creamy Layer,Promotion
landmark_case_1: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: 50% cap on reservations upheld; creamy layer must be excluded; no reservation in promotion for OBCs
landmark_case_2: M. Nagaraj v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Parliament can provide reservations in promotion for SC/ST subject to quantifiable data on backwardness
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry
exceptions_and_limitations: Permits reservations/affirmative action under clauses 4, 4A, 4B and 6, subject to constitutional limits.

title: Abolition of Untouchability
verbatim_text_excerpt: 'Untouchability' is abolished and its practice in any form is forbidden. The enforcement of any disability arising out of 'Untouchability' shall be an offence punishable in accordance with law
simplified_explanation: Untouchability in any form is constitutionally abolished. This right is enforceable against private individuals too, not just the State. The Protection of Civil Rights Act 1955 and SC/ST (Prevention of Atrocities) Act 1989 enforce this article.
keywords: Untouchability,Social Discrimination,SC/ST Atrocities,Civil Rights,Social Justice
landmark_case_1: People's Union for Democratic Rights v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Article 17 is enforceable against private individuals as well as the state
landmark_case_2: Devarajiah v. Padmanna
lc2_holding_summary: Article 17 is a blanket prohibition and applies to all forms of untouchability
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Protection of certain rights regarding freedom of speech
verbatim_text_excerpt: All citizens shall have the right to freedom of speech and expression; to assemble peaceably and without arms; to form associations or unions; to move freely throughout the territory of India; to reside and settle in any part of India; and to practise any profession
simplified_explanation: Article 19 guarantees 6 freedoms (originally 7) to citizens only. Freedom of speech includes press freedom, right to information, commercial speech, and right to silence. Each freedom has reasonable restrictions under Clauses 2-6 such as sovereignty, public order, morality, and security.
keywords: Freedom of Speech,Press Freedom,Assembly,Association,Movement,Profession,Reasonable Restrictions
landmark_case_1: Romesh Thappar v. State of Madras
lc1_holding_summary: Freedom of speech and press is the foundation of democratic governance
landmark_case_2: Shreya Singhal v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Section 66A IT Act struck down as unconstitutional for violating free speech
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Protection of life and personal liberty
verbatim_text_excerpt: No person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law
simplified_explanation: The most expansive Fundamental Right. 'Life' means much more than mere animal existence — it includes the right to livelihood, education, health, privacy, dignity, clean environment, speedy trial, legal aid, and many more. The procedure must be fair, just, and reasonable (not merely any procedure prescribed by law).
keywords: Right to Life,Personal Liberty,Due Process,Dignity,Privacy,Livelihood,Health
landmark_case_1: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Procedure under Article 21 must be fair, just and reasonable; not arbitrary or oppressive
landmark_case_2: K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Right to Privacy is a Fundamental Right under Article 21
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

title: Right to Education
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children of the age of six to fourteen years in such manner as the State may, by law, determine
simplified_explanation: Inserted by 86th Constitutional Amendment 2002. Makes education a Fundamental Right for children aged 6-14. The Right to Education Act 2009 operationalises this right mandating 25% seats for disadvantaged children in private schools.
keywords: Right to Education,RTE Act,86th Amendment,Children,Free Education,Compulsory Education
landmark_case_1: Society for Unaided Private Schools of Rajasthan v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: RTE Act 25% reservation for disadvantaged children in private schools is constitutional
landmark_case_2: Ashok Kumar Thakur v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Right to education must be real and effective, not illusory
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Protection against arrest and detention
verbatim_text_excerpt: No person who is arrested shall be detained in custody without being informed, as soon as may be, of the grounds for such arrest nor shall he be denied the right to consult, and to be defended by, a legal practitioner of his choice
simplified_explanation: Every arrested person has the right to be informed of grounds of arrest, consult a lawyer, and be produced before a magistrate within 24 hours. Preventive detention laws are an exception to these safeguards but have their own constitutional safeguards (maximum 3 months without Advisory Board approval).
keywords: Arrest,Detention,Magistrate,24 Hours,Legal Aid,Preventive Detention
landmark_case_1: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal
lc1_holding_summary: Laid down detailed guidelines for arrest to prevent custodial violence
landmark_case_2: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
lc2_holding_summary: Preventive detention under law satisfying Article 22 safeguards is valid
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labour
verbatim_text_excerpt: Traffic in human beings and begar and other similar forms of forced labour are prohibited and any contravention of this provision shall be an offence punishable in accordance with law
simplified_explanation: Human trafficking, begar (forced labour without payment), and bonded labour are constitutionally prohibited. Enforceable against private individuals too. The Bonded Labour System (Abolition) Act 1976 and Immoral Traffic (Prevention) Act 1956 operationalise this article.
keywords: Human Trafficking,Begar,Forced Labour,Bonded Labour,Exploitation
landmark_case_1: People's Union for Democratic Rights v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Payment below minimum wage constitutes forced labour under Article 23
landmark_case_2: Bandhua Mukti Morcha v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: State has affirmative obligation to identify and release bonded labourers
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Prohibition of employment of children in factories
verbatim_text_excerpt: No child below the age of fourteen years shall be employed to work in any factory or mine or engaged in any other hazardous employment
simplified_explanation: Children below 14 cannot be employed in factories, mines, or hazardous work. The Child Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Amendment Act 2016 extended this to prohibit employment in all occupations for children below 14 and in hazardous occupations for children aged 14-18.
keywords: Child Labour,Factory,Mine,Hazardous Employment,Children Rights
landmark_case_1: M.C. Mehta v. State of Tamil Nadu
lc1_holding_summary: Directions for rehabilitation of child labour and compulsory education
landmark_case_2: Bandhua Mukti Morcha v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Child labour is violative of Articles 24, 39(e), and 39(f)
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Freedom of conscience and free profession of religion
verbatim_text_excerpt: Subject to public order, morality and health and to the other provisions of this Part, all persons are equally entitled to freedom of conscience and the right freely to profess, practise and propagate religion
simplified_explanation: Every person has the right to freely hold, practice, and propagate their religion. However, the state can regulate secular activities associated with religion, provide for social welfare, and reform Hindu institutions. The right to propagate does not include the right to convert by force or fraud.
keywords: Religion,Conscience,Propagation,Secular Activities,Religious Freedom
landmark_case_1: Stanislaus v. State of Madhya Pradesh
lc1_holding_summary: Right to propagate religion does not include right to forcible or fraudulent conversion
landmark_case_2: Shirur Mutt Case (Commissioner v. LT Swamiar)
lc2_holding_summary: Religion includes practices and rituals integral to religion; state cannot interfere with essential religious practices
category: Fundamental Rights - Right to Freedom of Religion
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry
exceptions_and_limitations: Subject to public order, morality, health, and the other Fundamental Rights.

title: Freedom to manage religious affairs
verbatim_text_excerpt: Subject to public order, morality and health, every religious denomination or any section thereof shall have the right to establish and maintain institutions for religious and charitable purposes; to manage its own affairs in matters of religion
simplified_explanation: Every religious denomination can establish and manage its own religious institutions, manage religious affairs, own property, and administer it. But the State can regulate the secular activities of a religious institution.
keywords: Religious Denomination,Manage Affairs,Property,Administration
landmark_case_1: Shirur Mutt Case
lc1_holding_summary: Religious denomination can manage its own affairs; state cannot interfere in essentially religious matters
landmark_case_2: Durgah Committee Ajmer v. Syed Hussain Ali
lc2_holding_summary: Only essential religious practices are protected; mere superstitious practices are not
category: Fundamental Rights - Right to Freedom of Religion
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Protection of interests of minorities
verbatim_text_excerpt: Any section of the citizens residing in the territory of India or any part thereof having a distinct language, script or culture of its own shall have the right to conserve the same
simplified_explanation: Any section of citizens with a distinct language, script, or culture can conserve it. No citizen can be denied admission to state-funded educational institutions on grounds of religion, race, caste, or language.
keywords: Minority,Language,Script,Culture,Conservation
landmark_case_1: T.M.A. Pai Foundation v. State of Karnataka
lc1_holding_summary: Broad interpretation of minority rights; linguistic minorities also protected
landmark_case_2: State of Madras v. Champakam Dorairajan
lc2_holding_summary: Led to First Constitutional Amendment adding Clause 4 to Article 15 for reservations in education
category: Fundamental Rights - Cultural and Educational Rights
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Right of minorities to establish educational institutions
verbatim_text_excerpt: All minorities, whether based on religion or language, shall have the right to establish and maintain educational institutions of their choice
simplified_explanation: Religious and linguistic minorities can establish and administer their own educational institutions. The State cannot discriminate against minority institutions in granting aid. However, the State can impose reasonable regulations for ensuring educational standards.
keywords: Minority Educational Institutions,Religion,Language,Establish,Administer
landmark_case_1: T.M.A. Pai Foundation v. State of Karnataka
lc1_holding_summary: National level minority status for religious minorities; state level for linguistic minorities; reasonable regulation permitted
landmark_case_2: P.A. Inamdar v. State of Maharashtra
lc2_holding_summary: Minority institutions have right to admit students of their choice but must follow merit and transparency
category: Fundamental Rights - Cultural and Educational Rights
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Right to Constitutional Remedies
verbatim_text_excerpt: The right to move the Supreme Court by appropriate proceedings for the enforcement of the rights conferred by this Part is guaranteed
simplified_explanation: Called the 'heart and soul' of the Constitution by Dr. Ambedkar. Every person can directly approach the Supreme Court if their Fundamental Rights are violated. The Supreme Court can issue writs — Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Certiorari, and Quo Warranto.
keywords: Habeas Corpus,Mandamus,Prohibition,Certiorari,Quo Warranto,Supreme Court,Writ Jurisdiction
landmark_case_1: Romesh Thappar v. State of Madras
lc1_holding_summary: Article 32 is the cornerstone of the Constitution and cannot be abrogated even by emergency
landmark_case_2: ADM Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla
lc2_holding_summary: (Controversial) Right to move court suspended during emergency; later overruled by KS Puttaswamy
category: Fundamental Rights - Right to Constitutional Remedies
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Directive Principles of State Policy - Overview
verbatim_text_excerpt: The provisions contained in this Part shall not be enforceable by any court, but the principles therein laid down are nevertheless fundamental in the governance of the country and it shall be the duty of the State to apply these principles in making laws
simplified_explanation: DPSPs are non-justiciable guidelines to the State for governance. They aim to establish a welfare state. They can be classified into Socialistic (Articles 38, 39, 41, 42, 43, 47), Gandhian (Articles 40, 43, 43B, 46, 47, 48), and Liberal-Intellectual (Articles 44, 45, 48A, 49, 50, 51) categories.
keywords: DPSP,Welfare State,Non-Justiciable,Socialistic,Gandhian,Liberal
landmark_case_1: Minerva Mills v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Harmony between Fundamental Rights and DPSPs is part of the basic structure
landmark_case_2: State of Kerala v. N.M. Thomas
lc2_holding_summary: DPSPs can be used to determine reasonableness of restrictions on Fundamental Rights
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Certain principles of policy to be followed by State
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall, in particular, direct its policy towards securing that the citizens, men and women equally, have the right to an adequate means of livelihood; that the ownership and control of the material resources of the community are so distributed as best to subserve the common good
simplified_explanation: Key DPSP covering equal pay for equal work (Article 39(d)), equal right to livelihood, child protection from abuse (Article 39(e) & (f)), and equitable distribution of resources. Article 39B and 39C deal with distribution of material resources to prevent concentration of wealth.
keywords: Equal Pay,Livelihood,Material Resources,Child Protection,Wealth Distribution
landmark_case_1: Air India v. Nargesh Meerza
lc1_holding_summary: Equal pay for equal work is a constitutional goal enforceable in service matters
landmark_case_2: State of Rajasthan v. Vidhyawati
lc2_holding_summary: DPSPs must guide state policy in economic and social fields
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Uniform Civil Code
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall endeavour to secure for the citizens a uniform civil code throughout the territory of India
simplified_explanation: A directive to the State to enact a common civil law (marriage, divorce, inheritance) applicable to all citizens regardless of religion. Currently different personal laws apply to different religious communities. Goa has a common civil code. The UCC debate involves balancing national unity with religious freedom.
keywords: Uniform Civil Code,UCC,Personal Law,Secularism,Gender Justice
landmark_case_1: Mohd. Ahmed Khan v. Shah Bano Begum
lc1_holding_summary: Urged Parliament to enact UCC; Muslim women's right to maintenance highlighted
landmark_case_2: Sarla Mudgal v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Conversion to Islam for second marriage without divorce is void; UCC needed
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Protection and improvement of environment
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall endeavour to protect and improve the environment and to safeguard the forests and wild life of the country
simplified_explanation: Inserted by 42nd Amendment 1976. Imposes duty on the State to protect the environment, forests, and wildlife. This article along with Article 51A(g) (citizens' duty to protect environment) forms the constitutional basis for environmental jurisprudence in India.
keywords: Environment,Forest,Wildlife,42nd Amendment,Ecological Balance
landmark_case_1: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Ganga Pollution)
lc1_holding_summary: Polluter Pays Principle and right to clean environment derived from Articles 21 and 48A
landmark_case_2: Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Precautionary Principle and Sustainable Development are part of environmental law
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

title: Fundamental Duties
verbatim_text_excerpt: It shall be the duty of every citizen of India to abide by the Constitution and respect its ideals and institutions, the national Flag and the National Anthem; to cherish and follow the noble ideals which inspired our national struggle for freedom...
simplified_explanation: Added by 42nd Amendment 1976 on Swaran Singh Committee recommendations. Originally 10 duties, 11th added by 86th Amendment 2002 (duty of parents to provide education to children aged 6-14). Non-justiciable but courts use them to uphold laws and determine reasonableness of restrictions.
keywords: Fundamental Duties,42nd Amendment,Swaran Singh Committee,Non-Justiciable,Citizens Duty
landmark_case_1: Bijoe Emmanuel v. State of Kerala
lc1_holding_summary: Fundamental Duties cannot be enforced to compel citizens to sing national anthem
landmark_case_2: AIIMS Students' Union v. AIIMS
lc2_holding_summary: Fundamental Duties can be used to resolve conflicts between competing rights
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: The President of India
verbatim_text_excerpt: There shall be a President of India
simplified_explanation: The President is the constitutional head of India and the first citizen. All executive powers are formally vested in the President but are exercised on the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers headed by the Prime Minister. The President acts as a constitutional figurehead in India's parliamentary democracy.
keywords: President,Constitutional Head,Executive Power,Parliamentary Democracy
landmark_case_1: Ram Jawaya Kapur v. State of Punjab
lc1_holding_summary: Executive powers of the Union are vested in the President but exercised through Council of Ministers
landmark_case_2: Shamsher Singh v. State of Punjab
lc2_holding_summary: President must act on the advice of the Council of Ministers in all matters
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Pardoning Powers of President
verbatim_text_excerpt: The President shall have the power to grant pardons, reprieves, respites or remissions of punishment or to suspend, remit or commute the sentence of any person convicted of any offence in all cases where the punishment or sentence is a punishment or sentence of death
simplified_explanation: The President can pardon (completely absolve), commute (reduce severity), reprieve (postpone), or remit (reduce period of) punishment. This power is exercised on the advice of the Council of Ministers. It extends to death sentences, court martial sentences, and offences against Union laws.
keywords: Pardon,Reprieve,Remission,Commutation,Death Sentence,Mercy Petition
landmark_case_1: Epuru Sudhakar v. Govt of AP
lc1_holding_summary: Pardoning power is subject to judicial review if exercised arbitrarily, mala fide or without application of mind
landmark_case_2: Maru Ram v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: President must act on Council of Ministers' advice in pardoning; power is not absolute
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Council of Ministers to aid and advise President
verbatim_text_excerpt: There shall be a Council of Ministers with the Prime Minister at the head to aid and advise the President who shall, in the exercise of his functions, act in accordance with such advice: Provided that the President may require the Council of Ministers to reconsider such advice and the President shall act in accordance with the advice tendered after such reconsideration
simplified_explanation: The Council of Ministers headed by the PM is the real executive. The President must act on their advice. By 44th Amendment, the President can return the advice once for reconsideration but must accept the reconsidered advice. This establishes Cabinet government and collective responsibility.
keywords: Council of Ministers,Prime Minister,Aid and Advice,44th Amendment,Cabinet Responsibility
landmark_case_1: U.N.R. Rao v. Indira Gandhi
lc1_holding_summary: Council of Ministers can continue in office in caretaker capacity after dissolution of Lok Sabha
landmark_case_2: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Floor of the House is the only place to test majority; President cannot act on subjective satisfaction without objective material
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Attorney General for India
verbatim_text_excerpt: The President shall appoint a person who is qualified to be appointed a Judge of the Supreme Court to be Attorney General for India
simplified_explanation: The Attorney General is the chief legal adviser to the Government of India. Appointed by the President and holds office during the President's pleasure. Has right of audience in all courts in India. Does not hold a government office — is not a full-time government servant and can practice privately.
keywords: Attorney General,Legal Adviser,Union Government,Supreme Court
landmark_case_1: Additional District Magistrate v. S.S. Shukla
lc1_holding_summary: Attorney General's role in representing the Union government before courts
landmark_case_2: Union of India v. Association for Democratic Reforms
lc2_holding_summary: Attorney General's arguments on government's behalf do not bind courts on constitutional matters
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Constitution of Parliament
verbatim_text_excerpt: There shall be a Parliament for the Union which shall consist of the President and two Houses to be known respectively as the Council of States and the House of the People
simplified_explanation: The Indian Parliament has three components: the President, Rajya Sabha (Council of States — upper house), and Lok Sabha (House of the People — lower house). The President is an integral part of Parliament though not a member of either house.
keywords: Parliament,Rajya Sabha,Lok Sabha,Bicameral,President
landmark_case_1: Special Reference No. 1 of 2002
lc1_holding_summary: Parliamentary privileges and the relationship between Parliament and courts
landmark_case_2: Raja Ram Pal v. Speaker Lok Sabha
lc2_holding_summary: Parliament's power to expel members is subject to judicial review in cases of violation of constitutional provisions
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Definition of Money Bills
verbatim_text_excerpt: A Bill shall be deemed to be a Money Bill if it contains only provisions dealing with all or any of the following matters, namely— (a) the imposition, abolition, remission, alteration or regulation of any tax
simplified_explanation: Money Bills can only be introduced in Lok Sabha. Rajya Sabha can only suggest amendments (not compulsory) within 14 days. If Rajya Sabha doesn't act within 14 days, the bill is deemed passed. The Speaker's certification that a bill is a Money Bill is final.
keywords: Money Bill,Lok Sabha,Rajya Sabha,Speaker,Financial Legislation
landmark_case_1: Mohd. Saeed Siddiqui v. State of Uttar Pradesh
lc1_holding_summary: Money Bill certification by Speaker is final and cannot be questioned in courts
landmark_case_2: Rojer Mathew v. South Indian Bank Ltd
lc2_holding_summary: Aadhaar Act unconstitutionally passed as Money Bill; referred to larger bench
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

title: Power of President to promulgate Ordinances
verbatim_text_excerpt: If at any time, except when both Houses of Parliament are in session, the President is satisfied that circumstances exist which render it necessary for him to take immediate action, he may promulgate such Ordinances as the circumstances appear to him to require
simplified_explanation: When Parliament is not in session, the President can issue Ordinances which have the same force as Parliament-enacted laws. Ordinances must be approved by Parliament within 6 weeks of reassembly, else they lapse. An Ordinance cannot be used to amend the Constitution.
keywords: Ordinance,Legislative Power,Parliamentary Recess,Immediate Action
landmark_case_1: D.C. Wadhwa v. State of Bihar
lc1_holding_summary: Promulgating Ordinances repeatedly to avoid parliamentary scrutiny is unconstitutional
landmark_case_2: Krishna Kumar Singh v. State of Bihar
lc2_holding_summary: Ordinance-making power is a conditional power; satisfaction of President is subject to judicial review
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Establishment and constitution of Supreme Court
verbatim_text_excerpt: There shall be a Supreme Court of India consisting of a Chief Justice of India and, until Parliament by law prescribes a larger number, of not more than seven other Judges
simplified_explanation: The Supreme Court is the apex court and final interpreter of the Constitution. Judges are appointed by the President after consultation with the Chief Justice. The collegium system (evolved through NJAC judgment) governs judicial appointments. Judges hold office till age 65.
keywords: Supreme Court,Chief Justice,Judicial Appointments,Collegium,Independence of Judiciary
landmark_case_1: S.P. Gupta v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: First Judges Case: consultation with CJI not binding on government
landmark_case_2: Supreme Court Advocates on Record Association v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Second Judges Case: collegium system established; CJI's opinion is primacy
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Power of President to consult Supreme Court
verbatim_text_excerpt: If at any time it appears to the President that a question of law or fact has arisen, or is likely to arise, which is of such a nature and of such public importance that it is expedient to obtain the opinion of the Supreme Court upon it, he may refer the question to that Court for consideration
simplified_explanation: The President can seek advisory opinion from the Supreme Court on questions of law or fact of public importance. The Supreme Court may decline to give opinion. This opinion is not binding on either the President or the courts.
keywords: Advisory Opinion,Presidential Reference,Supreme Court,Public Importance
landmark_case_1: In re the Delhi Laws Act
lc1_holding_summary: Extensive use of Article 143 to clarify distribution of legislative powers
landmark_case_2: Special Reference No. 1 of 2012
lc2_holding_summary: Presidential reference on election of Vice-President and procedures
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Governors of States
verbatim_text_excerpt: There shall be a Governor for each State: Provided that nothing in this article shall prevent the appointment of the same person as Governor for two or more States
simplified_explanation: The Governor is the constitutional head of a State equivalent to the President at the Centre. Appointed by the President (effectively by the Central government). Holds office at President's pleasure. All executive actions of the state are taken in the Governor's name.
keywords: Governor,State Executive,Presidential Appointment,Constitutional Head
landmark_case_1: B.P. Singhal v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Governor can be removed but only for valid reasons; pleasure doctrine not absolute
landmark_case_2: Rameshwar Prasad v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Governor's report to President recommending President's Rule must be bona fide and based on objective material
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Council of Ministers to aid and advise Governor
verbatim_text_excerpt: There shall be a Council of Ministers with the Chief Minister at the head to aid and advise the Governor in the exercise of his functions, except in so far as he is by or under this Constitution required to act in his discretion
simplified_explanation: Similar to Article 74, the Governor must act on the CM-headed council's advice except in matters of discretion (reserving bills, reporting on breakdown of constitutional machinery, appointing CM when no clear majority). Discretionary powers are to be exercised carefully.
keywords: Council of Ministers,Chief Minister,Governor,Discretion,State Government
landmark_case_1: Nabam Rebia v. Deputy Speaker
lc1_holding_summary: Governor's discretionary powers must be exercised within constitutional bounds
landmark_case_2: Rameshwar Prasad v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Governor cannot arbitrarily refuse to invite the leader of the largest party to form government
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Assent to Bills by Governor
verbatim_text_excerpt: When a Bill has been passed by the Legislative Assembly of a State, it shall be presented to the Governor and the Governor shall declare either that he assents to the Bill, or that he withholds assent therefrom, or that he reserves the Bill for the consideration of the President
simplified_explanation: The Governor can assent, withhold assent, return a non-money bill for reconsideration once, or reserve the bill for President's consideration. The concept of 'pocket veto' (indefinitely withholding assent) has been a constitutional controversy.
keywords: Assent,Governor,Reserve,President,State Legislation
landmark_case_1: In re Article 200 of the Constitution
lc1_holding_summary: SC held Governors cannot indefinitely withhold assent; must act within reasonable time
landmark_case_2: State of Punjab v. Principal Secretary to Governor of Punjab
lc2_holding_summary: Governor's withholding of assent indefinitely amounts to unconstitutional pocket veto
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

part_chapter: Part XI - Relations between Union and States
title: Extent of laws made by Parliament and by Legislatures of States
verbatim_text_excerpt: Subject to the provisions of this Constitution, Parliament may make laws for the whole or any part of the territory of India, and the Legislature of a State may make laws for the whole or any part of the State
simplified_explanation: Parliament can make laws for the entire country; State legislatures can make laws only for their state. Parliament can also make laws for areas not covered by any state (e.g., Union Territories, international waters).
keywords: Legislative Jurisdiction,Parliament,State Legislature,Territorial Extent
landmark_case_1: GVK Industries v. Income Tax Officer
lc1_holding_summary: Parliament's power to make extraterritorial laws is not unlimited; must have nexus with India
landmark_case_2: State of Bombay v. F.N. Balsara
lc2_holding_summary: Legislative competence determined by pith and substance of the law
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

part_chapter: Part XI - Relations between Union and States
title: Adjudication of disputes relating to waters of inter-State rivers
verbatim_text_excerpt: Parliament may by law provide for the adjudication of any dispute or complaint with respect to the use, distribution or control of the waters of, or in, any inter-State river or river valley
simplified_explanation: Parliament can by law exclude the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court and all other courts in inter-state water disputes. The Inter-State River Water Disputes Act 1956 operationalises this. Tribunals like Cauvery, Krishna, Ravi-Beas Tribunals are constituted under this provision.
keywords: Inter-State River,Water Disputes,Tribunal,River Water Sharing
landmark_case_1: State of Karnataka v. State of Andhra Pradesh
lc1_holding_summary: Water disputes can only be resolved through statutory tribunal, not Supreme Court directly
landmark_case_2: Cauvery Water Disputes Tribunal
lc2_holding_summary: Supreme Court upheld tribunal allocation between Karnataka and Tamil Nadu
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Finance Commission
verbatim_text_excerpt: The President shall, within two years from the commencement of this Constitution and thereafter at the expiration of every fifth year or at such earlier time as the President considers necessary, by order constitute a Finance Commission
simplified_explanation: The Finance Commission is a constitutional body constituted every 5 years to recommend the distribution of tax revenues between the Centre and States (vertical devolution) and among States (horizontal devolution). Also recommends grants-in-aid. 16th Finance Commission is currently constituted.
keywords: Finance Commission,Devolution,Tax Revenue,Grants,Fiscal Federalism
landmark_case_1: State of Kerala v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Finance Commission recommendations are not binding but are persuasive on the government
landmark_case_2: West Bengal v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Centre-State financial relations are governed by constitutional provisions and Finance Commission recommendations
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

title: Proclamation of Emergency
verbatim_text_excerpt: If the President is satisfied that a grave emergency exists whereby the security of India or of any part of the territory thereof is threatened, whether by war or external aggression or armed rebellion, he may, by Proclamation, make a declaration to that effect
simplified_explanation: National Emergency can be proclaimed when India's security is threatened by war, external aggression, or armed rebellion. Post-44th Amendment: Cabinet must recommend in writing; Lok Sabha can revoke by simple majority; must be approved by 2/3 special majority of Parliament every 6 months.
keywords: National Emergency,Article 352,Armed Rebellion,War,44th Amendment,Cabinet Recommendation
landmark_case_1: Minerva Mills v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Emergency provisions subject to judicial review; Parliament cannot use emergency to abrogate basic structure
landmark_case_2: ADM Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla
lc2_holding_summary: (Controversial) Fundamental Rights suspended during emergency — largely overruled
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry
exceptions_and_limitations: Emergency proclamation requires constitutional conditions and parliamentary approval; subject to judicial review principles.

title: Provisions in case of failure of constitutional machinery in State
verbatim_text_excerpt: If the President, on receipt of a report from the Governor of a State or otherwise, is satisfied that the government of the State cannot be carried on in accordance with the provisions of this Constitution, the President may by Proclamation assume to himself all or any of the functions of the Government of that State
simplified_explanation: President's Rule imposed when a State's constitutional machinery fails. Governor's report is the basis. After 44th Amendment, must be approved by Parliament every 6 months (max 3 years). Post-S.R. Bommai judgment, it is subject to judicial review and the floor test.
keywords: President's Rule,Article 356,Governor's Report,State Emergency,44th Amendment
landmark_case_1: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: President's Rule subject to judicial review; imposed only when floor test proves loss of majority
landmark_case_2: Rameshwar Prasad v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: President's Rule in Bihar was unconstitutional as it was imposed to prevent majority party from forming government
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry
exceptions_and_limitations: Subject to constitutional tests, floor-test doctrine, and judicial review; cannot be used as routine political control.

title: Power of Parliament to amend the Constitution and procedure therefor
verbatim_text_excerpt: Notwithstanding anything in this Constitution, Parliament may in exercise of its constituent power amend by way of addition, variation or repeal any provision of this Constitution in accordance with the procedure laid down in this Article
simplified_explanation: Parliament can amend the Constitution by special majority (2/3 of members present and voting + more than 50% of total membership). Some provisions also require ratification by half the state legislatures. But the basic structure of the Constitution cannot be amended.
keywords: Constitutional Amendment,Special Majority,Basic Structure,Constituent Power,State Ratification
landmark_case_1: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
lc1_holding_summary: Parliament cannot amend the basic structure of the Constitution
landmark_case_2: Minerva Mills v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Clauses 4 and 5 inserted in Article 368 by 42nd Amendment struck down for destroying basic structure
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: First Constitutional Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: Added Ninth Schedule to protect land reform laws from judicial review; added reasonable restrictions to Article 19; inserted Articles 31A and 31B
simplified_explanation: The very first amendment was passed to overcome Supreme Court judgments that struck down land reform legislation. It created the controversial Ninth Schedule — a 'protected zone' for laws where courts cannot strike them down for violating Fundamental Rights. Also added protective discrimination clause (Article 15(4)) in education.
keywords: Ninth Schedule,Land Reform,Protective Discrimination,Judicial Review,Fundamental Rights
landmark_case_1: I.R. Coelho v. State of Tamil Nadu
lc1_holding_summary: Laws placed in Ninth Schedule after April 1973 are subject to judicial review if they violate basic structure
landmark_case_2: Shankari Prasad v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Constitutional amendments are not 'law' and hence not subject to Article 13
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Mini Constitution — 42nd Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: The most sweeping amendment passed during the Emergency. Added Fundamental Duties, DPSPs got precedence over Fundamental Rights, word 'Socialist' and 'Secular' added to Preamble, courts' jurisdiction restricted, Parliament's power to amend made unlimited
simplified_explanation: Called the 'Mini Constitution', it was passed during the Internal Emergency and made radical changes. Many provisions were later struck down by the Supreme Court in Minerva Mills (1980). It represents a period of constitutional stress and the danger of parliamentary overreach.
keywords: 42nd Amendment,Emergency,Mini Constitution,Preamble,Socialist,Secular,Fundamental Duties
landmark_case_1: Minerva Mills v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Several provisions of 42nd Amendment struck down for destroying balance between Fundamental Rights and DPSPs
landmark_case_2: Indira Gandhi v. Raj Narain
lc2_holding_summary: 42nd Amendment was fallout of this case where PM's election was set aside by Allahabad HC
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Restoration of Democratic Values — 44th Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: Reversed several Emergency-era changes: restored right to property as legal right (not Fundamental Right), required Cabinet's written advice before Presidential Emergency proclamation, restored right to life and personal liberty even during emergency, reduced term of Lok Sabha back to 5 years
simplified_explanation: Passed after the Emergency by the Janata government to undo the damage of the 42nd Amendment. Made Article 21 non-suspendable during emergency — a direct reversal of ADM Jabalpur. Removed right to property from Fundamental Rights and made it a constitutional right under Article 300A.
keywords: 44th Amendment,Right to Property,Emergency Safeguards,Janata Government,Article 300A
landmark_case_1: Minerva Mills v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: 44th Amendment and its restorations of democratic balance upheld
landmark_case_2: People's Union for Civil Liberties v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Article 21's non-suspendability during emergency is a key democratic safeguard
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Panchayati Raj — 73rd Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: Added Part IX to the Constitution establishing a three-tier Panchayati Raj system (Gram Panchayat, Panchayat Samiti, Zila Parishad); mandated elections every 5 years; reserved 1/3 seats for women; created State Finance Commission and State Election Commission
simplified_explanation: Constitutionalised local self-government. Made elections to Panchayats mandatory with a 5-year term. Reserved seats for SC/ST proportional to population and 1/3 for women. Created parallel constitutional bodies — State Finance Commission (financial devolution) and State Election Commission (free elections).
keywords: 73rd Amendment,Panchayati Raj,Local Self Government,Women Reservation,SC ST Reservation,State Finance Commission,State Election Commission
landmark_case_1: State of Bihar v. Bal Mukund Shah
lc1_holding_summary: Panchayats have constitutional status but their powers come from state legislation
landmark_case_2: K. Krishna Murthy v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Reservation for backward classes in Panchayat elections valid under Article 243D
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice

title: Municipalities — 74th Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: Added Part IX-A to the Constitution establishing Urban Local Bodies; created 18 functional categories (12th Schedule) for municipalities; mandated State Election Commissions; reserved seats for women and backward classes in municipalities
simplified_explanation: Constitutionalised urban local self-government. Created Ward Committees for areas with 3 lakh or more population. Listed 18 functions in the 12th Schedule for municipalities including urban planning, regulation of land use, public health, and poverty alleviation.
keywords: 74th Amendment,Urban Local Bodies,Municipalities,Ward Committees,12th Schedule,Urban Planning
landmark_case_1: Ahmedabad Municipal Corporation v. Nawab Khan Gulab Khan
lc1_holding_summary: Municipalities have constitutional responsibility for urban governance but State can define scope
landmark_case_2: Delhi Municipal Corporation v. Shibu Nath Brahmin
lc2_holding_summary: Functional autonomy of municipalities upheld within the framework of state law
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Right to Education — 86th Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: Inserted Article 21A (free and compulsory education for 6-14 years as Fundamental Right); amended Article 45 (DPSP for early childhood care); added Fundamental Duty under Article 51A(k) for parents to provide education to children
simplified_explanation: Made elementary education a Fundamental Right. Implemented through the Right to Education Act 2009 (RTE). The 25% reservation for disadvantaged children in private schools (Section 12(1)(c) of RTE) was upheld by the Supreme Court in 2012. Children below 6 are covered under the amended DPSP in Article 45.
keywords: 86th Amendment,Right to Education,RTE Act,21A,Free Education,Fundamental Duty
landmark_case_1: Society for Unaided Private Schools v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: 25% RTE reservation in private unaided schools is constitutionally valid
landmark_case_2: Ashok Kumar Thakur v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: RTE Act is constitutionally valid and implements Article 21A effectively
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Right to Information Act 2005
verbatim_text_excerpt: Every citizen shall have the right to information and this Act may be called the Right to Information Act, 2005. The object of this Act is to empower the citizens, promote transparency and accountability in the working of the Government
simplified_explanation: Citizens can seek information from public authorities within 30 days (48 hours for life/liberty matters). Public authorities must maintain records and appoint Public Information Officers. Central and State Information Commissions adjudicate appeals. Exempt: national security, intelligence, Cabinet discussions, personal information.
keywords: RTI,Transparency,Public Information Officer,Central Information Commission,Exemptions,30 Days
landmark_case_1: CBSE v. Aditya Bandopadhyay
lc1_holding_summary: RTI Act must be used to promote transparency; information that cannot be denied should not be denied
landmark_case_2: Girish Ramchandra Deshpande v. Central Information Commission
lc2_holding_summary: Personal information of public servants related to their public duties is disclosable under RTI
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Personnel, Public Grievances and Pensions
punishment_quantum: Penalty up to ₹25,000 (₹250/day up to maximum) against the PIO under Section 20; disciplinary action may also follow.
exceptions_and_limitations: Subject to exemptions in Sections 8, 9 and 24; life and liberty requests are accelerated under Section 7(1).

title: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 consolidates and amends the law relating to offences and matters connected therewith; it came into force on 1 July 2024.
simplified_explanation: The Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 is the operative penal code, replacing the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 1860 from 1 July 2024. Offence definitions, general exceptions, abetment, attempt, hurt, theft, robbery, sexual offences, and public order offences are now codified under the BNS.
keywords: BNS,Penal Code,Criminal Law,Offences,BNS Repeal,1 July 2024
landmark_case_1: K.M. Nanavati v. State of Maharashtra
lc1_holding_summary: Landmark murder case; distinguished culpable homicide from murder
landmark_case_2: S. Khushboo v. Kanniammal
lc2_holding_summary: Criminal law cannot be used to stifle free speech on moral issues
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Home Affairs
legal_classification: Section-specific; generally cognizable / non-bailable for core offences
punishment_quantum: Offence-specific; penalties range section-wise from fines to imprisonment, life imprisonment, and in rare cases death.

title: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 consolidates and amends the law relating to criminal procedure and came into force on 1 July 2024.
simplified_explanation: The Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 is the operative criminal procedure code, replacing the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS), 1973 from 1 July 2024. It governs police powers, arrest, bail, remand, investigation, cognizance, trial, and appeals.
keywords: BNSS,Criminal Procedure,Arrest,Bail,Remand,BNSS Repeal,1 July 2024
landmark_case_1: Arnab Manoranjan Goswami v. State of Maharashtra
lc1_holding_summary: Liberty under Article 21 and bail provisions must be interpreted broadly
landmark_case_2: Satender Kumar Antil v. CBI
lc2_holding_summary: Default bail right is inviolable; courts must grant bail if charge sheet not filed in time
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Home Affairs
punishment_quantum: Procedural code; no standalone punishment quantum.

title: Seventh Schedule — Legislative Lists
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Seventh Schedule contains three Lists: Union List (List I - 98 subjects), State List (List II - 59 subjects), and Concurrent List (List III - 52 subjects) dividing legislative subjects between Parliament and State Legislatures
simplified_explanation: The Union List has subjects of national importance (defence, banking, foreign affairs, atomic energy). The State List has local subjects (police, agriculture, land). The Concurrent List has subjects where both can legislate but Centre prevails in case of conflict (under Article 254). Residuary power with Parliament.
keywords: Union List,State List,Concurrent List,Legislative Subjects,Residuary Power,Article 254
landmark_case_1: M. Karunanidhi v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Repugnancy between Centre and State laws resolved by Article 254; Presidential assent to State law protects it
landmark_case_2: State of Rajasthan v. G. Chawla
lc2_holding_summary: Pith and substance doctrine used to determine which list a law falls in
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Defence

title: 101st Constitutional Amendment Act 2016 — GST
verbatim_text_excerpt: Parliament may, by law, on the recommendation of the Goods and Services Tax Council, provide for compensation to the States for loss of revenue arising on account of implementation of the goods and services tax
simplified_explanation: The 101st Amendment inserted Articles 246A, 269A, and 279A to implement GST. Article 246A gives both Parliament and State legislatures concurrent power to tax goods and services. The GST Council (Article 279A) with Union and State finance ministers determines GST rates. Abolished CST, Service Tax, VAT, etc.
keywords: GST,101st Amendment,GST Council,Indirect Tax,One Nation One Tax,Article 246A
landmark_case_1: Union of India v. Mohit Mineral Pvt Ltd
lc1_holding_summary: GST Council recommendations are not binding; Centre and States retain legislative autonomy under Article 246A
landmark_case_2: Unicorn Industries v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: GST structure does not violate fiscal federalism
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

title: Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act 2006
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Act recognises and vests the forest rights and occupation in forest land in forest dwelling Scheduled Tribes and other traditional forest dwellers who have been residing in such forests for generations but whose rights could not be recorded
simplified_explanation: Recognizes pre-existing rights of STs and other traditional forest dwellers over forest land they have been using. Rights include right to live, cultivate, use minor forest produce, protect community forest resources. Gram Sabha is the key authority for recognizing rights. Overcomes colonial-era forest laws.
keywords: Forest Rights,Tribal Rights,Gram Sabha,Minor Forest Produce,Traditional Forest Dwellers
landmark_case_1: Orissa Mining Corporation v. Ministry of Environment & Forest
lc1_holding_summary: Mining in scheduled areas requires Gram Sabha consent under Forest Rights Act
landmark_case_2: Wildlife First & Ors v. Ministry of Forest & Climate Change
lc2_holding_summary: SC stayed evictions of forest dwellers pending rejection of forest rights claims by states
category: Statutory Laws - Environment and Tribal Rights
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

title: Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act 2012
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Act was enacted to protect children from offences of sexual assault, sexual harassment and pornography and provide for establishment of Special Courts for trial of such offences
simplified_explanation: POCSO protects children (below 18) from sexual offences. Defines aggravated sexual assault, sexual harassment, and use of children in pornography. Mandates Special Courts for speedy trial. Creates obligation to report child sexual abuse. Child-friendly procedures for victim testimony. Strict punishments.
keywords: POCSO,Child Sexual Abuse,Special Court,Mandatory Reporting,Child Victim,Child Protection
landmark_case_1: Alakh Alok Srivastava v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Supreme Court suo motu directed expeditious POCSO trials and monitoring
landmark_case_2: State of Maharashtra v. Bhikhari Paswan
lc2_holding_summary: POCSO must be interpreted broadly to protect all children regardless of gender
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry
legal_classification: Section-specific; generally cognizable / non-bailable for core offences

title: Environment (Protection) Act 1986
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Central Government shall have the power to take all such measures as it deems necessary or expedient for the purpose of protecting and improving the quality of the environment and preventing, controlling and abating environmental pollution
simplified_explanation: Umbrella legislation for environmental protection enacted after Bhopal Gas Tragedy. Empowers the Centre to set environmental standards, conduct inspections, and close polluting industries. EIA (Environmental Impact Assessment) notifications are issued under this Act. Also source of Polluter Pays and Precautionary Principles.
keywords: Environment Protection,Pollution Control,EIA,Bhopal,Polluter Pays,Precautionary Principle
landmark_case_1: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Absolute Liability principle — industries engaged in hazardous activities absolutely liable for harm caused
landmark_case_2: Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Precautionary Principle and Polluter Pays Principle are part of Indian environmental law
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

title: Panchayats (Extension to Scheduled Areas) Act 1996
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Act extends the provisions of Part IX of the Constitution relating to Panchayats to the Scheduled Areas with certain modifications and exceptions
simplified_explanation: PESA extends Panchayati Raj to Fifth Schedule (tribal) areas with special protections for tribal autonomy. Gram Sabha has primacy in tribal areas — must be consulted for land acquisition, minor forest produce management, and social sector schemes. Intended to prevent exploitation and protect tribal customs.
keywords: PESA,Scheduled Areas,Fifth Schedule,Tribal,Gram Sabha,Traditional Rights,Land Acquisition
landmark_case_1: Samantha v. State of AP
lc1_holding_summary: Tribal land cannot be leased to non-tribal private companies even for industry; government is trustee of tribal land
landmark_case_2: Nandini Sundar v. State of Chhattisgarh
lc2_holding_summary: State cannot use tribal vigilantes (Salwa Judum); PESA and Fifth Schedule must be respected
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Panchayati Raj
exceptions_and_limitations: Operates within Fifth Schedule areas and subject to tribal self-governance safeguards through Gram Sabha consent/consultation.
cross_references: Fifth Schedule; Articles 243A-243O; Gram Sabha powers

title: Representation of the People Act 1951
verbatim_text_excerpt: The qualifications and disqualifications for membership of Parliament and State Legislatures and the corrupt practices and other offences at or in connection with such elections shall be as provided by or under any law made by Parliament
simplified_explanation: Governs elections, electoral rolls, qualifications/disqualifications of candidates, and election disputes. Key sections: Section 8 (disqualification on conviction), Section 123 (corrupt practices), Section 33A (disclosure of criminal antecedents). Under Article 102/191, disqualification includes office of profit, unsound mind, citizenship, etc.
keywords: RPA 1951,Election Law,Corrupt Practices,Disqualification,Electoral Roll,Dispute Resolution
landmark_case_1: Association for Democratic Reforms v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Candidates must disclose criminal antecedents, assets, and educational qualifications
landmark_case_2: Public Interest Foundation v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Parliament must consider passing law to permanently bar convicted persons from elections
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice
exceptions_and_limitations: Subject to election rules, disqualification provisions, and constitutional election supervision under Article 324.
cross_references: Article 324; Conduct of Election Rules 1961; Representation of the People Act 1950

title: Tenth Schedule — Anti-Defection Law
verbatim_text_excerpt: A member of a House belonging to any political party shall be disqualified for being a member of the House if he has voluntarily given up his membership of such political party
simplified_explanation: Added by 52nd Amendment 1985 to prevent political defections. A legislator is disqualified if they voluntarily give up party membership or vote against party whip without permission. Merger of 2/3 members (post-91st Amendment 2003) into another party is not a defection. Speaker/Chairman decides disqualification but this is subject to judicial review.
keywords: Anti-Defection,10th Schedule,Whip,Voluntary Giving Up,Merger,Speaker,Disqualification
landmark_case_1: Kihoto Hollohan v. Zachillhu
lc1_holding_summary: 10th Schedule is constitutional; Speaker's decision is subject to judicial review
landmark_case_2: Nabam Rebia v. Deputy Speaker Arunachal Pradesh
lc2_holding_summary: Speaker cannot decide disqualification petitions while facing a no-confidence motion
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: National Green Tribunal Act 2010
verbatim_text_excerpt: There shall be established, by the Central Government, by notification, a tribunal to be known as the National Green Tribunal for the effective and expeditious disposal of cases relating to environmental protection and conservation of forests and other natural resources
simplified_explanation: NGT is a specialized quasi-judicial body for environmental disputes. Has original jurisdiction for substantial questions relating to environment. Can award compensation and give relief in cases of environmental damage. Applies the Precautionary and Polluter Pays Principles. Benches at major cities.
keywords: NGT,National Green Tribunal,Environmental Disputes,Compensation,Precautionary Principle,Specialized Tribunal
landmark_case_1: Braj Foundation v. State of UP
lc1_holding_summary: NGT has jurisdiction over all environmental matters; cannot be ousted by state legislation
landmark_case_2: M.K. Ranjitsinh v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: NGT should be strengthened for effective environmental justice; SC expanded scope of climate rights
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
punishment_quantum: No standalone punishment quantum at the tribunal level; relief and compensation are adjudicatory, not penal.
cross_references: Schedule I-III; Environment Protection Act 1986; Article 21 environmental jurisprudence

title: Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code 2016
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Code provides for reorganisation and insolvency resolution of corporate persons, partnership firms and individuals in a time bound manner for maximisation of value of assets of such persons
simplified_explanation: IBC provides for corporate insolvency resolution within 180+90 days through Committee of Creditors. Liquidation if resolution fails. National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) adjudicates corporate insolvency. Debt Recovery Tribunal (DRT) for individuals. Resolution applicant submits plan approved by 66% of Committee of Creditors.
keywords: IBC,Insolvency,Bankruptcy,NCLT,CIRP,Committee of Creditors,Resolution Plan
landmark_case_1: Swiss Ribbons Pvt Ltd v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: IBC's classification of financial and operational creditors is constitutionally valid
landmark_case_2: Committee of Creditors of Essar Steel v. Satish Kumar Gupta
lc2_holding_summary: Resolution plan must equitably distribute value; NCLT cannot rewrite commercial decision of Committee of Creditors
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Corporate Affairs

title: Disaster Management Act 2005
verbatim_text_excerpt: For the purpose of this Act, 'disaster' means a catastrophe, mishap, calamity or grave occurrence in any area, arising from natural or man-made causes, or by accident or negligence which results in substantial loss of life or human suffering or damage to, and destruction of, property
simplified_explanation: Established the National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) chaired by the Prime Minister. Created the institutional framework for disaster preparedness, response, and mitigation. State Disaster Management Authorities and District Disaster Management Authorities form the three-tier structure. NDRF is the dedicated disaster response force.
keywords: NDMA,Disaster Management,DM Act,NDRF,State SDMA,National Disaster
landmark_case_1: Re: Cognizance for Extension of Limitation (COVID-19)
lc1_holding_summary: NDMA Act invoked during COVID-19 to extend limitation periods; force majeure provisions recognized
landmark_case_2: Gaurav Kumar Bansal v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: NDMA must use its powers to ensure equitable access to oxygen and COVID-19 relief
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Home Affairs
exceptions_and_limitations: Subject to constitutional division of powers, disaster-specific notifications, and delegated directions.
cross_references: National Disaster Management Authority; National Disaster Response Fund; Article 355

title: Basic Structure Doctrine
verbatim_text_excerpt: Parliament's amending power under Article 368, though wide, cannot be used to damage or destroy the basic structure or essential features of the Constitution. These basic features include supremacy of the Constitution, republican and democratic form of government, secular character, separation of powers, and federal character.
simplified_explanation: The Basic Structure Doctrine, evolved in Kesavananda Bharati (1973), limits Parliament's power to amend the Constitution. Neither Parliament nor State legislatures can abridge, alter or destroy the basic structure. It is a judge-made doctrine not found in the text of the Constitution. It protects democracy from constitutional authoritarianism.
keywords: Basic Structure,Kesavananda Bharati,Constitutional Amendment,Judicial Review,Democracy,Federalism,Secularism
landmark_case_1: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
lc1_holding_summary: 13-judge bench held that basic structure of Constitution is inviolable even by constitutional amendment
landmark_case_2: Minerva Mills v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Harmony between Fundamental Rights and DPSPs is part of basic structure; Articles 14 and 19 form its core
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Habeas Corpus — You may have the body
verbatim_text_excerpt: A writ of habeas corpus is a court order directing an authority holding a person in custody to produce the person before the court so the court can determine whether the detention is lawful or not. It is the primary safeguard against illegal detention and imprisonment.
simplified_explanation: 'Habeas Corpus' means 'you shall have the body'. It is the most important writ to protect personal liberty. Petition can be filed by detainee or anyone on their behalf. Courts will scrutinize whether arrest was under valid authority, procedure followed, and if continued detention is justified. Cannot be suspended even during emergencies (post-44th Amendment).
keywords: Habeas Corpus,Illegal Detention,Personal Liberty,Article 21,Writ Jurisdiction
landmark_case_1: ADM Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla
lc1_holding_summary: (Wrongly decided) Habeas Corpus can be suspended during emergency — overruled by KS Puttaswamy
landmark_case_2: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal
lc2_holding_summary: Habeas Corpus includes protection against custodial torture; detailed arrest guidelines issued
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Mandamus — We Command
verbatim_text_excerpt: A writ of mandamus is a court order commanding a public authority, tribunal, corporation, or inferior court to perform a public or statutory duty it has refused or failed to perform, or to refrain from acting unlawfully.
simplified_explanation: 'Mandamus' means 'we command'. Issued against public bodies that fail to perform a mandatory public duty. Cannot be issued against: private individuals, President or Governor for discretionary acts, or where alternative remedy exists. The body must have a legal duty and petitioner a legal right to its performance.
keywords: Mandamus,Public Duty,Statutory Duty,Public Authority,Performance
landmark_case_1: Rai Shivendra Bahadur v. Board of Governors NIT Jamshedpur
lc1_holding_summary: Mandamus lies to compel performance of public duty by educational institutions
landmark_case_2: S.P. Gupta v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Mandamus can issue to direct appointment of judges as per collegium recommendations
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Separation of Powers in Indian Constitution
verbatim_text_excerpt: India follows a modified separation of powers — the three organs (Legislature, Executive, Judiciary) have defined roles, and each has checks and balances over the other. Courts can strike down executive actions and legislation. Parliament can remove judges and amend the Constitution. The Executive controls Parliament's agenda.
simplified_explanation: India does not have strict separation of powers as in the US. The President/Governor executes on ministerial advice; Parliament passes laws; Courts interpret. Checks and balances are provided through judicial review, Parliamentary accountability, President's assent, etc. Basic structure doctrine is the most powerful check on legislative supremacy.
keywords: Separation of Powers,Checks and Balances,Judicial Review,Legislature,Executive,Judiciary
landmark_case_1: Ram Jawaya Kapur v. State of Punjab
lc1_holding_summary: Indian Constitution does not adopt strict separation of powers; each organ has some overlap
landmark_case_2: I.C. Golak Nath v. State of Punjab
lc2_holding_summary: Judicial power to invalidate constitutional amendments is the ultimate check on parliamentary power
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Quasi-Federal Nature of Indian Constitution
verbatim_text_excerpt: India has a federal structure with a strong Centre. Federal features: Written Constitution, rigid amendment procedure, independent judiciary, two houses in Parliament, Seventh Schedule distribution. Unitary features: Single Constitution, single citizenship, emergency provisions, All India Services, Centre's residuary powers, power to alter state boundaries.
simplified_explanation: India's federalism is called 'quasi-federal' or 'federal with strong unitary features' (K.C. Wheare). During normal times it works as a federation; during emergencies it becomes more unitary. The holding in SR Bommai upheld federalism as a basic feature of the Constitution. Cooperative federalism is now emphasized through GST Council, NITI Aayog, etc.
keywords: Federalism,Quasi-Federal,Unitary Features,Centre-State,Cooperative Federalism,SR Bommai
landmark_case_1: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Federalism is a basic feature of the Constitution; President's Rule must not be imposed to destabilize democratically elected governments
landmark_case_2: State of West Bengal v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Indian federation is not a compact between States; States cannot challenge federal laws on sovereignty grounds
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: PMLA 2002
verbatim_text_excerpt: Whosoever directly or indirectly attempts to indulge or knowingly assists or knowingly is a party or is actually involved in any process or activity connected proceeds of crime and projecting it as untainted property shall be guilty of offence of money laundering
simplified_explanation: PMLA criminalizes money laundering — conversion of 'proceeds of crime' into clean assets. Enforcement Directorate investigates. Conviction of scheduled offence is necessary. ED can attach properties provisionally. Stringent bail conditions: accused must prove they are not guilty and won't commit offence again (twin test). Adjudicating Authority and Appellate Tribunal for appeals.
keywords: PMLA,Money Laundering,Enforcement Directorate,Attachment,Proceeds of Crime,Twin Test Bail
landmark_case_1: Vijay Madanlal Choudhary v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: PMLA's wide powers of arrest, search, seizure and bail conditions are constitutionally valid
landmark_case_2: Y. Balaji v. Karthik Desari
lc2_holding_summary: PMLA must be applied strictly within its scope; cannot be used to harass
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

title: Proportionality in Constitutional Law
verbatim_text_excerpt: Proportionality requires that the means chosen by the State must be suitable to achieve the legitimate aim, necessary (least restrictive alternative available), and proportionate in balance (benefits must not be outweighed by harms to rights). It is the global standard for rights adjudication.
simplified_explanation: Proportionality replaces the older 'reasonableness' test. Courts now ask: Is the state's aim legitimate? Is the measure suitable? Is it necessary (is there a less restrictive alternative)? Is it proportionate (do the benefits outweigh the rights violation)? Applied in Article 19, 21, and Article 14 challenges.
keywords: Proportionality,Judicial Review,Least Restrictive,Balancing Test,Rights Adjudication
landmark_case_1: Modern Dental College v. State of MP
lc1_holding_summary: Four-stage proportionality test adopted from comparative constitutional law
landmark_case_2: KS Puttaswamy v. Union of India (Aadhaar)
lc2_holding_summary: Proportionality test applied rigorously to data protection and privacy restrictions
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Abrogation of Article 370 and Reorganisation of J&K
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (Application to Jammu and Kashmir) Order 2019 operationalised Article 370(3) to abrogate all special provisions for Jammu & Kashmir. The J&K Reorganisation Act 2019 bifurcated the State into two Union Territories — J&K (with legislature) and Ladakh (without legislature)
simplified_explanation: Article 370 gave special status to J&K allowing it to have its own Constitution and limiting Parliament's jurisdiction.
