In August 2019, the President issued an order extending all provisions of the Indian Constitution to J&K and effectively abrogating Article 370. The State was simultaneously bifurcated into two UTs under Article 3.
keywords: Article 370,Special Status,Jammu Kashmir,Abrogation,Reorganisation,Union Territory,Ladakh
landmark_case_1: In Re Article 370 of the Constitution
lc1_holding_summary: SC upheld abrogation of Article 370; J&K did not have internal sovereignty; statehood to be restored at appropriate time
landmark_case_2: Prem Nath Kaul v. State of J&K
lc2_holding_summary: Article 370 was a transitional provision; constituent assembly of J&K had limited role
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Transgender Persons (Protection of Rights) Act 2019
verbatim_text_excerpt: A transgender person shall have a right to be recognised as such and every transgender person who has been recognised shall have a right to self-perceived gender identity
simplified_explanation: India's first comprehensive law protecting transgender persons. Prohibits discrimination in education, employment, healthcare. Recognises right to self-identified gender. Mandates certificate of identity. However, criticized for mandatory medical certification for 'male' or 'female' recognition and inadequate penal provisions.
keywords: Transgender,Gender Identity,Self-Identification,LGBTQ,Discrimination Protection
landmark_case_1: National Legal Services Authority v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Transgender persons have right to self-identify their gender; are third gender with all constitutional rights
landmark_case_2: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Section 377 BNS decriminalized; sexual orientation and gender identity protected under Articles 14, 15, 19, 21
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023
verbatim_text_excerpt: A Data Principal shall have a right to obtain from a Data Fiduciary a summary of the personal data being processed; to know the identities of all other Data Fiduciaries and Data Processors with whom the personal data has been shared
simplified_explanation: India's first comprehensive data protection law implementing the right to privacy under Article 21. Requires consent for processing personal data. Data Principals (individuals) have rights to access, correction, erasure, and grievance redressal. Data Protection Board adjudicates complaints. Significant financial penalties for breaches.
keywords: DPDP Act,Data Protection,Personal Data,Consent,Data Principal,Data Fiduciary,Privacy
landmark_case_1: KS Puttaswamy v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Right to Privacy is a Fundamental Right; data protection legislation is constitutionally mandated
landmark_case_2: Justice Srikrishna Committee Report
lc2_holding_summary: Foundational report that recommended data protection framework eventually enacted as DPDP 2023
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology
punishment_quantum: Schedule-linked monetary penalties; contraventions can attract penalties up to ₹250 crore depending on the breach.
exceptions_and_limitations: Contains statutory exemptions and legitimate-use grounds; balance is subject to rules and government notifications.
cross_references: Article 21; IT Act 2000; data fiduciary/digital governance framework

title: Model Code of Conduct — Constitutional Basis
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Model Code of Conduct (MCC) is a set of guidelines issued by the Election Commission of India for the conduct of political parties and candidates during elections. It comes into force from the date of announcement of election schedule.
simplified_explanation: The MCC has no statutory basis but derives its force from Article 324 (Election Commission's superintendence, direction, and control of elections). It prohibits use of government machinery and resources for election campaigning, communal appeals, and bribery. The Election Commission enforces it through moral and constitutional authority. Violators face derecognition, candidate disqualification.
keywords: Model Code of Conduct,Election Commission,Article 324,Free and Fair Elections,Government Machinery
landmark_case_1: T.N. Seshan v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Election Commission has wide powers under Article 324 to ensure free and fair elections including enforcement of MCC
landmark_case_2: Union of India v. Association for Democratic Reforms
lc2_holding_summary: Election Commission can direct disclosure of criminal and financial background of candidates
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice

title: Abolition of Titles
verbatim_text_excerpt: No title, not being a military or academic distinction, shall be conferred by the State. No citizen of India shall accept any title from any foreign State
simplified_explanation: The State cannot confer titles like Sir, Rai Bahadur, or Khan Bahadur. Only military (Param Vir Chakra, Arjun Award) and academic distinctions are allowed. Bharat Ratna, Padma awards were challenged but SC held they are not 'titles' as they carry no hereditary privilege or legal rights.
keywords: Abolition of Titles,Bharat Ratna,Padma Awards,Foreign Titles,Equality
landmark_case_1: Balaji Raghavan v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Bharat Ratna and Padma awards are not titles under Article 18; they do not confer any legal right or privilege
landmark_case_2: Revathi v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: National honours like Padma Vibhushan are forms of recognition, not titles prohibited by Article 18
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Freedom of Speech and Expression
verbatim_text_excerpt: All citizens shall have the right to freedom of speech and expression
simplified_explanation: Freedom of speech and expression includes press freedom, right to broadcast, commercial speech, right to remain silent, right to information, right to dissent, artistic expression, and academic freedom. Reasonable restrictions under Article 19(2) include sovereignty, integrity, security of state, public order, decency, morality, contempt of court, defamation, and incitement.
keywords: Free Speech,Press Freedom,Right to Information,Commercial Speech,Right to Silence,19(2) Restrictions
landmark_case_1: Shreya Singhal v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Section 66A IT Act struck down; online speech has same constitutional protection as offline speech
landmark_case_2: Indian Express Newspapers v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Freedom of press is integral to Article 19(1)(a); import duties on newsprint can restrict press freedom
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry
exceptions_and_limitations: Subject to reasonable restrictions in Article 19(2): sovereignty and integrity, security of State, friendly relations, public order, decency, morality, contempt of court, defamation, and incitement to an offence.
cross_references: Article 19(2); RTI Act 2005; IT Act 2000; Shreya Singhal v. Union of India

title: Freedom of Assembly
verbatim_text_excerpt: All citizens shall have the right to assemble peaceably and without arms
simplified_explanation: Citizens have the right to hold public meetings and demonstrations. Must be peaceful and unarmed. State can impose reasonable restrictions under Article 19(3) in interests of sovereignty, integrity, or public order. Police cannot pre-emptively ban assemblies without evidence of likely breach of peace.
keywords: Assembly,Peaceful Assembly,Demonstration,Public Meeting,Section 163 (formerly Section 144) BNSS
landmark_case_1: Babulal Parate v. State of Bombay
lc1_holding_summary: Restrictions under Section 163 (formerly Section 144) BNSS on assembly must satisfy the test of reasonable restriction under Article 19(3)
landmark_case_2: Ramlila Maidan Incident v. Home Secretary Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Lathi-charging peaceful assembly at night without sufficient warning violates Articles 19 and 21
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Home Affairs

title: Freedom of Association
verbatim_text_excerpt: All citizens shall have the right to form associations or unions or co-operative societies
simplified_explanation: Citizens can form trade unions, political parties, companies, clubs, religious bodies. This right includes the right NOT to join an association. But it does not include a right to recognition of the association. Compulsory trade union membership violates this right.
keywords: Association,Trade Union,Political Party,Right Not to Join,Co-operative Society
landmark_case_1: All India Bank Employees Association v. National Industrial Tribunal
lc1_holding_summary: Right to form trade union does not include right to strike or right to recognition
landmark_case_2: Damayanti Naranga v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Compulsory merger of cooperative societies violates Article 19(1)(c)
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Freedom of Movement and Residence
verbatim_text_excerpt: All citizens shall have the right to move freely throughout the territory of India and to reside and settle in any part of the territory of India
simplified_explanation: Citizens can travel anywhere in India and settle anywhere. Reasonable restrictions in the interests of general public or protection of Scheduled Tribes are permitted. This includes protection of tribal areas from non-tribal influx. Inner Line Permit system for Northeast states is a restriction under this clause.
keywords: Freedom of Movement,Residence,Settlement,Inner Line Permit,Scheduled Tribes,Restrictions
landmark_case_1: State of UP v. Kaushaliya
lc1_holding_summary: Externment orders restricting movement must satisfy test of reasonable restriction for public interest
landmark_case_2: Ebrahim Vazir Mavat v. State of Bombay
lc2_holding_summary: Indian citizens cannot be prevented from returning to India; their right of entry is absolute
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Freedom of Profession and Trade
verbatim_text_excerpt: All citizens shall have the right to practice any profession, or to carry on any occupation, trade or business
simplified_explanation: Citizens can practice any profession or run any business. Reasonable restrictions under Article 19(6) include professional qualification requirements, State monopoly of certain trades. The State can by law carry on any trade or business. Licensing requirements and professional regulations are valid restrictions.
keywords: Profession,Trade,Business,Occupation,Licensing,State Monopoly,Reasonable Restriction
landmark_case_1: Sodan Singh v. New Delhi Municipal Committee
lc1_holding_summary: Hawkers have right to carry on trade on public streets subject to reasonable regulation
landmark_case_2: Chintaman Rao v. State of MP
lc2_holding_summary: Blanket ban on bidi manufacturing in certain seasons was excessive and not a reasonable restriction
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Protection in respect of conviction for offences
verbatim_text_excerpt: No person shall be convicted of any offence except for violation of a law in force at the time of the commission of the act charged as an offence, nor be subjected to a penalty greater than that which might have been inflicted under the law in force at the time of the commission of the offence
simplified_explanation: Article 20 provides three protections: (1) No ex post facto law — cannot be convicted for an act that was not an offence when committed; (2) No double jeopardy — cannot be prosecuted twice for the same offence; (3) No self-incrimination — cannot be compelled to be a witness against oneself.
keywords: Ex Post Facto Law,Double Jeopardy,Self Incrimination,Criminal Procedure,Protection against Conviction
landmark_case_1: M.P. Sharma v. Satish Chandra
lc1_holding_summary: Right against self-incrimination under Article 20(3) extends to documents but not physical evidence
landmark_case_2: Maqbool Hussain v. State of Bombay
lc2_holding_summary: Customs proceedings and criminal prosecution for same act are not double jeopardy as they are different proceedings
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Freedom from attending religious instruction
verbatim_text_excerpt: No religious instruction shall be provided in any educational institution wholly maintained out of State funds
simplified_explanation: State-funded schools cannot provide religious instruction. Schools maintained by religious denominations even if aided by State can provide religious instruction. Attendance at religious instruction in aided schools must be voluntary. This protects the secular character of State education.
keywords: Secular Education,Religious Instruction,State Funded Schools,Voluntary Attendance,Secularism
landmark_case_1: DAV College v. State of Punjab
lc1_holding_summary: Government-aided colleges cannot be compelled to teach a particular religion's doctrines
landmark_case_2: Aruna Roy v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Teaching about religions in a comparative, secular manner in schools does not violate Article 28
category: Fundamental Rights - Cultural and Educational Rights
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Saving of Laws providing for acquisition of estates
verbatim_text_excerpt: Notwithstanding anything contained in Article 13, no law providing for the acquisition by the State of any estate or of any rights therein or the extinguishment or modification of any such rights shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with, or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Article 14 or Article 19
simplified_explanation: Saves land reform laws from challenge under Articles 14 and 19. Was inserted by 1st Amendment to protect zamindari abolition laws. Cannot be used to save laws violating Article 21. Along with Article 31B and Ninth Schedule, forms a comprehensive shield for agrarian reform legislation.
keywords: Land Reform,Estate Acquisition,Zamindari Abolition,First Amendment,Article 31B,Ninth Schedule
landmark_case_1: State of Bihar v. Kameshwar Singh
lc1_holding_summary: Article 31A protects genuine land reform; arbitrary acquisition for non-public purpose still challengeable
landmark_case_2: Waman Rao v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Laws added to Ninth Schedule before 24 April 1973 are fully immune from challenge
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Writ Jurisdiction — High Courts (Article 226)
verbatim_text_excerpt: Every High Court shall have power, throughout the territories in relation to which it exercises jurisdiction, to issue to any person or authority including in appropriate cases any Government, within those territories directions, orders or writs including writs in the nature of habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, quo warranto and certiorari
simplified_explanation: Article 226 gives High Courts wider writ jurisdiction than Supreme Court's Article 32. High Courts can issue writs for both Fundamental Rights violations AND legal rights. Article 32 is for Fundamental Rights only. When an alternative remedy exists, High Courts may decline to exercise writ jurisdiction (not an absolute bar).
keywords: Article 226,High Court,Writ Jurisdiction,Alternative Remedy,Habeas Corpus,Certiorari
landmark_case_1: Dwarkanath v. Income Tax Officer
lc1_holding_summary: Article 226 is wider than Article 32; it enables enforcement of all legal rights, not just Fundamental Rights
landmark_case_2: Whirlpool Corporation v. Registrar of Trade Marks
lc2_holding_summary: Existence of alternative remedy is not an absolute bar to High Court writ jurisdiction under Article 226
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: State to secure a social order for promotion of welfare of people
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall strive to promote the welfare of the people by securing and protecting as effectively as it may a social order in which justice — social, economic and political — shall inform all the institutions of the national life
simplified_explanation: The first and foundational DPSP. Directs the State to create a welfare state where social, economic, and political justice is available to all. Reduces inequalities in income, status, and opportunities. This is the socialist DPSP closest to the vision of the Preamble.
keywords: Welfare State,Social Justice,Economic Justice,Political Justice,DPSP,Inequality Reduction
landmark_case_1: Minerva Mills v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Harmony between Fundamental Rights and DPSPs is basic structure; both must be advanced together
landmark_case_2: Unni Krishnan v. State of AP
lc2_holding_summary: Right to education up to age 14 was read as Fundamental Right from Articles 21 and 45 before 86th Amendment
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Certain principles of policy — Equal Pay, Child Protection
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall, in particular, direct its policy towards securing: (a) that citizens have right to adequate means of livelihood; (b) that ownership of material resources subserves common good; (c) prevention of concentration of wealth; (d) equal pay for equal work; (e) protection of workers' health; (f) protection of children from exploitation
simplified_explanation: Article 39 is the most comprehensive economic DPSP. Clauses (b) and (c) were the basis of nationalisation and land reforms. Clause (d) — equal pay for equal work — has been enforced by courts in service matters. Clauses (e) and (f) protect workers and children from abuse.
keywords: Equal Pay,Livelihood,Material Resources,Child Protection,Concentration of Wealth,DPSP
landmark_case_1: Randhir Singh v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Equal pay for equal work under Article 39(d) is a constitutional goal enforceable in service law
landmark_case_2: Bandhua Mukti Morcha v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Child labour violates Article 39(e) and (f); State has affirmative duty to eliminate it
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Equal Justice and Free Legal Aid
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall secure that the operation of the legal system promotes justice, on a basis of equal opportunity, and shall, in particular, provide free legal aid, by suitable legislation or schemes or in any other way, to ensure that opportunities for securing justice are not denied to any citizen by reason of economic or other disabilities
simplified_explanation: Inserted by 42nd Amendment 1976. Mandates free legal aid to ensure access to justice is not denied due to poverty. National Legal Services Authority Act 1987 implements this. Right to free legal aid has been read as part of Article 21 by the Supreme Court. Lok Adalats and PLVs (Paralegal Volunteers) further this goal.
keywords: Free Legal Aid,Equal Justice,NALSA,Legal Services,42nd Amendment,Lok Adalat
landmark_case_1: Hussainara Khatoon v. State of Bihar
lc1_holding_summary: Right to free legal aid is an integral part of fair procedure under Article 21; State must provide it
landmark_case_2: M.H. Hoskot v. State of Maharashtra
lc2_holding_summary: Right to legal aid is a Fundamental Right under Article 21 in criminal cases involving imprisonment
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Organisation of Village Panchayats
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall take steps to organise village panchayats and endow them with such powers and authority as may be necessary to enable them to function as units of self-government
simplified_explanation: The Gandhian DPSP that inspired the 73rd Amendment 1992. Gram Swaraj — Gandhian vision of village republics — was the basis. Before 1992 this was an unenforceable directive. Post-73rd Amendment, panchayati raj became constitutionally mandated. This directive shaped India's decentralization movement.
keywords: Village Panchayat,Gram Swaraj,Gandhian DPSP,Decentralization,73rd Amendment,Local Self Government
landmark_case_1: State of Rajasthan v. Vidhyawati
lc1_holding_summary: DPSPs cannot be enforced against the State but guide legislative policy
landmark_case_2: Asif Hameed v. State of J&K
lc2_holding_summary: DPSPs are fundamental in governance and courts must ensure legislation aligns with them
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Right to work, to education and to public assistance
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall, within the limits of its economic capacity and development, make effective provision for securing the right to work, to education, and to public assistance in cases of unemployment, old age, sickness and disablement, and in other cases of undeserved want
simplified_explanation: Directs the State to provide employment, education, and social security. The phrase 'within the limits of its economic capacity' acknowledges resource constraints. MGNREGA 2005 (right to 100 days of work) implements the right to work. National Social Assistance Programme implements right to assistance for elderly, disabled, and destitute.
keywords: Right to Work,MGNREGA,Social Security,Education,Unemployment,Old Age,Disability
landmark_case_1: Olga Tellis v. Bombay Municipal Corporation
lc1_holding_summary: Right to livelihood is part of right to life under Article 21; pavement dwellers cannot be evicted without rehabilitation
landmark_case_2: People's Union for Civil Liberties v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Right to food is part of Article 21; SC enforced DPSP through Articles 21 in mid-day meal scheme order
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Provision for just and humane conditions of work
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall make provision for securing just and humane conditions of work and for maternity relief
simplified_explanation: Directs the State to ensure fair working conditions and maternity benefits. Implemented through Maternity Benefit Act 1961 (amended 2017 to extend paid leave to 26 weeks), Factories Act 1948, and various labour codes. Maternity leave for surrogates and adoptive mothers was recognized based on this directive.
keywords: Maternity Benefit,Working Conditions,Labour Law,Maternity Leave,DPSP
landmark_case_1: Municipal Corporation of Delhi v. Female Workers
lc1_holding_summary: Female daily wage workers entitled to maternity benefits; Article 42 must be implemented broadly
landmark_case_2: Kavita Yadav v. Secretary Ministry of Health
lc2_holding_summary: Maternity benefits cannot be denied to contract workers; Article 42 mandates protection for all workers
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Living wage for workers
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall endeavour to secure, by suitable legislation or economic organisation or in any other way, to all workers, agricultural, industrial or otherwise, a living wage, conditions of work ensuring a decent standard of life and full enjoyment of leisure and social and cultural opportunities
simplified_explanation: Directs the State to ensure workers get not just minimum wages but a 'living wage' — enough for a dignified life with leisure and cultural participation. The Code on Wages 2019 consolidates four labour laws and seeks to universalize minimum wages. Courts have held that wages below minimum wage is unconstitutional.
keywords: Living Wage,Minimum Wage,Workers Rights,Decent Standard of Life,Labour Law,Code on Wages
landmark_case_1: Workmen of M/s Hindustan Tin Works v. Employers
lc1_holding_summary: Living wage principle must guide wage fixation; bare subsistence wages are inadequate
landmark_case_2: Pay Commission Reference
lc2_holding_summary: Government salaries must be fixed with reference to living wage and Article 43 principles
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Participation of workers in management
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall take steps, by suitable legislation or in any other way, to secure the participation of workers in the management of undertakings, establishments or other organisations engaged in any industry
simplified_explanation: Inserted by 42nd Amendment 1976. Directs the State to give workers a say in the management of industries. This is the constitutional basis for industrial democracy. The Companies Act 2013 has provisions for worker representation but full implementation remains limited. Inspired by European models of co-determination.
keywords: Workers Participation,Management,Industrial Democracy,42nd Amendment,Co-determination
landmark_case_1: Hindustan Antibiotics Ltd v. Workmen
lc1_holding_summary: Workers' participation in management strengthens industrial relations; courts encouraged such schemes
landmark_case_2: Gujarat Steel Tubes Ltd v. Gujarat Steel Tubes Mazdoor Sabha
lc2_holding_summary: Industrial adjudication must balance management rights and workers participation principles
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Promotion of co-operative societies
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall endeavour to promote voluntary formation, autonomous functioning, democratic control and professional management of co-operative societies
simplified_explanation: Inserted by 97th Amendment 2011. Directs the State to promote autonomous, democratically managed co-operative societies. The 97th Amendment also inserted Article 19(1)(c) right to form co-operative societies and Part IX-B (provisions for Co-operatives). The Supreme Court partly struck down the 97th Amendment for not getting state ratification.
keywords: Co-operative Societies,97th Amendment,Autonomous Functioning,Democratic Control,Part IX-B
landmark_case_1: Union of India v. Rajendra N. Shah
lc1_holding_summary: Part IX-B of Constitution on co-operatives as inserted by 97th Amendment struck down partly as unconstitutional for want of state ratification
landmark_case_2: Gujarat Rajya Karmachari Sahakari Bank v. State of Gujarat
lc2_holding_summary: Co-operative societies have right to autonomous functioning under Article 43B
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Provision for early childhood care and education
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall endeavour to provide early childhood care and education for all children until they complete the age of six years
simplified_explanation: Pre-86th Amendment, Article 45 directed the State to provide free compulsory education up to age 14 within 10 years. Post-86th Amendment (2002), Article 45 was amended to cover only early childhood care for children below 6. Children 6-14 are now covered by the Fundamental Right under Article 21A.
keywords: Early Childhood Care,ECCE,Anganwadi,ICDS,86th Amendment,Article 21A
landmark_case_1: Unni Krishnan v. State of AP
lc1_holding_summary: Right to education up to age 14 is a Fundamental Right derived from Article 21 read with Article 45
landmark_case_2: Miss Mohini Jain v. State of Karnataka
lc2_holding_summary: Right to education is a Fundamental Right; capitation fees violate this right
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Promotion of educational and economic interests of SC, ST and weaker sections
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall promote with special care the educational and economic interests of the weaker sections of the people, and, in particular, of the Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes, and shall protect them from social injustice and all forms of exploitation
simplified_explanation: Directs the State to give special attention to the educational and economic development of SC/ST and weaker sections. This is the constitutional basis for the vast body of reservation law, scholarship schemes, pre-matric and post-matric scholarships, and special government schools for tribal children.
keywords: SC,ST,Weaker Sections,Educational Interests,Economic Interests,Social Justice,Reservations
landmark_case_1: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Article 46 read with Articles 15(4) and 16(4) permits reservations for socially and educationally backward classes
landmark_case_2: Valsamma Paul v. Cochin University
lc2_holding_summary: Reservation benefits under Article 46 must be confined to the genuinely backward; not those who have advanced
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Duty of State to raise level of nutrition and standard of living
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall regard the raising of the level of nutrition and the standard of living of its people and the improvement of public health as among its primary duties and, in particular, the State shall endeavour to bring about prohibition of the consumption except for medicinal purposes of intoxicating drinks and drinks that are injurious to health
simplified_explanation: Directs the State to improve nutrition, public health, and work toward prohibition of alcohol. This is the constitutional basis for state prohibition laws (Gujarat, Bihar, AP). Also basis for National Food Security Act 2013 and PDS. Nutrition programmes like POSHAN Abhiyan trace their roots to this directive.
keywords: Nutrition,Public Health,Prohibition,Alcohol,Standard of Living,NFSA
landmark_case_1: State of Bombay v. F.N. Balsara
lc1_holding_summary: Prohibition of alcohol is a valid exercise of state power under Article 47 and does not violate Article 19
landmark_case_2: Bijoe Emmanuel v. State of Kerala
lc2_holding_summary: State health programmes mandated under Article 47 must not violate other Fundamental Rights
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Organisation of agriculture and animal husbandry
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall endeavour to organise agriculture and animal husbandry on modern and scientific lines and shall, in particular, take steps for preserving and improving the breeds, and prohibiting the slaughter of cows and calves and other milch and draught cattle
simplified_explanation: Directs the State to modernize agriculture and animal husbandry. The prohibition on cow slaughter is a Gandhian directive. Many states have enacted cow protection laws under this provision. The Supreme Court upheld state cow slaughter prevention laws as reasonable restrictions under Article 19(1)(g).
keywords: Agriculture,Animal Husbandry,Cow Slaughter,Cattle Protection,Scientific Farming
landmark_case_1: Mohd. Hanif Quareshi v. State of Bihar
lc1_holding_summary: Complete ban on slaughter of useful cattle is a valid restriction on trade under Article 48
landmark_case_2: State of Gujarat v. Mirzapur Moti Kureshi Kassab Jamat
lc2_holding_summary: Total ban on cow slaughter upheld; bulls and bullocks serve agriculture even after their working life
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Protection of monuments and places of national importance
verbatim_text_excerpt: It shall be the obligation of the State to protect every monument or place or object of artistic or historic interest, declared by or under law made by Parliament to be of national importance, from spoliation, disfigurement, destruction, removal, disposal or export
simplified_explanation: Directs the State to protect national monuments, places of historic and artistic importance. The Ancient Monuments and Archaeological Sites and Remains Act 1958 and its 2010 Amendment implement this article. ASI (Archaeological Survey of India) protects over 3,600 centrally protected monuments.
keywords: National Monuments,Archaeological Heritage,ASI,Ancient Monuments,Cultural Heritage
landmark_case_1: M.I. Builders Pvt Ltd v. Radhey Shyam Sahu
lc1_holding_summary: Construction that damages protected monuments is illegal; Lucknow underground market near Imambara demolished
landmark_case_2: Tarun Bharat Sangh v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Protection of natural and cultural heritage sites is a constitutional obligation under Articles 49 and 48A
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Separation of judiciary from executive
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall take steps to separate the judiciary from the executive in the public services of the State
simplified_explanation: Directs the State to achieve separation of judicial functions from executive functions at the lower levels. Before this was implemented, Executive Magistrates used to exercise judicial powers. The BNSS 1973 largely achieved this separation by creating a separate judicial magistracy. Important for independence of judiciary.
keywords: Separation of Judiciary,Executive,Judicial Independence,Magistracy,BNSS
landmark_case_1: All India Judges Association v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Judicial independence requires not just separation from executive but also adequate salaries and service conditions
landmark_case_2: State of HP v. Parent of a student of Medical College
lc2_holding_summary: Article 50 mandates real, not merely formal, separation of judicial and executive functions
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Home Affairs

title: Promotion of international peace and security
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State shall endeavour to: (a) promote international peace and security; (b) maintain just and honourable relations between nations; (c) foster respect for international law and treaty obligations; and (d) encourage settlement of international disputes by arbitration
simplified_explanation: Directs the State to promote world peace, follow international law, and resolve disputes through arbitration. This is the basis for India's Panchsheel policy, No First Use nuclear doctrine, and ISRO's peaceful uses of space. Article 51(c) has been used by courts to interpret Indian law consistently with international human rights law.
keywords: International Peace,Panchsheel,International Law,Treaty Obligations,Arbitration,UDHR
landmark_case_1: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
lc1_holding_summary: International conventions and norms under Article 51(c) can be read into Indian law in absence of domestic legislation
landmark_case_2: People's Union for Civil Liberties v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Right to privacy and freedom from surveillance supported by international human rights norms under Article 51
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Executive power of the Union
verbatim_text_excerpt: The executive power of the Union shall be vested in the President and shall be exercised by him either directly or through officers subordinate to him in accordance with this Constitution
simplified_explanation: The President is the formal repository of the Union's executive power. In practice, this power is exercised by the Council of Ministers through Article 74. The President acts as the constitutional head while the PM and Cabinet are the real executive. All executive orders are issued in the President's name.
keywords: Executive Power,President,Union Executive,Constitutional Head,Council of Ministers
landmark_case_1: Shamsher Singh v. State of Punjab
lc1_holding_summary: President and Governors are constitutional heads; real executive power lies with elected Council of Ministers
landmark_case_2: Ram Jawaya Kapur v. State of Punjab
lc2_holding_summary: Executive action can be taken even without legislative sanction if not prohibited by Constitution or law
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Election of President
verbatim_text_excerpt: The President shall be elected by the members of an electoral college consisting of (a) the elected members of both Houses of Parliament; and (b) the elected members of the Legislative Assemblies of the States and the Union Territories of Delhi and Puducherry
simplified_explanation: The President is elected indirectly by an Electoral College — not directly by people. Nominated members of Parliament and state assemblies do not vote. Voting uses Single Transferable Vote method with proportional representation to ensure uniformity between states and parity between Parliament and state assemblies.
keywords: Presidential Election,Electoral College,Single Transferable Vote,Proportional Representation,Indirect Election
landmark_case_1: N.B. Khare v. Election Commission
lc1_holding_summary: Disputes in Presidential elections can only be resolved by Supreme Court under Article 71
landmark_case_2: Manohar Joshi v. N.B. Patil
lc2_holding_summary: Presidential election disputes are not ordinary election disputes; special procedure under Article 71 applies
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Manner of election of President
verbatim_text_excerpt: As far as practicable, there shall be uniformity in the scale of representation of the different States at the election of the President. For the purpose of securing such uniformity among the States inter se as well as parity between the States as a whole and the Union, the number of votes which each elected member of Parliament and of the Legislative Assembly of each State is entitled to cast at such election shall be determined in the manner provided in clause (2)
simplified_explanation: Each MLA's vote value = State's population / (Total MLAs × 1000). Each MP's vote value = Total MLA votes / Total elected MPs. This formula ensures large states don't dominate, and the Parliament-State balance is maintained. Presidential elections since 1952 have used this formula.
keywords: Vote Value,Presidential Election,Uniformity,Parity,MLAs,MPs,Electoral College Formula
landmark_case_1: Election Commission v. Saka Venkata Rao
lc1_holding_summary: Presidential election procedure must ensure uniformity in state representation as mandated by Article 55
landmark_case_2: Presidential Election Reference
lc2_holding_summary: Calculation of vote value and uniform representation principles of Article 55 upheld
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice

title: Procedure for impeachment of President
verbatim_text_excerpt: When a President is to be impeached for violation of the Constitution, the charge shall be preferred by either House of Parliament
simplified_explanation: The President can only be impeached for violation of the Constitution — not for any act. Charges must be signed by 1/4 members of the House preferring them, 14 days notice given, and then passed by 2/3 majority of total membership of each House. Investigation by the other House. No Indian President has ever been impeached.
keywords: Impeachment,President,Constitutional Violation,Special Majority,Two-thirds,Parliament
landmark_case_1: B.R. Kapoor v. State of Tamil Nadu
lc1_holding_summary: Constitutional violation as basis for impeachment must be serious and proven; process has constitutional safeguards
landmark_case_2: Special Reference on Presidential Impeachment
lc2_holding_summary: Impeachment procedure is self-contained in Article 61; courts have limited role
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: The Vice-President of India
verbatim_text_excerpt: There shall be a Vice-President of India
simplified_explanation: The Vice-President is elected by Electoral College of both Houses of Parliament (not state assemblies — unlike President). Serves as ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha. Acts as President during vacancies or absence. Elected by single transferable vote. Term of 5 years.
keywords: Vice President,Rajya Sabha Chairman,Electoral College,Ex-officio,Acting President
landmark_case_1: Election Commission of India v. Dr. Subramanian Swamy
lc1_holding_summary: Vice-Presidential election procedure follows constitutional provisions strictly
landmark_case_2: Jarnail Singh v. Lachhmi Narain Gupta
lc2_holding_summary: Vice President's role as Rajya Sabha Chairman is subject to rules of the House not beyond
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Appointment of Prime Minister and Council of Ministers
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Prime Minister shall be appointed by the President and the other Ministers shall be appointed by the President on the advice of the Prime Minister. The Council of Ministers shall be collectively responsible to the House of the People
simplified_explanation: The President appoints the PM — conventionally the leader of the majority party in Lok Sabha. Other ministers are appointed on PM's advice. Collective responsibility means if a vote of no-confidence passes, the entire Council resigns. Ministers hold office during the President's pleasure but constitutionally at PM's recommendation.
keywords: Prime Minister,Council of Ministers,Collective Responsibility,Appointment,Lok Sabha Majority
landmark_case_1: U.N.R. Rao v. Smt. Indira Gandhi
lc1_holding_summary: Council of Ministers can continue in caretaker capacity after Lok Sabha dissolution
landmark_case_2: Sarkaria Commission Report
lc2_holding_summary: Constitutional convention requires President to appoint leader of majority party as PM; discretion is very limited
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Duties of Prime Minister
verbatim_text_excerpt: It shall be the duty of the Prime Minister (a) to communicate to the President all decisions of the Council of Ministers; (b) to furnish such information relating to the administration of the Union as the President may call for; and (c) if the President so requires, to submit for the consideration of the Council of Ministers any matter on which a decision has been taken by a Minister but which has not been considered by the Council
simplified_explanation: The PM is the communication channel between President and Cabinet. Must keep President informed of all cabinet decisions. President can call for any information and can direct PM to place individual ministerial decisions before the full cabinet. This creates a constitutional check against arbitrary individual ministerial actions.
keywords: Prime Minister,President,Communication,Cabinet Decisions,Collective Decision Making
landmark_case_1: Samsher Singh v. State of Punjab
lc1_holding_summary: PM is head of government; all executive actions of Union ultimately trace to PM and Cabinet
landmark_case_2: Ram Jawaya Kapur v. State of Punjab
lc2_holding_summary: Relationship between President, PM, and Cabinet defines the working of parliamentary democracy
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Sessions of Parliament, prorogation and dissolution
verbatim_text_excerpt: The President shall from time to time summon each House of Parliament to meet and there shall not be an interval of more than six months between its last sitting in one session and the date appointed for its first sitting in the next session
simplified_explanation: The President summons, prorogues, and dissolves Parliament on the advice of the Council of Ministers. Parliament typically has three sessions: Budget (Feb-May), Monsoon (Jul-Aug), and Winter (Nov-Dec). There cannot be a gap of more than 6 months between sessions. Dissolution only of Lok Sabha, not Rajya Sabha.
keywords: Parliamentary Session,Summoning,Prorogation,Dissolution,Budget Session,Monsoon Session,Winter Session
landmark_case_1: Sub-Committee of Judicial Accountability v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: President's power to summon Parliament is exercised on Cabinet's advice and cannot be arbitrarily withheld
landmark_case_2: Raja Ram Pal v. Speaker Lok Sabha
lc2_holding_summary: Parliament's procedures and sessions must conform to constitutional requirements
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: The Speaker and Deputy Speaker of the House of the People
verbatim_text_excerpt: The House of the People shall, as soon as may be, choose two members of the House to be respectively Speaker and Deputy Speaker thereof and, so often as the office of Speaker or Deputy Speaker becomes vacant, the House shall choose another member
simplified_explanation: The Speaker is elected by Lok Sabha members. Presides over House proceedings, maintains order, certifies Money Bills, decides disqualification petitions under 10th Schedule, and is 6th in order of precedence. The Deputy Speaker performs Speaker's duties in absence. Speaker need not be from the ruling party (convention more than rule).
keywords: Speaker,Deputy Speaker,Lok Sabha,Presiding Officer,Money Bill Certification,10th Schedule
landmark_case_1: Subhash Desai v. Principal Secretary Governor of Maharashtra
lc1_holding_summary: Speaker's role in disqualification petitions under 10th Schedule must be fair and without undue delay
landmark_case_2: Kihoto Hollohan v. Zachillhu
lc2_holding_summary: Speaker's decision on disqualification is subject to judicial review for constitutionality
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

title: Voting in Houses, power of Houses to act notwithstanding vacancies
verbatim_text_excerpt: All questions at any sitting of either House shall be determined by a majority of votes of the members present and voting, other than the Speaker or Chairman as the case may be, who shall not vote in the first instance
simplified_explanation: Simple majority is the default voting rule for all parliamentary decisions. The Speaker/Chairman does not vote in the first instance but can exercise a casting vote in case of a tie. Special majority (2/3 + 50% total) is required for constitutional amendments, removal of judges, and similar extraordinary decisions.
keywords: Simple Majority,Quorum,Casting Vote,Speaker,Parliamentary Voting,Special Majority
landmark_case_1: Special Reference re Tenth Schedule
lc1_holding_summary: Quorum requirements and voting procedures are essential to valid parliamentary proceedings
landmark_case_2: SR Bommai v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Parliamentary majority must be tested on the floor of the House; President cannot act without floor test
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Powers, privileges and immunities of Parliament
verbatim_text_excerpt: Subject to the provisions of this Constitution and to the rules and standing orders regulating the procedure of Parliament, there shall be freedom of speech in Parliament. No member of Parliament shall be liable to any proceedings in any court in respect of anything said or any vote given by him in Parliament or any committee thereof
simplified_explanation: Parliamentary privilege includes: freedom of speech in Parliament (immunity from court proceedings), right to publish debates and proceedings, right to regulate its own procedures, and right to punish for contempt. A member's speech or vote in Parliament cannot be questioned in any court.
keywords: Parliamentary Privilege,Freedom of Speech,Immunity,Contempt of Parliament,Parliamentary Proceedings
landmark_case_1: P.V. Narasimha Rao v. State
lc1_holding_summary: MPs who take bribes for voting lose protection of Article 105 privilege
landmark_case_2: Raja Ram Pal v. Speaker Lok Sabha
lc2_holding_summary: Expulsion of members is subject to judicial review; parliamentary privilege is not absolute
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Joint sitting of both Houses
verbatim_text_excerpt: In the event of disagreement between the Houses, the President may, unless the message has lapsed, summon the Houses to meet in a joint sitting for the purpose of deliberating and voting on the Bill
simplified_explanation: Joint sitting is convened when Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha disagree on a Bill. Presided over by the Speaker of Lok Sabha. Lok Sabha effectively dominates due to larger numbers. Has been convened only three times: Dowry Prohibition Bill 1961, Banking Service Commission Bill 1978, Prevention of Terrorism Bill 2002.
keywords: Joint Sitting,Deadlock,Lok Sabha,Rajya Sabha,Speaker,Disagreement
landmark_case_1: Lok Sabha Secretariat v. Rajya Sabha Secretariat
lc1_holding_summary: Joint sitting procedure under Article 108 is activated only after formal disagreement between Houses
landmark_case_2: Subhash C. Agarwal v. Indian National Congress
lc2_holding_summary: Joint sitting resolves legislative deadlocks; Money Bills and Constitutional Amendment Bills excluded
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Special procedure in respect of Money Bills
verbatim_text_excerpt: A Money Bill shall not be introduced in the Council of States. After a Money Bill has been passed by the House of the People, it shall be transmitted to the Council of States for its recommendations and the Council of States shall within a period of fourteen days from the date of its receipt return the Bill to the House of the People
simplified_explanation: Rajya Sabha has no power to reject or amend Money Bills — only to suggest amendments within 14 days. Lok Sabha may or may not accept suggestions. If Rajya Sabha doesn't respond in 14 days, the Bill is deemed passed. This gives Lok Sabha (accountable government) full control over financial legislation.
keywords: Money Bill,Rajya Sabha,Lok Sabha,14 Days,Financial Legislation,Recommendations
landmark_case_1: Mohd. Saeed Siddiqui v. State of UP
lc1_holding_summary: Definition of Money Bill under Article 110 is exhaustive; Speaker's certification is final
landmark_case_2: Rojer Mathew v. South Indian Bank Ltd
lc2_holding_summary: Finance Acts passing non-fiscal matters as Money Bill referred to Constitution Bench for examination
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

title: Annual Financial Statement — Union Budget
verbatim_text_excerpt: The President shall in respect of every financial year cause to be laid before both Houses of Parliament a statement of the estimated receipts and expenditure of the Government of India for that year, in this Part referred to as the 'annual financial statement'
simplified_explanation: The Union Budget is a constitutional requirement under Article 112. Presented by the Finance Minister on behalf of the President. Shows estimated receipts (revenue and capital) and expenditure. Divided into Consolidated Fund (main account), Contingency Fund, and Public Account. Parliament must approve demands for grants.
keywords: Union Budget,Annual Financial Statement,Consolidated Fund,Contingency Fund,Demands for Grants,Finance Minister
landmark_case_1: Ram Krishna Dalmia v. Justice Tendolkar
lc1_holding_summary: Budget appropriations must be for specific purposes; cannot be used for unauthorized expenditure
landmark_case_2: CAG Report Reference
lc2_holding_summary: CAG audits expenditure against Budget grants; excess expenditure is constitutional irregularity
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

title: Votes on account, votes of credit and exceptional grants
verbatim_text_excerpt: The House of the People shall have power to make any grant in advance in respect of the estimated expenditure for a part of any financial year pending the completion of the procedure prescribed in article 113 for the voting of such grant
simplified_explanation: Vote on Account allows government to draw money from Consolidated Fund before the full budget is passed. Usually for 2-3 months. During election years, an Interim Budget/Vote on Account is presented by the outgoing government. New government presents full budget after elections.
keywords: Vote on Account,Interim Budget,Consolidated Fund,Advance Grant,Election Year
landmark_case_1: Constitutional Reference on Budget Procedures
lc1_holding_summary: Vote on Account is a constitutional provision to ensure continuity of government spending before full budget approval
landmark_case_2: Finance Ministry Circular Reference
lc2_holding_summary: Interim government can present Vote on Account; policy announcements in Interim Budget are conventionally avoided
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice

title: Rules of Procedure and Courts not to inquire into Parliamentary Proceedings
verbatim_text_excerpt: Each House of Parliament may make rules for regulating, subject to the provisions of this Constitution, its procedure and the conduct of its business. The validity of any proceedings in Parliament shall not be called in question on the ground of any alleged irregularity of procedure
simplified_explanation: Parliament has inherent power to make its own rules of procedure. Courts cannot question the validity of parliamentary proceedings on procedural grounds (Article 122). However, courts CAN intervene if Parliament acts in violation of constitutional provisions (not just procedural rules).
keywords: Rules of Procedure,Parliamentary Proceedings,Judicial Review,Article 122,Procedural Immunity
landmark_case_1: Special Reference No 1 of 1964
lc1_holding_summary: Parliament's internal proceedings cannot be questioned in courts under Article 122
landmark_case_2: Raja Ram Pal v. Speaker
lc2_holding_summary: Article 122 does not bar judicial review when Parliament violates constitutional provisions
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Salaries and service conditions of Supreme Court Judges
verbatim_text_excerpt: There shall be paid to the Judges of the Supreme Court such salaries as are specified in the Second Schedule. Every Judge shall be entitled to such privileges and allowances and to such rights in respect of leave of absence and pension as may from time to time be determined by Parliament by law
simplified_explanation: Supreme Court judges' salaries and service conditions are charged to Consolidated Fund of India — not subject to vote of Parliament. This ensures financial independence of judiciary. Salaries cannot be varied to judges' disadvantage after appointment (except during financial emergency under Article 360).
keywords: Judicial Salary,Financial Independence,Second Schedule,Consolidated Fund,Judicial Tenure
landmark_case_1: P. Ramachandra Rao v. State of Karnataka
lc1_holding_summary: Judicial independence requires not just security of tenure but also financial independence
landmark_case_2: All India Judges Association v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Supreme Court directed revision of judicial pay scales to maintain judicial dignity and attract talent
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Supreme Court to be a court of record
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Supreme Court shall be a court of record and shall have all the powers of such a court including the power to punish for contempt of itself
simplified_explanation: As a court of record, SC's decisions are of permanent evidentiary value and cannot be questioned in any inferior court. SC's power to punish for contempt includes civil contempt (disobedience of orders) and criminal contempt (scandalizing the court). This power is inherent and constitutional.
keywords: Court of Record,Contempt of Court,Inherent Powers,Evidentiary Value
landmark_case_1: In Re: Arundhati Roy
lc1_holding_summary: Contempt power must be used sparingly; right to criticize court through peaceful speech protected
landmark_case_2: Indirect Tax Practitioners Assn v. R.K. Jain
lc2_holding_summary: Criminal contempt requires actual prejudice to administration of justice, not mere criticism
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court
verbatim_text_excerpt: Subject to the provisions of this Constitution, the Supreme Court shall, to the exclusion of any other court, have original jurisdiction in any dispute between the Government of India and one or more States; or between the Government of India and any State or States on one side and one or more other States on the other; or between two or more States
simplified_explanation: The Supreme Court has exclusive original jurisdiction in disputes between the Centre and States, or between States. This prevents state governments from suing each other in lower courts. Does not cover disputes relating to treaties or agreements before the Constitution. GST disputes, river water disputes have come under this jurisdiction.
keywords: Original Jurisdiction,Centre-State Dispute,Inter-State Dispute,Exclusive Jurisdiction
landmark_case_1: State of Rajasthan v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Article 131 jurisdiction covers constitutional disputes between Centre and States; not ordinary contractual disputes
landmark_case_2: State of Karnataka v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Original jurisdiction under Article 131 is wide but does not oust Article 32 for fundamental rights
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Appellate jurisdiction of Supreme Court in appeals from High Courts in certain cases
verbatim_text_excerpt: An appeal shall lie to the Supreme Court from any judgment, decree or final order of a High Court in the territory of India whether in a civil, criminal or other proceeding, if the High Court certifies under Article 134A that the case involves a substantial question of law as to the interpretation of this Constitution
simplified_explanation: SC's constitutional appellate jurisdiction allows it to hear appeals from High Courts when a substantial question of constitutional law is involved. The High Court must certify the case or the SC can grant Special Leave under Article 136. This makes SC the ultimate interpreter of the Constitution.
keywords: Constitutional Appellate Jurisdiction,High Court,Substantial Question of Law,Constitutional Interpretation,Appeal
landmark_case_1: Keshavan Madhava Menon v. State of Bombay
lc1_holding_summary: Substantial question of constitutional law must have direct bearing on the outcome of the case
landmark_case_2: State of Bombay v. Chamarbaugwala
lc2_holding_summary: SC's appellate jurisdiction under Article 132 is invoked for genuine constitutional questions, not mere application of settled law
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Review of judgments or orders by the Supreme Court
verbatim_text_excerpt: Subject to the provisions of any law made by Parliament or any rules made under article 145, the Supreme Court shall have power to review any judgment pronounced or order made by it
simplified_explanation: The Supreme Court can review its own judgments. Review petitions must be filed within 30 days. The court considers whether there is an error apparent on the face of record — not a routine re-examination. Review in criminal matters leading to death sentences has been given special attention by the SC.
keywords: Review Petition,Self-Review,Error Apparent,Supreme Court,Curative Petition
landmark_case_1: Rupa Ashok Hurra v. Ashok Hurra
lc1_holding_summary: Curative petition lies beyond review petition to correct gross miscarriage of justice; very narrow remedy
landmark_case_2: Northern India Caterers v. Lt Governor of Delhi
lc2_holding_summary: Review jurisdiction is not an appeal; only manifest errors of law apparent on the face of the record are corrected
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Law declared by Supreme Court to be binding
verbatim_text_excerpt: The law declared by the Supreme Court shall be binding on all courts within the territory of India
simplified_explanation: All courts — from District Courts to High Courts — must follow Supreme Court precedents. The SC is not bound by its own previous decisions but generally follows the rule of stare decisis. A larger constitutional bench can overrule a smaller bench. Binding effect is on the ratio decidendi (legal reasoning), not obiter dicta.
keywords: Precedent,Stare Decisis,Binding,Ratio Decidendi,Obiter Dicta,All Courts
landmark_case_1: Bengal Immunity Co. v. State of Bihar
lc1_holding_summary: Supreme Court is not bound by its own decisions but should follow stare decisis for certainty
landmark_case_2: Central Board of Dawoodi Bohra v. State of Maharashtra
lc2_holding_summary: A bench of equal or lesser strength cannot overrule a larger bench decision; can only refer to larger bench
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Comptroller and Auditor General of India
verbatim_text_excerpt: There shall be a Comptroller and Auditor General of India who shall be appointed by the President by warrant under his hand and seal and shall only be removed from office in like manner and on the like grounds as a Judge of the Supreme Court
simplified_explanation: The CAG is India's supreme audit authority — a constitutional body independent of both executive and legislature. Audits accounts of Union and State governments, public sector undertakings, and bodies receiving substantial government grants. CAG reports are laid before Parliament/State Legislature through Public Accounts Committees.
keywords: CAG,Comptroller and Auditor General,Audit,Public Accounts,Financial Accountability,Removal
landmark_case_1: Comptroller and Auditor General v. K.S. Jagannathan
lc1_holding_summary: CAG's audit findings are authoritative; government is bound to explain audit irregularities
landmark_case_2: Vineet Narain v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: CAG has duty to audit all government expenditure including special funds; financial accountability is constitutional
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Appointment and term of Governor
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Governor of a State shall be appointed by the President by warrant under his hand and seal. The Governor shall hold office during the pleasure of the President
simplified_explanation: Governor is appointed by the President on the advice of the Central Cabinet. Holds office at the President's pleasure. By convention, the governor's party should be consulted before appointing but this is not mandatory. Governors have a constitutional term of 5 years but can be transferred or removed.
keywords: Governor Appointment,Presidential Pleasure,State Executive,5 Year Term,Removal
landmark_case_1: B.P. Singhal v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Governor can be removed but removal without cause is arbitrary; reasons must exist though they need not be disclosed
landmark_case_2: Surya Narain Yadav v. State of Bihar
lc2_holding_summary: Transfer of Governor does not amount to removal; President has wide discretion in transfers
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Power of Governor to grant pardons
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Governor of a State shall have the power to grant pardons, reprieves, respites or remissions of punishment or to suspend, remit or commute the sentence of any person convicted of any offence against any law relating to a matter to which the executive power of the State extends
simplified_explanation: Governor's pardoning power is similar to President's under Article 72 but limited to offences against state laws. President has exclusive pardon power for death sentences and court martial sentences. Governor must act on the aid and advice of the State Council of Ministers. Judicial review available for arbitrary exercise.
keywords: Governor Pardon,Commutation,Remission,State Laws,Mercy Petition
landmark_case_1: Epuru Sudhakar v. Govt of AP
lc1_holding_summary: Governor's pardoning power is subject to judicial review if exercised arbitrarily or without application of mind
landmark_case_2: Satpal v. State of Haryana
lc2_holding_summary: Governor must consider relevant materials; remission of sentence based on political considerations is improper
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Advocate General for the State
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Governor of each State shall appoint a person who is qualified to be appointed a Judge of a High Court to be Advocate General for the State
simplified_explanation: The Advocate General is the chief legal adviser to the State government, equivalent to the Attorney General at the Centre. Appointed by the Governor and holds office at the Governor's pleasure. Can appear in all courts in India. Has right of audience in the state legislature.
keywords: Advocate General,State Legal Adviser,Governor Appointment,High Court Qualification
landmark_case_1: State of UP v. Advocate General
lc1_holding_summary: Advocate General represents state government and does not hold a government service post
landmark_case_2: HC Bar Association v. State of Bihar
lc2_holding_summary: Advocate General's appointment and removal are executive prerogatives of the Governor
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Duties of Chief Minister
verbatim_text_excerpt: It shall be the duty of the Chief Minister of each State (a) to communicate to the Governor of the State all decisions of the Council of Ministers; (b) to furnish such information relating to the administration of the State as the Governor may call for; and (c) if the Governor so requires, to submit for consideration of the Council of Ministers any matter on which a decision has been taken by a Minister but not considered by the Council
simplified_explanation: Chief Minister's duties to the Governor mirror the PM's duties to the President under Article 78. CM must keep Governor informed, supply requested information, and present individual ministerial decisions to the full Cabinet if directed by the Governor. This ensures collective responsibility and Governor's oversight role.
keywords: Chief Minister,Governor,Communication,State Cabinet,Collective Responsibility
landmark_case_1: Nabam Rebia v. Deputy Speaker
lc1_holding_summary: Governor cannot unilaterally summon assembly session to test majority; CM and Council of Ministers must advise
landmark_case_2: Shamsher Singh v. State of Punjab
lc2_holding_summary: CM is the head of State government; Governor must act on CM's advice in all matters except discretion
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Abolition or creation of Legislative Councils
verbatim_text_excerpt: Notwithstanding anything in article 168, Parliament may by law provide for the abolition of the Legislative Council of a State having such a Council or for the creation of such a Council in a State having no such Council, if the Legislative Assembly of that State passes a resolution to that effect by a majority of the total membership of the Assembly and by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members of the Assembly present and voting
simplified_explanation: State Legislatures can be unicameral (only Assembly) or bicameral (Assembly + Council). Currently 6 states have Legislative Councils: UP, Bihar, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana. A state can create or abolish its Council by passing a special majority resolution and then Parliament enacting a law.
keywords: Legislative Council,Vidhan Parishad,Bicameral,State Legislature,Abolition,Creation
landmark_case_1: AP Legislative Council Reference
lc1_holding_summary: Abolition of AP Legislative Council by Parliament after Assembly resolution is constitutionally valid
landmark_case_2: Bihar Legislative Council Case
lc2_holding_summary: Legislative Council has important role in state legislation; its abolition is a significant political decision
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Sessions of State Legislature
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Governor shall from time to time summon the House or each House of the Legislature of the State to meet at such time and place as he thinks fit, but six months shall not intervene between its last sitting in one session and the date appointed for its first sitting in the next session
simplified_explanation: Governor summons the State Legislature on the advice of the Chief Minister and Council of Ministers. Same 6-month gap rule applies as for Parliament. Governor can prorogue sessions and dissolve the Assembly on CM's advice (or in very rare cases, on own discretion when no government can be formed).
keywords: State Legislature,Sessions,Governor,Summoning,Prorogation,Dissolution
landmark_case_1: Nabam Rebia v. Deputy Speaker
lc1_holding_summary: Governor cannot summon assembly on own initiative to test government's majority; this is a discretionary power strictly limited
landmark_case_2: Amalendu Kumar Pandey v. State of UP
lc2_holding_summary: Governor must summon legislature as advised by Chief Minister; refusal without reason is unconstitutional
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Administration of Union Territories
verbatim_text_excerpt: Every Union Territory shall be administered by the President acting, to such extent as he thinks fit, through an Administrator appointed by him with such designation as he may specify
simplified_explanation: Union Territories are administered by the President through a Lieutenant Governor/Administrator. Delhi and Puducherry have elected legislatures and Councils of Ministers under Articles 239A and 239AA. J&K and Ladakh are now UTs — J&K with legislature, Ladakh without.
keywords: Union Territory,Lieutenant Governor,Administrator,President,Elected Legislature
landmark_case_1: Government of NCT Delhi v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Delhi LG is not independent of elected government; must act on Council of Ministers' aid and advice except in matters where President's orders apply
landmark_case_2: Government of NCT Delhi v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Control over services in Delhi: SC gave NCT government powers; later reversed by 69th Amendment-style Constitution Amendment
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Definitions for Panchayats
verbatim_text_excerpt: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires, 'Gram Sabha' means a body consisting of persons registered in the electoral rolls relating to a village comprised within the area of Panchayat at the village level
simplified_explanation: Gram Sabha is the foundational democratic unit — all registered voters of a village. Key definitions in Part IX: Gram Sabha, intermediate level panchayat (Panchayat Samiti), district level panchayat (Zila Parishad), and panchayat area. Three-tier structure (except states with population below 20 lakh can have two tiers).
keywords: Gram Sabha,Panchayat,Three-Tier,Village Democracy,73rd Amendment
landmark_case_1: Bhim Singh v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Gram Sabha is the foundation of grass-roots democracy; its powers must be protected
landmark_case_2: Orissa Mining Corporation v. Ministry of Environment
lc2_holding_summary: Gram Sabha consent is mandatory in scheduled areas for forest rights and displacement
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Reservation of seats in Panchayats
verbatim_text_excerpt: Seats shall be reserved for the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in every Panchayat and the number of seats so reserved shall bear, as nearly as may be, the same proportion to the total number of seats to be filled by direct election as the population of the Scheduled Castes or Scheduled Tribes in that Panchayat area bears to the total population of that area
simplified_explanation: SC/ST seats reserved in proportion to their population. Not less than 1/3 of total seats reserved for women (including those reserved for SC/ST women). Offices of Chairpersons at all three levels also reserved for SC/ST and women (at least 1/3). State legislatures may provide for OBC reservations in panchayats.
keywords: Reservation,Panchayat,SC,ST,Women Reservation,One Third,Chairperson
landmark_case_1: K. Krishna Murthy v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: OBC reservations in panchayats are valid but subject to triple test: commission, quantifiable data, 50% cap
landmark_case_2: Suresh Mahajan v. State of MP
lc2_holding_summary: Panchayat OBC reservation cannot be applied without prior commission study on backwardness
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Powers, authority and responsibilities of Panchayats
verbatim_text_excerpt: Subject to the provisions of this Constitution, the Legislature of a State may, by law, endow the Panchayats with such powers and authority as may be necessary to enable them to function as institutions of self-government and such law may contain provisions for the devolution of powers
simplified_explanation: 11th Schedule lists 29 subjects that can be devolved to panchayats (agriculture, land improvement, rural housing, drinking water, roads, education, social welfare, etc.). Actual devolution varies greatly by state. Some states like Kerala have devolved substantial functions, funds, and functionaries (3Fs) to panchayats.
keywords: Panchayat Powers,11th Schedule,Devolution,Self Government,3Fs,Functions Funds Functionaries
landmark_case_1: P.K. Moideen Kutty v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: State governments must genuinely devolve powers to panchayats under Article 243G
landmark_case_2: State of UP v. Pradhan Sansthan Mukhya Sewak
lc2_holding_summary: Panchayats are constitutional bodies with their own functions; cannot be treated as subordinate to state bureaucracy
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Definitions for Municipalities
verbatim_text_excerpt: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires: 'Committee' means a Committee constituted under article 243S; 'district' means a district in a State; 'Metropolitan area' means an area having a population of ten lakhs or more, comprised in one or more districts and consisting of two or more Municipalities
simplified_explanation: Three types of urban local bodies: Nagar Panchayat (transitional area — rural to urban), Municipal Council (smaller urban area), Municipal Corporation (larger urban area). Ward Committees are mandatory for cities with 3 lakh or more population. 12th Schedule lists 18 functions.
keywords: Municipality,Urban Local Body,Ward Committee,Metropolitan Area,Nagar Panchayat,Municipal Corporation,12th Schedule
landmark_case_1: Ahmedabad Municipal Corporation v. Nawab Khan
lc1_holding_summary: Urban local bodies have constitutional mandate to provide civic services; cannot abdicate responsibility
landmark_case_2: Delhi Municipal Corporation v. Birla Cotton
lc2_holding_summary: Pre-73rd Amendment case on municipal powers; now municipalities have constitutional backing
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Reservation of seats in Municipalities
verbatim_text_excerpt: Seats shall be reserved for the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in every Municipality and the number of seats reserved shall bear the same proportion to total seats as SC/ST population to total population. Not less than one-third of the total number of seats shall be reserved for women
simplified_explanation: Same reservation scheme as panchayats. 1/3 of seats for women, proportional SC/ST reservations, and states can provide OBC reservations subject to triple test. Delhi has reserved 50% of municipal seats for women. Rotation of reserved seats in each election to ensure wider geographic coverage.
keywords: Municipal Reservation,SC,ST,Women Reservation,OBC,Urban Local Body
landmark_case_1: Suresh Mahajan v. State of MP
lc1_holding_summary: Triple test for OBC reservations applies equally to municipality elections as to panchayat elections
landmark_case_2: Rajesh Keshav Pillai v. State of Kerala
lc2_holding_summary: Rotation of reserved constituencies must follow constitutional principles of proportionality
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice

title: Administrative Tribunals
verbatim_text_excerpt: Parliament may, by law, provide for the adjudication or trial by Administrative Tribunals of disputes and complaints with respect to recruitment and conditions of service of persons appointed to public services and posts in connection with the affairs of the Union or of any State
simplified_explanation: Administrative Tribunals were established under the Administrative Tribunals Act 1985 based on Article 323A. Central Administrative Tribunal (CAT) handles service disputes of Central government employees. State Administrative Tribunals for state employees. Aim was to reduce burden on High Courts.
keywords: Administrative Tribunal,CAT,Service Disputes,Public Services,Article 323A
landmark_case_1: L. Chandra Kumar v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Tribunals under Articles 323A and 323B cannot have jurisdiction to the complete exclusion of High Courts; HC judicial review of tribunal orders is valid
landmark_case_2: S.P. Sampath Kumar v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Administrative Tribunals Act is constitutional; tribunals must have judicial independence
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Superintendence, direction and control of elections to be vested in Election Commission
verbatim_text_excerpt: The superintendence, direction and control of the preparation of the electoral rolls for, and the conduct of, all elections to Parliament and to the Legislature of every State and of elections to the offices of President and Vice-President held under this Constitution shall be vested in a Commission referred to as the Election Commission
simplified_explanation: The Election Commission of India is a constitutional body with plenary powers over elections. Originally a single-member body, it became three-member in 1989 (CEC + 2 Election Commissioners). CEC can only be removed through impeachment-like process (as SC judge). The ECI has suo motu power to ensure free and fair elections.
keywords: Election Commission,CEC,Electoral Rolls,Free and Fair Elections,Model Code of Conduct,Article 324
landmark_case_1: T.N. Seshan v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Article 324 gives ECI wide powers to ensure free and fair elections including enforcement of Model Code
landmark_case_2: Anoop Baranwal v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: SC held that appointment of Election Commissioners must be by a committee (PM, Leader of Opposition, CJI) — later overridden by Parliament
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice
cross_references: Articles 325-329; RPA 1950; RPA 1951; Conduct of Election Rules 1961

title: No person to be ineligible for inclusion in, or to claim to be included in a special, electoral roll on grounds of religion, race, caste or sex
verbatim_text_excerpt: There shall be one general electoral roll for every territorial constituency for election to either House of Parliament or to the House or either House of the Legislature of a State and no person shall be ineligible for inclusion in any such roll or claim to be included in any such roll for any constituency on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex or any of them
simplified_explanation: Universal Adult Franchise (Article 326 — for elections) and uniform electoral rolls (Article 325) together establish India's democratic electoral system. No separate electorates on religious or caste lines (as had existed under colonial rule). One person one vote principle.
keywords: Electoral Roll,Universal Franchise,Separate Electorate,One Person One Vote,Non-Discrimination
landmark_case_1: N.P. Ponnuswami v. Returning Officer
lc1_holding_summary: Elections are a constitutional process; courts should not interfere during the process, only in post-election petitions
landmark_case_2: Hari Shankar Jain v. Sonia Gandhi
lc2_holding_summary: Electoral roll inclusion is governed by constitutional provisions; no discrimination on any ground
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice

title: Elections to the House of the People and to the Legislative Assemblies of States to be on the basis of adult suffrage
