verbatim_text_excerpt: The elections to the House of the People and to the Legislative Assembly of every State shall be on the basis of adult suffrage; that is to say, every person who is a citizen of India and who is not less than eighteen years of age on such date as may be fixed in that behalf by or under any law made by the appropriate Legislature and is not otherwise disqualified under this Constitution or any law made by the appropriate Legislature on the ground of non-residence, unsoundness of mind, crime or corrupt or illegal practice, shall be entitled to be registered as a voter
simplified_explanation: Universal Adult Suffrage — every adult citizen above 18 can vote. Voting age was reduced from 21 to 18 by 61st Constitutional Amendment 1988. Disqualifications: non-resident, unsound mind, convicted, corrupt practice. Postal ballot for some categories. NOTA (None of the Above) option introduced in 2013.
keywords: Universal Adult Suffrage,Voting Age,61st Amendment,NOTA,Electoral Roll,Disqualification
landmark_case_1: Mohan Lal v. State of Rajasthan
lc1_holding_summary: Universal adult suffrage is a fundamental democratic principle; restrictions on voting rights must be strictly construed
landmark_case_2: Association for Democratic Reforms v. Election Commission
lc2_holding_summary: Right to know antecedents of candidates is part of voters' right under Article 326 read with Article 19(1)(a)
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: First Schedule — States and Union Territories
verbatim_text_excerpt: The First Schedule lists the 28 States and 8 Union Territories of India with their territorial extent. It is modified whenever new states are created or boundaries are changed under Article 3.
simplified_explanation: Currently lists 28 states (after J&K became UT in 2019) and 8 UTs (Jammu & Kashmir, Ladakh, Delhi, Puducherry, Chandigarh, Dadra & Nagar Haveli and Daman & Diu, Lakshadweep, Andaman & Nicobar Islands). J&K's downgrade from state to UT was challenged and upheld by SC in 2023 with a promise of restoring statehood.
keywords: First Schedule,States,Union Territories,Territorial Organization
landmark_case_1: In Re Article 370
lc1_holding_summary: Reorganisation of J&K into two UTs is constitutionally valid; statehood to be restored
landmark_case_2: Babulal Parate v. State of Bombay
lc2_holding_summary: First Schedule can be amended by Parliament through Article 3 procedure
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Second Schedule — Salaries and Allowances of Constitutional Functionaries
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Second Schedule specifies the salaries, allowances, and other service conditions of: the President, the Governors of States, the Speaker and Deputy Speaker of Lok Sabha, the Chairman and Deputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha, Supreme Court Judges, High Court Judges, and the Comptroller and Auditor General
simplified_explanation: These emoluments are charged to the Consolidated Fund and are not subject to parliamentary vote — ensuring financial independence of these constitutional offices. Salaries of SC judges cannot be reduced during their service. This protects constitutional functionaries from financial pressure or coercion.
keywords: Second Schedule,Salaries,Constitutional Functionaries,President,Judges,Speaker,CAG
landmark_case_1: All India Judges Association v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Second Schedule emoluments of judges must be revised periodically to maintain judicial dignity and independence
landmark_case_2: Union of India v. Sankalchand Himatlal Sheth
lc2_holding_summary: Conditions of service of High Court Judges including salary fall under Second Schedule; cannot be changed to their disadvantage
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Third Schedule — Forms of Oaths and Affirmations
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Third Schedule contains the forms of oaths and affirmations to be taken by various constitutional functionaries including the President, Governors, Union Ministers, Members of Parliament, Supreme Court Judges, Comptroller and Auditor General, High Court Judges, and Members of State Legislatures
simplified_explanation: Constitutional oath-taking is a solemn act of commitment to the Constitution. Judges swear to bear true faith and allegiance to the Constitution and to uphold it. Ministers swear to discharge their duties faithfully. MPs/MLAs swear to bear true faith to the Constitution. Violation of oath is a ground for removal.
keywords: Oath,Affirmation,Third Schedule,Constitutional Oath,Swearing In
landmark_case_1: State of Rajasthan v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Constitutional oath is not merely ceremonial; breach of oath by a constitutional functionary can be grounds for action
landmark_case_2: Union of India v. Gopal Chandra Misra
lc2_holding_summary: Oath of office carries constitutional significance; oath-takers are bound by its terms
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Fifth Schedule — Provisions for Administration of Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Fifth Schedule deals with the administration and control of Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes in states other than Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, and Mizoram. The Governor of every state having Scheduled Areas must annually report to the President.
simplified_explanation: Special constitutional provision for tribal governance in mainland India. The Governor has special powers for Scheduled Areas. Tribes Advisory Council advises Governor. Regulations enacted by Governor can be made applicable to Scheduled Areas. Land transfer in Scheduled Areas to non-tribals requires special approval. PESA 1996 implements Fifth Schedule.
keywords: Fifth Schedule,Scheduled Areas,Scheduled Tribes,Governor,Tribes Advisory Council,PESA
landmark_case_1: Samatha v. State of AP
lc1_holding_summary: State cannot transfer tribal land in Scheduled Areas to non-tribal companies; government is trustee of tribal resources
landmark_case_2: Nandini Sundar v. State of Chhattisgarh
lc2_holding_summary: Fifth Schedule areas require special constitutional protection; armed vigilantes violate tribal rights
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Sixth Schedule — Provisions for Administration of Tribal Areas in North-East
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Sixth Schedule applies to tribal areas of Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, and Mizoram. It provides for Autonomous District Councils (ADCs) and Autonomous Regional Councils with legislative, judicial, and administrative powers.
simplified_explanation: Stronger than Fifth Schedule — ADCs have quasi-legislative, executive, and judicial powers. They can make laws on land management, forests, use of waterways, money lending regulation, social customs. HC has jurisdiction over ADC courts but ADC courts have original jurisdiction in tribal disputes. NEFA (Arunachal Pradesh) and Manipur hills have similar special status.
keywords: Sixth Schedule,Autonomous District Council,North East India,Tribal Areas,ADC Powers,Assam,Meghalaya
landmark_case_1: State of Assam v. Barga Tea Estate
lc1_holding_summary: ADC powers under Sixth Schedule are constitutional and derive from constituent power not from state legislature
landmark_case_2: Shillong Municipal Board v. Government of Meghalaya
lc2_holding_summary: Sixth Schedule ADC areas have special constitutional status; municipal laws must be adapted to tribal governance
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

title: Ninth Schedule — Acts and Regulations placed beyond Judicial Review
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Ninth Schedule was added by the 1st Amendment 1951. It contains a list of Central and State Acts which are declared to be immune from challenge under Fundamental Rights (originally under Articles 31A and 31B).
simplified_explanation: Originally created to protect land reform laws from judicial invalidation. Now contains 284 laws including land reform acts, mines and minerals acts, import and export acts, and various economic legislation. Post 2007 IR Coelho judgment, laws added after 24 April 1973 ARE subject to judicial review if they violate basic structure.
keywords: Ninth Schedule,Judicial Review,Land Reform,IR Coelho,Basic Structure,First Amendment,31B
landmark_case_1: I.R. Coelho v. State of Tamil Nadu
lc1_holding_summary: Laws in Ninth Schedule added after 24 April 1973 are subject to judicial review on basic structure grounds
landmark_case_2: Waman Rao v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Laws in Ninth Schedule added before 24 April 1973 have full immunity from Fundamental Rights challenge
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Tenth Schedule (Constitution) — Anti-Defection Law
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Tenth Schedule (added by 52nd Amendment 1985) disqualifies a Member of Parliament or State Legislature from membership if: (a) they voluntarily give up the membership of their political party; or (b) they vote or abstain from voting contrary to the party's direction without permission
simplified_explanation: The Tenth Schedule constitutionalizes anti-defection rules for elected legislators. It disqualifies members who defect, subject to statutory interpretation and the shifting jurisprudence on Speaker/Chairman adjudication, splits, and mergers.
keywords: Tenth Schedule,Anti-Defection,Defection,Disqualification,Legislators
landmark_case_1: Kihoto Hollohan v. Zachillhu
lc1_holding_summary: 10th Schedule is constitutional; Speaker's disqualification decision judicially reviewable only on limited grounds
landmark_case_2: Subhash Desai v. Principal Secretary Governor of Maharashtra
lc2_holding_summary: Speaker cannot delay disqualification petitions indefinitely; SC can intervene in such delays
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Eleventh Schedule — Panchayat Functions (29 Subjects)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Eleventh Schedule (added by 73rd Amendment 1992) lists 29 subjects over which panchayats can be given powers by State Legislature. These include agriculture, land improvement, irrigation, animal husbandry, fisheries, social forestry, rural housing, drinking water, roads, education, social welfare, and poverty alleviation programmes.
simplified_explanation: The 29 subjects in the 11th Schedule represent the potential scope of panchayat governance. However, actual devolution is left to state governments. Most states have devolved some but not all functions. Kerala's 'People's Plan Campaign' (1996-97) is cited as the best practice for genuine devolution of all 29 subjects.
keywords: Eleventh Schedule,29 Subjects,Panchayat Functions,Devolution,73rd Amendment,Local Governance
landmark_case_1: Bhim Singh v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: States must genuinely devolve 11th Schedule functions to panchayats; tokenism is insufficient
landmark_case_2: State of Kerala v. Panchayat
lc2_holding_summary: Panchayats must be given functional autonomy over 11th Schedule subjects once devolution is made by state
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Twelfth Schedule — Municipality Functions (18 Subjects)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Twelfth Schedule (added by 74th Amendment 1992) lists 18 subjects for municipalities: urban planning, regulation of land use, public health, water supply, waste management, fire services, urban forestry, safeguarding weaker sections, slum improvement, public amenities, and regulation of butcher-houses, tanneries
simplified_explanation: 18 urban functions that state legislatures may devolve to municipalities. Like the 11th Schedule, actual devolution varies. Metropolitan Planning Committees (for cities above 10 lakh) and District Planning Committees (for rural-urban integration) are also provided under Articles 243ZD and 243ZE.
keywords: Twelfth Schedule,18 Subjects,Municipality Functions,Urban Planning,74th Amendment,Urban Governance
landmark_case_1: Ahmedabad Municipal Corporation v. Nawab Khan Gulab Khan
lc1_holding_summary: Municipalities have constitutional duty to provide civic services under 12th Schedule functions
landmark_case_2: T.N. Godavarman v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Urban local bodies must integrate environmental concerns in urban planning under 12th Schedule
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: No Thirteenth Schedule — Note on Schedule Numbering
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Indian Constitution does not have a Thirteenth Schedule. The Schedules go from First to Twelfth. This is a common misconception in competitive exams. There are only 12 Schedules in the Indian Constitution as of 2024.
simplified_explanation: Important factual note: India's Constitution has exactly 12 Schedules. There is no 13th Schedule. Original Constitution had 8 Schedules. Amendments added the 9th (1951), 10th (1985), 11th (1992), and 12th (1992) Schedules. Total: 12 Schedules, 25 Parts (approximately), 448 Articles (as originally numbered).
keywords: Schedules,12 Schedules,Constitution Structure,Common Misconception
landmark_case_1: Constitution of India Bare Text
lc1_holding_summary: Indian Constitution has 12 Schedules — a commonly tested UPSC fact
landmark_case_2: Constitutional Reference
lc2_holding_summary: Original Constitution had 8 Schedules; four more added through amendments
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: States Reorganisation — 7th Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (Seventh Amendment) Act 1956 reorganised the States on linguistic basis following the States Reorganisation Commission Report (Fazl Ali Commission). It abolished the four-tier classification of states (Parts A, B, C, D) and created a two-tier system of States and Union Territories
simplified_explanation: The most significant territorial reorganisation of independent India. Abolished the distinction between former princely state territories and British India territories. Created linguistic states as recommended by SRC 1955. Merged Part B states (Hyderabad, Mysore, etc.) and reorganised boundaries. Total states went from 14 Part A + 8 Part B to a unified system.
keywords: States Reorganisation,Linguistic States,SRC,Fazl Ali Commission,7th Amendment,Part A B C D States
landmark_case_1: Babulal Parate v. State of Bombay
lc1_holding_summary: Parliament's power to reorganise states under Article 3 is plenary; state legislature's view is not binding
landmark_case_2: Berubari Union Case
lc2_holding_summary: Cession of territory requires constitutional amendment; executive action alone is insufficient
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Parliament's Power to Amend — 24th Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (Twenty-Fourth Amendment) Act 1971 amended Article 13 and Article 368 to make it clear that Parliament has power to amend any provision of the Constitution including Fundamental Rights, and to provide that the President shall give assent to Constitutional Amendment Bills
simplified_explanation: Passed in direct response to Golak Nath v. State of Punjab (1967) which held Parliament could not amend Fundamental Rights. The 24th Amendment overruled Golak Nath by making explicit that Article 368 is Parliament's constituent power, not ordinary legislative power, and hence Article 13 does not apply to constitutional amendments.
keywords: 24th Amendment,Constitutional Amendment Power,Article 368,Constituent Power,Golak Nath
landmark_case_1: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
lc1_holding_summary: While upholding Parliament's power to amend under 24th Amendment, SC held basic structure cannot be amended
landmark_case_2: Golak Nath v. State of Punjab
lc2_holding_summary: Overruled by 24th Amendment; Parliament cannot amend Fundamental Rights was the holding — later reversed
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Right to Property and Compensation — 25th Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (Twenty-Fifth Amendment) Act 1971 amended Article 31 (Right to Property) to substitute 'amount' for 'compensation', meaning the law need only provide an 'amount' for acquisition which may not necessarily be market value, and also inserted Article 31C to protect laws implementing Articles 39(b) and 39(c)
simplified_explanation: Article 31C shielded laws implementing Articles 39(b) and (c) — equitable distribution of material resources — from challenge under Articles 14, 19, and 31. This was a major step toward socialist economic policies. In Kesavananda Bharati, the SC struck down the part of 31C that excluded judicial review.
keywords: 25th Amendment,Right to Property,Compensation,Article 31C,Equitable Distribution,39(b)(c)
landmark_case_1: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
lc1_holding_summary: Part of Article 31C excluding judicial review struck down; Parliament cannot totally exclude courts
landmark_case_2: Minerva Mills v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Expanded 31C inserted by 42nd Amendment struck down; original 31C partially valid
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Abolition of Privy Purses — 26th Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (Twenty-Sixth Amendment) Act 1971 abolished the privy purses and privileges of the Rulers of former Indian States by omitting Articles 291 and 362 from the Constitution
simplified_explanation: After the merger of princely states, former rulers were guaranteed privy purses (annual payments) and privileges. Indira Gandhi's government abolished these through the 26th Amendment — a populist measure. Earlier, a Presidential Order derecognizing the Rulers had been struck down by the SC in 1971 as unconstitutional.
keywords: Privy Purses,Former Rulers,Princely States,26th Amendment,Derecognition
landmark_case_1: Madhav Rao Scindia v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Presidential Order derecognizing rulers without Parliament approval struck down — led to 26th Amendment
landmark_case_2: Raghunathrao Ganpatrao v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: 26th Amendment abolishing privy purses is a valid exercise of constituent power
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Election Disputes — Emergency Era — 39th Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (Thirty-Ninth Amendment) Act 1975 placed the election disputes of the President, Vice-President, Prime Minister and Speaker in a newly inserted Article 329A beyond judicial review
simplified_explanation: Passed during the Emergency specifically to nullify the Allahabad High Court judgment setting aside Indira Gandhi's election to Parliament on grounds of corrupt practices. The Supreme Court in Indira Gandhi v. Raj Narain (1975) struck down the relevant clause as violating the basic structure (free and fair elections, judicial review, rule of law).
keywords: 39th Amendment,Election Dispute,Emergency,Indira Gandhi,Allahabad HC,Raj Narain
landmark_case_1: Indira Nehru Gandhi v. Raj Narain
lc1_holding_summary: Retroactive validation of PM's election by 39th Amendment struck down as it destroyed basic structure of free and fair elections
landmark_case_2: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
lc2_holding_summary: Basic structure limits Parliament's amending power — applied by Indira Gandhi case to electoral matters
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice

title: Anti-Defection — 52nd Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (Fifty-Second Amendment) Act 1985 added the Tenth Schedule to the Constitution providing for disqualification of members of Parliament and State Legislatures on grounds of defection from their political party
simplified_explanation: Enacted to curb the menace of political defections ('Aaya Ram Gaya Ram' phenomenon from 1967). Added the Tenth Schedule. Originally permitted split (1/3 members) as exception — deleted by 91st Amendment 2003. Kihoto Hollohan case (1992) upheld the 10th Schedule as constitutional.
keywords: 52nd Amendment,Anti-Defection,Tenth Schedule,Aaya Ram Gaya Ram,Split,Merger
landmark_case_1: Kihoto Hollohan v. Zachillhu
lc1_holding_summary: 10th Schedule is constitutionally valid; Speaker's decision is judicially reviewable on limited grounds
landmark_case_2: G. Viswanathan v. Speaker Tamil Nadu
lc2_holding_summary: Joining another party after voluntary resignation from original party amounts to defection
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Voting Age Reduction — 61st Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (Sixty-First Amendment) Act 1988 reduced the minimum age for voting in elections to the House of the People and Legislative Assemblies of States from 21 years to 18 years by amending Article 326
simplified_explanation: Brought approximately 3.5 crore new voters into the electoral fold. Based on the rationale that persons who are old enough to bear arms for the country (18 years for military service) should have the right to vote. India joins most democracies in setting voting age at 18.
keywords: 61st Amendment,Voting Age,18 Years,Universal Suffrage,Youth Voters
landmark_case_1: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Voting age reduction reflects expanding democratic participation — context for wider reservation discussions
landmark_case_2: Election Commission of India Reference
lc2_holding_summary: Implementation of reduced voting age required complete revision of electoral rolls
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Delhi as National Capital Territory — 69th Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (Sixty-Ninth Amendment) Act 1991 designated Delhi as the National Capital Territory (NCT) and inserted Articles 239AA and 239AB providing for a Legislative Assembly and Council of Ministers for Delhi, with special provisions preserving Central government's control over certain matters
simplified_explanation: Made Delhi unique among UTs — it has an elected legislature and government but with restrictions: Parliament can make laws for Delhi on all matters, Delhi Legislative Assembly cannot legislate on Public Order, Police, and Land. The LG is the administrator and LG-CM conflicts have been subject of major SC rulings.
keywords: Delhi NCT,69th Amendment,Article 239AA,LG,Chief Minister,National Capital Territory
landmark_case_1: Government of NCT Delhi v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Delhi's elected government has primacy in governance; LG must act on Cabinet's advice except in specified matters
landmark_case_2: Government of NCT Delhi v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Services under Delhi government's control — SC gave power to Delhi; later Parliament amended Constitution
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Home Affairs

title: Panchayati Raj — 73rd Amendment Details
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (Seventy-Third Amendment) Act 1992 inserted Part IX (Articles 243 to 243O) and the Eleventh Schedule. It mandates three-tier Panchayati Raj, 5-year terms, reservations for SC/ST/Women, State Election Commission, and State Finance Commission
simplified_explanation: Constitutionalised local self-government for the first time. Key features: (1) Three-tier structure; (2) Gram Sabha as foundation; (3) Mandatory elections every 5 years; (4) Reservation — 1/3 women, proportional SC/ST; (5) State Finance Commission for financial devolution; (6) State Election Commission for free elections; (7) District Planning Committee.
keywords: 73rd Amendment,Panchayati Raj,Part IX,Article 243,Three Tier,Gram Sabha,State Finance Commission,State Election Commission
landmark_case_1: K. Krishna Murthy v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: OBC reservation in Panchayats valid subject to triple test
landmark_case_2: Suresh Mahajan v. State of MP
lc2_holding_summary: Triple test for OBC reservation in panchayats: commission study, quantifiable data, 50% cap
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice

title: Tamil Nadu Reservations — 76th Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (Seventy-Sixth Amendment) Act 1994 placed the Tamil Nadu Backward Classes, Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Reservation of Seats in Educational Institutions and of Appointments or Posts in Services under the State) Act 1993 in the Ninth Schedule, thus protecting Tamil Nadu's 69% reservation from judicial challenge
simplified_explanation: Tamil Nadu's 69% reservation (50% OBC + 19% SC/ST) far exceeded the 50% cap laid down in Indra Sawhney (1992). The State placed the law in the Ninth Schedule to shield it from invalidation. Post IR Coelho (2007), laws added to Ninth Schedule after April 1973 are subject to basic structure review.
keywords: Tamil Nadu,69% Reservation,Ninth Schedule,76th Amendment,Indra Sawhney
landmark_case_1: I.R. Coelho v. State of Tamil Nadu
lc1_holding_summary: Tamil Nadu 69% reservation law in Ninth Schedule subject to basic structure review
landmark_case_2: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: 50% cap on reservations is a constitutional requirement under Article 16
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: SC/ST Reservation in Promotion — 77th Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (Seventy-Seventh Amendment) Act 1995 inserted clause (4A) in Article 16 enabling the State to make provisions for reservation in matters of promotion in favour of Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes if they are not adequately represented in services under the State
simplified_explanation: Passed to overcome the Supreme Court's ruling in Indra Sawhney that there can be no reservation in promotions. Article 16(4A) was later amended by 85th Amendment (2001) to also provide for consequential seniority in promotion. M. Nagaraj case (2006) added conditions — quantifiable data, backwardness, inadequate representation.
keywords: SC ST Reservation Promotion,16(4A),77th Amendment,M Nagaraj,Quantifiable Data
landmark_case_1: M. Nagaraj v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: State must have quantifiable data on backwardness, inadequacy of representation before giving SC/ST reservation in promotion
landmark_case_2: Jarnail Singh v. Lachhmi Narain Gupta
lc2_holding_summary: Creamy layer concept applies to SC/ST reservation in promotion; overruled requirement of overall efficiency data
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Right to Education — 86th Amendment Details
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (Eighty-Sixth Amendment) Act 2002 inserted Article 21A (free and compulsory education for 6-14 years as Fundamental Right), amended Article 45 (early childhood care for under-6), and added clause (k) to Article 51A (parental duty to provide education to children aged 6-14)
simplified_explanation: Implemented after decade-long advocacy. The RTE Act 2009 gives effect to Article 21A mandating 25% reservation for disadvantaged children in private unaided schools, no detention policy (up to Class 8), child-friendly teaching, and RTE norms for school infrastructure. The no-detention policy was later amended in 2019 allowing detention from Class 5.
keywords: 86th Amendment,Article 21A,RTE Act,Right to Education,25% Reservation,No Detention
landmark_case_1: Society for Unaided Private Schools of Rajasthan v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: RTE Act 25% reservation in private unaided schools is constitutionally valid
landmark_case_2: Ashok Kumar Thakur v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Right to education under Article 21A is a Fundamental Right enforceable against State and aided institutions
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Council of Ministers Size and Anti-Defection — 91st Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (Ninety-First Amendment) Act 2003 amended Article 75 to limit the size of the Council of Ministers at the Centre and in States to 15% of the total strength of the Lower House (minimum 12 ministers), and amended the Tenth Schedule to delete the provision allowing exemption for a party split (originally 1/3 members)
simplified_explanation: Two major changes: (1) Cabinet size cap at 15% of Lok Sabha/Assembly strength prevents oversized cabinets and expenditure waste; (2) Deletion of 'split' exemption from anti-defection law — now only a merger (2/3 members into another party) is permitted. This significantly tightened anti-defection provisions.
keywords: 91st Amendment,Cabinet Size,15% Cap,Anti-Defection,Split Deleted,Merger,10th Schedule
landmark_case_1: Subhash Desai v. Principal Secretary Governor of Maharashtra
lc1_holding_summary: After 91st Amendment, split of 1/3 members is no longer a valid defence against disqualification; only merger of 2/3 saves from disqualification
landmark_case_2: Kihoto Hollohan v. Zachillhu
lc2_holding_summary: Pre-91st Amendment anti-defection provisions upheld; 91st Amendment further strengthened the law
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Co-operative Societies — 97th Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (Ninety-Seventh Amendment) Act 2011 inserted Article 43B (DPSP for promotion of co-operatives) and Part IX-B (Articles 243ZH to 243ZT) dealing with co-operative societies, providing for democratic elections, audit, and autonomy of co-operatives
simplified_explanation: Aimed to provide constitutional protection to co-operative societies from political interference. However, the Supreme Court in 2021 struck down Part IX-B (except in relation to multi-state co-operatives) as unconstitutional because the amendment was not ratified by at least half the state legislatures as required under Article 368(2) proviso.
keywords: 97th Amendment,Co-operative Societies,Part IX-B,Article 43B,State Ratification
landmark_case_1: Union of India v. Rajendra N. Shah
lc1_holding_summary: Part IX-B of 97th Amendment struck down — required state ratification under Article 368 which was not done
landmark_case_2: Harnek Singh v. State of Punjab
lc2_holding_summary: Co-operative societies have right to autonomous functioning; state interference must be minimal
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Land Boundary Agreement with Bangladesh — 100th Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (One Hundredth Amendment) Act 2015 gave effect to the Land Boundary Agreement (LBA) signed between India and Bangladesh in 1974 and the Protocol of 2011, involving exchange of enclaves and adverse possessions between the two countries
simplified_explanation: The LBA settled the complex enclave issue — 111 Indian enclaves in Bangladesh and 51 Bangladeshi enclaves in India — residents being stateless for decades. Under the 100th Amendment, India transferred some territories to Bangladesh and acquired others. Required constitutional amendment as it involved cession of territory (Berubari Union case principle).
keywords: 100th Amendment,Bangladesh,LBA,Land Boundary Agreement,Enclaves,Territory Exchange
landmark_case_1: In Re Berubari Union
lc1_holding_summary: Territory cession requires constitutional amendment under Article 368 — applied to Bangladesh LBA
landmark_case_2: State of Assam v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Constitutional amendment required for any territorial cession to foreign country
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: GST — 101st Amendment Details
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (One Hundred and First Amendment) Act 2016 inserted Article 246A (concurrent power to levy GST), Article 269A (levy and collection of GST in inter-state trade), Article 279A (GST Council), and amended Articles 248, 249, 250, 268, 269, 270, 271, 286, 366, and 368
simplified_explanation: The most significant fiscal federalism reform. The GST Council (Article 279A) consists of Union Finance Minister (Chair), Union Minister of State for Finance, and Finance Ministers of all States. Decisions by 3/4 weighted majority (Centre has 1/3 weightage, States have 2/3 weightage collectively). Replaced over 17 Central and State indirect taxes.
keywords: 101st Amendment,GST,Article 246A,Article 279A,GST Council,Indirect Tax Reform,Fiscal Federalism
landmark_case_1: Union of India v. Mohit Mineral Pvt Ltd
lc1_holding_summary: GST Council recommendations are persuasive but not binding; Centre and States retain independent taxing power
landmark_case_2: Unicorn Industries v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: GST constitutional structure preserves federal principles while achieving economic integration
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

title: EWS Reservation — 103rd Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (One Hundred and Third Amendment) Act 2019 inserted clauses (6) in Articles 15 and 16 to enable the State to make special provisions for the advancement of Economically Weaker Sections (EWS) of citizens — up to 10% reservation in educational institutions and public employment — for those not covered by existing reservation schemes
simplified_explanation: First reservation for upper castes (General Category below income threshold of Rs 8 lakh per annum). Challenged as violating basic structure by crossing 50% cap (now 60% with EWS) and by extending reservation to non-backward classes. Supreme Court upheld the amendment in November 2022 by a 3:2 majority.
keywords: 103rd Amendment,EWS Reservation,10%,Economically Weaker Sections,50% Cap,Upper Caste
landmark_case_1: Janhit Abhiyan v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: 103rd Amendment upheld by 3:2 majority; EWS reservation does not violate basic structure; 50% cap is a rule not a ceiling
landmark_case_2: Neil Aurelio Nunes v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: EWS reservation in NEET admissions is valid; exclusion of OBC/SC/ST from EWS category is not discriminatory
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Extension of SC/ST Reservation in Legislature — 104th Amendment
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (One Hundred and Fourth Amendment) Act 2020 extended the reservation of seats for SCs and STs in the Lok Sabha and State Assemblies by 10 years (from 2020 to 2030) and removed the reservation for the Anglo-Indian community in Parliament and State Legislatures
simplified_explanation: Reservations for SC/ST in legislature (Articles 330 and 332) have been renewed every 10 years since 1950. The 104th Amendment continued this practice. It also ended the nominated Anglo-Indian seats in Parliament and State Assemblies — a colonial legacy that had been continued since 1950.
keywords: 104th Amendment,SC ST Legislative Reservation,Anglo-Indian,Extension,Article 330,Article 332
landmark_case_1: Constitutional Reference on SC ST Reservation
lc1_holding_summary: Each 10-year extension of legislative reservation must be reviewed for continued necessity
landmark_case_2: Constituent Assembly Debates Reference
lc2_holding_summary: Anglo-Indian reservation was intended to be temporary; removal after 70 years is constitutionally appropriate
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Union Public Service Commission
verbatim_text_excerpt: A Public Service Commission shall be established for the Union and a Public Service Commission shall be established for each group of States specified in Part A of the First Schedule. The members of a Commission shall be appointed by the President in the case of the Union Commission
simplified_explanation: UPSC is the central recruiting agency for Group A and Group B Central Government services including IAS, IPS, IFS, and other Central Services. Conducts the Civil Services Examination, Combined Medical Services, Engineering Services, etc. Members have security of tenure similar to judges. Reports to Parliament through President.
keywords: UPSC,Civil Services,Recruitment,Constitutional Body,Article 315,IAS,IPS
landmark_case_1: S.B. Mathur v. Chief Justice of India
lc1_holding_summary: UPSC members have constitutional protection of tenure; removal only through presidential reference to SC
landmark_case_2: All India Services (Conduct) Rules Reference
lc2_holding_summary: UPSC's recommendations are generally binding; government departure requires recorded reasons
nodal_ministry: Not applicable (constitutional body)

title: Finance Commission — Composition and Functions
verbatim_text_excerpt: The President shall constitute the Finance Commission every five years. It shall consist of a Chairman and four other members. The Finance Commission shall make recommendations as to the distribution between the Union and the States of the net proceeds of taxes; the principles which should govern the grants-in-aid to the States
simplified_explanation: Finance Commission determines Centre-State fiscal relations. Key functions: (1) Vertical devolution — share of Central taxes to States (currently 41% as per 15th FC); (2) Horizontal devolution — distribution among states (based on population, area, income distance, forest cover, demographic performance); (3) Grants-in-aid. FC is a quasi-judicial constitutional body.
keywords: Finance Commission,15th Finance Commission,Devolution,Vertical,Horizontal,Grants,Article 280
landmark_case_1: State of West Bengal v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Finance Commission's role in federal fiscal relations is crucial; Centre must follow its recommendations in spirit
landmark_case_2: Kerala v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: State challenged unilateral Centre decisions bypassing Finance Commission framework on fiscal transfers
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

title: Inter-State Council
verbatim_text_excerpt: If it appears to the President that the public interests would be served by the establishment of a Council charged with the duty of inquiring into and advising upon disputes which have arisen between States; or investigating and discussing subjects in which some or all of the States, or the Union and one or more of the States, have a common interest
simplified_explanation: The Inter-State Council (ISC) established in 1990 under Article 263 is a constitutional forum for Centre-State and inter-State coordination. Chaired by PM with all CMs and some Union Cabinet Ministers as members. Meetings are rare in practice. Sarkaria Commission and Punchhi Commission recommended strengthening of ISC.
keywords: Inter-State Council,Article 263,Centre-State Coordination,Cooperative Federalism,Sarkaria Commission
landmark_case_1: State of Rajasthan v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Need for consultation between Centre and States on matters of common interest; ISC is the constitutional mechanism
landmark_case_2: Punchhi Commission Report
lc2_holding_summary: Inter-State Council should meet regularly and be strengthened as the premier Centre-State forum
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Public Service Commissions — Constitutional Protections
verbatim_text_excerpt: A member of a Public Service Commission shall only be removed from office by order of the President on the ground of misbehaviour after the Supreme Court, on reference being made to it by the President, has, on inquiry held in accordance with the procedure prescribed, reported that the member ought on any such ground to be removed
simplified_explanation: PSC members have strong tenure protection — they cannot be removed except through a SC inquiry — similar to judges. They cannot hold any other government position after serving on the Commission. This independence is crucial for merit-based public service recruitment free from political interference.
keywords: PSC,Public Service Commission,Security of Tenure,Independence,Merit,Article 317
landmark_case_1: Moti Ram v. Chief Justice
lc1_holding_summary: PSC's constitutional independence is essential for meritocratic civil services
landmark_case_2: State of Punjab v. Salil Sabhlok
lc2_holding_summary: CAT judgments on PSC recommendations must uphold merit principle and PSC's constitutional independence
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: National Commission for Scheduled Castes
verbatim_text_excerpt: There shall be a Commission for the Scheduled Castes to be known as the National Commission for Scheduled Castes. The Chairperson, Vice-Chairperson and each member of the Commission shall be appointed by the President by warrant under his hand and seal
simplified_explanation: The NCSC (National Commission for Scheduled Castes) is a constitutional body established by the 89th Amendment 2003. It investigates and monitors safeguards for SCs, inquires into specific complaints, participates in planning for SC development, and submits annual reports to the President. Its predecessor body covered both SC and ST.
keywords: NCSC,Scheduled Castes,Article 338,89th Amendment,Constitutional Body,Safeguards
landmark_case_1: National Commission for Scheduled Castes v. State of Andhra Pradesh
lc1_holding_summary: NCSC's findings on violation of SC safeguards must be taken seriously; State must provide action taken report
landmark_case_2: K.C. Vasanth Kumar v. State of Karnataka
lc2_holding_summary: Pre-NCSC case; constitutional provisions for SC protection require active monitoring
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: National Commission for Scheduled Tribes
verbatim_text_excerpt: There shall be a Commission for the Scheduled Tribes to be known as the National Commission for Scheduled Tribes. The Commission shall consist of a Chairperson, Vice-Chairperson and three other Members
simplified_explanation: Established by 89th Amendment 2003 by bifurcating the earlier combined National Commission for SC and ST. The NCST monitors safeguards under Fifth and Sixth Schedules, investigates complaints of atrocities against STs, reviews tribal land alienation, displacement due to development projects, and functioning of Tribal Sub-Plan.
keywords: NCST,Scheduled Tribes,Article 338A,89th Amendment,Fifth Schedule,Sixth Schedule
landmark_case_1: Nandini Sundar v. State of Chhattisgarh
lc1_holding_summary: Constitutional bodies like NCST must be empowered to protect tribal rights; their recommendations cannot be ignored
landmark_case_2: Samatha v. State of AP
lc2_holding_summary: Tribal land rights are constitutional; NCST has duty to prevent tribal land alienation
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Control of Union over Scheduled Areas
verbatim_text_excerpt: The President may at any time and shall, at the expiration of ten years from the commencement of this Constitution by order appoint a Commission to report on the administration of the Scheduled Areas and the welfare of the Scheduled Tribes in the States
simplified_explanation: This provision enabled the Dhebar Commission (1960-61), the first Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes Commission. Subsequent periodic reviews have been conducted. The findings guide revisions of Tribal Sub-Plans and implementation of Fifth and Sixth Schedule protections.
keywords: Scheduled Areas,Tribal Welfare,Article 339,Dhebar Commission,Presidential Order
landmark_case_1: Bhuria Committee Report
lc1_holding_summary: Review leading to PESA 1996 — tribal self-governance for Scheduled Areas
landmark_case_2: Xaxa Committee Report
lc2_holding_summary: Most comprehensive recent review of tribal situation in India; recommended Constitutional protection for tribal land rights
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Appointment of Commission to investigate conditions of backward classes
verbatim_text_excerpt: The President may by order appoint a Commission consisting of such persons as he thinks fit to investigate the conditions of socially and educationally backward classes within the territory of India and the difficulties under which they labour and to make recommendations as to the steps that should be taken by the Union or any State to remove such difficulties
simplified_explanation: Two landmark OBC Commissions: (1) Kaka Kalelkar Commission (1955) — First OBC Commission; recommendations not fully implemented; (2) Mandal Commission (1980) — recommended 27% reservation for OBCs; implemented in 1990 by V.P. Singh government; led to Indra Sawhney case.
keywords: Article 340,OBC Commission,Kaka Kalelkar,Mandal Commission,27% Reservation,Backward Classes
landmark_case_1: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: OBC 27% reservation upheld with 50% total cap; creamy layer must be excluded; no reservation in promotion for OBCs
landmark_case_2: M.R. Balaji v. State of Mysore
lc2_holding_summary: Pre-Mandal case; 68% reservation struck down as exceeding reasonable limit
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Official Language of the Union
verbatim_text_excerpt: The official language of the Union shall be Hindi in Devanagari script. The form of numerals to be used for the official purposes of the Union shall be the international form of Indian numerals
simplified_explanation: Hindi in Devanagari script is the official language for Union purposes. English continues as an associate official language under the Official Languages Act 1963 (indefinitely, not for 15 years as originally contemplated). The 8th Schedule lists 22 scheduled languages. English is not in the 8th Schedule but is official under a statute.
keywords: Official Language,Hindi,Devanagari,English,8th Schedule,22 Languages
landmark_case_1: Tamilnadu v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Hindi imposition must follow constitutional safeguards; other languages have equal constitutional status
landmark_case_2: Raja Ram Pal v. Speaker
lc2_holding_summary: Parliamentary debates can be conducted in any scheduled language; Official Languages Act governs translation
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Official Languages Commission and Committee of Parliament
verbatim_text_excerpt: There shall be constituted a Commission on the Official Languages of the Union, the Commission to make a report on the progressive use of Hindi for the official purposes of the Union
simplified_explanation: The Official Languages Commission (first constituted in 1955) reviews the use of Hindi in government. Its report is considered by a Parliamentary Committee. The Official Languages Act 1963 and Official Languages Rules 1976 govern use of languages. Three-language formula for schools was recommended by the Kothari Commission.
keywords: Official Languages Commission,Hindi Promotion,Official Languages Act,Three Language Formula,Parliamentary Committee
landmark_case_1: State of Madras v. Champakam Dorairajan
lc1_holding_summary: Language rights are constitutional; state cannot impose a language for educational purposes against Article 29
landmark_case_2: Hindi Implementation Committee Reference
lc2_holding_summary: Anti-Hindi agitations in Tamil Nadu led to continued use of English as associate official language
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Facilities for instruction in mother-tongue at primary stage
verbatim_text_excerpt: It shall be the endeavour of every State and of every local authority within the State to provide adequate facilities for instruction in the mother-tongue at the primary stage of education to children belonging to linguistic minority groups
simplified_explanation: Every state must try to provide primary education in the mother tongue of linguistic minority children. This implements the constitutional principle that linguistic minorities have a right to their language and culture. Implemented through Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan guidelines for linguistic minority children.
keywords: Mother Tongue,Linguistic Minority,Primary Education,Article 350A,Language Rights
landmark_case_1: DAV College v. State of Punjab
lc1_holding_summary: Linguistic minority educational rights are protected; State cannot impose medium of instruction
landmark_case_2: State of Karnataka v. Associated Management of English Medium Primary and Secondary Schools
lc2_holding_summary: Medium of instruction in schools is a sensitive issue; mother tongue instruction rights of minorities protected
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Subject-matter of laws made by Parliament and by Legislatures of States
verbatim_text_excerpt: Notwithstanding anything in clauses (2) and (3), Parliament has exclusive power to make laws with respect to any of the matters enumerated in List I in the Seventh Schedule to this Constitution. Notwithstanding anything in clause (3), Parliament, and subject to clause (1), the Legislature of any State also, have power to make laws with respect to any of the matters enumerated in List III
simplified_explanation: Article 246 is the central provision for distribution of legislative powers. Parliament is supreme for Union List (97 subjects). State Legislature for State List (66 subjects). Both for Concurrent List (47 subjects), with Parliament prevailing in case of conflict. Residuary power with Parliament under Article 248.
keywords: Legislative Powers,Union List,State List,Concurrent List,Article 246,Residuary Power
landmark_case_1: Prafulla Kumar Mukherjee v. Bank of Commerce
lc1_holding_summary: Pith and substance doctrine — if a law's true nature falls in one list, incidental encroachment on another list is valid
landmark_case_2: ITC Ltd v. Agricultural Produce Market Committee
lc2_holding_summary: Article 246 distribution is not watertight; liberal interpretation to avoid deadlock preferred
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Power of Parliament to legislate on State List in national interest
verbatim_text_excerpt: Notwithstanding anything in article 246, if the Council of States has declared by resolution supported by not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting that it is necessary or expedient in the national interest that Parliament should make laws with respect to any matter enumerated in the State List, it shall be lawful for Parliament to make laws for the whole or any part of the territory of India with respect to that matter
simplified_explanation: Rajya Sabha can by 2/3 majority empower Parliament to legislate on a State List subject in national interest. This law operates for 1 year (renewable). This is a cooperative federalism mechanism that allows Centre to address national concerns even on state subjects. Such laws do not amend the Constitution.
keywords: Article 249,National Interest,State List,Rajya Sabha Resolution,2/3 Majority,Centre-State
landmark_case_1: State of Rajasthan v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Parliament's power to legislate on state subjects under Articles 249, 250, 252 is exceptional and bounded
landmark_case_2: Constitutional Reference on Centre-State Legislative Conflict
lc2_holding_summary: Article 249 cannot be used as a routine device to override state legislative autonomy
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Power of Parliament to legislate for two or more States by consent and adoption of such legislation by any other State
verbatim_text_excerpt: If it appears to the Legislatures of two or more States to be desirable that any of the matters with respect to which Parliament has no power to make laws for the States except as provided in articles 249 and 250 should be regulated in those States by Parliament by law, and if resolutions to that effect are passed by all the Houses of the Legislatures of those States, it shall be lawful for Parliament to pass an Act for regulating that matter accordingly
simplified_explanation: If two or more states pass resolutions requesting Parliament to legislate on a State List subject, Parliament can do so and the law applies to those states. Other states can later adopt the law by passing a resolution. This provision was used for Wildlife Protection Act 1972 and Water Prevention and Control of Pollution Act 1974.
keywords: Article 252,State Consent,Parliamentary Legislation,Wildlife Protection Act,Pollution Control
landmark_case_1: Wildlife (Protection) Act Reference
lc1_holding_summary: Wildlife Protection Act 1972 was enacted under Article 252 after states passed resolutions requesting Parliamentary legislation
landmark_case_2: Water Prevention Control Pollution Act Reference
lc2_holding_summary: Water pollution being a State List subject, states requested Parliament to enact uniform law under Article 252
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Legislation for giving effect to international agreements
verbatim_text_excerpt: Notwithstanding anything in the foregoing provisions of this Chapter, Parliament has power to make any law for the whole or any part of the territory of India for implementing any treaty, agreement or convention with any foreign country or countries or any decision made at any international conference, association or other body
simplified_explanation: Parliament can legislate on any subject (even State List subjects) to implement international treaties, agreements, and obligations. Used for environmental laws (Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, Hazardous Waste Rules), Patent (Amendment) Act 1999 (TRIPS compliance), and Biological Diversity Act 2002 (CBD implementation).
keywords: Article 253,International Treaties,Treaty Implementation,State List,Parliament,TRIPS,CBD
landmark_case_1: State of Bihar v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Parliament's power under Article 253 to implement treaties even on State List subjects is broad
landmark_case_2: Bajaj Auto v. TVS Motor Company
lc2_holding_summary: Patent law enacted under Article 253 for TRIPS compliance binds states and individuals
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

title: Inconsistency between laws made by Parliament and laws made by the Legislatures of States
verbatim_text_excerpt: If any provision of a law made by the Legislature of a State is repugnant to any provision of a law made by Parliament which Parliament is competent to enact, or to any provision of an existing law with respect to one of the matters enumerated in the Concurrent List, then, subject to the provisions of clause (2), the law made by Parliament, whether passed before or after the law made by the Legislature of such State, shall prevail
simplified_explanation: In the Concurrent List, Parliament's law prevails over State law in case of conflict. Exception: If the State law receives Presidential assent and Parliament had not expressly excluded state legislation, the State law prevails in that state. This conditional overriding protects cooperative federalism.
keywords: Article 254,Repugnancy,Concurrent List,Presidential Assent,Parliamentary Supremacy
landmark_case_1: M. Karunanidhi v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Parliamentary law on Concurrent List subject prevails over state law; repugnancy determined by direct conflict test
landmark_case_2: Deep Chand v. State of UP
lc2_holding_summary: Repugnancy under Article 254: test is whether state law trenches upon Parliament's law — both direct and indirect inconsistency
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Obligation of States and the Union
verbatim_text_excerpt: The executive power of every State shall be so exercised as to ensure compliance with the laws made by Parliament and any existing laws which apply in that State, and the executive power of the Union shall extend to the giving of such directions to a State as may appear to the Government of India to be necessary for that purpose
simplified_explanation: The Centre can give directions to States to ensure compliance with Parliamentary laws. If a State fails to comply with Central directions, it can be a ground for invoking Article 356 (President's Rule). This is one of the unitary features of Indian federalism — Centre's executive superiority over States.
keywords: Article 256,Centre-State Executive Relations,Directions,Compliance,Parliamentary Law
landmark_case_1: State of Rajasthan v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Centre's power to give directions under Articles 256 and 257 is wide but must be for legitimate constitutional purpose
landmark_case_2: Sarbananda Sonowal v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: State's failure to comply with Central directions on illegal immigration is a valid ground for Central intervention
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Control of the Union over States in certain cases
verbatim_text_excerpt: The executive power of every State shall be so exercised as not to impede or prejudice the exercise of the executive power of the Union, and the executive power of the Union shall extend to the giving of such directions to a State as may appear to the Government of India to be necessary for that purpose
simplified_explanation: The Centre can give directions to States for: (1) construction and maintenance of communications declared to be of national or military importance; (2) protection of railways within States. States cannot obstruct Central infrastructure. This article forms the basis for National Highway construction directives to States.
keywords: Article 257,National Infrastructure,Railway Protection,Central Directions,Highway Construction
landmark_case_1: Indian Railways v. State of UP
lc1_holding_summary: States must cooperate with Central railway infrastructure; Article 257 directions are binding on States
landmark_case_2: National Highway Authority of India v. State of Goa
lc2_holding_summary: Centre's directions for national highway construction under Article 257 override state objections on limited grounds
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Taxes not to be imposed save by authority of law
verbatim_text_excerpt: No tax shall be levied or collected except by authority of law
simplified_explanation: The foundational principle of taxation — no taxation without parliamentary/legislative authority. Executive cannot impose taxes by mere notification or executive order. Every tax must be supported by specific legislation. This principle prevents arbitrary or illegal taxation.
keywords: Taxation,No Tax Without Law,Article 265,Tax Authority,Parliamentary Approval
landmark_case_1: Kewal Krishan Puri v. State of Punjab
lc1_holding_summary: Every element of tax — subject, rate, and procedure — must be prescribed by law; no executive discretion in taxation
landmark_case_2: Commissioner of Sales Tax v. Radhakrishan
lc2_holding_summary: Article 265 requires that both levy and collection of tax must have legislative authority
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

title: Consolidated Funds and public accounts of India and of the States
verbatim_text_excerpt: Moneys received by or on behalf of the Government of India shall be credited to a Consolidated Fund. Other public money received by or on behalf of the Government of India shall be credited to the Public Account of India
simplified_explanation: All government revenues go into the Consolidated Fund of India — the main government account. No money can be withdrawn from Consolidated Fund without appropriation by Parliament. The Public Account holds provident fund, small savings, and similar deposits. The Contingency Fund is for unforeseen expenses (Rs 500 crore).
keywords: Consolidated Fund,Public Account,Contingency Fund,Article 266,Government Finance
landmark_case_1: Comptroller and Auditor General of India v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Expenditure from Consolidated Fund requires parliamentary appropriation; CAG audits all such expenditure
landmark_case_2: State of UP v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Article 266 creates a clear framework for public finances; all receipts must go into Consolidated Fund
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

title: Grants from Union to certain States
verbatim_text_excerpt: Such sums as Parliament may by law provide shall be charged on the Consolidated Fund of India in each year as grants-in-aid of the revenues of such States as Parliament may determine to be in need of assistance, and different sums may be fixed for different States
simplified_explanation: Article 275 grants are provided by the Finance Commission's recommendations. These are statutory grants and are charged (not voted) from the Consolidated Fund. They supplement States' own revenues, especially for scheduled tribe welfare and administrative improvements. Distinguished from discretionary grants under Article 282.
keywords: Grants-in-Aid,Article 275,Finance Commission,Statutory Grants,State Revenues
landmark_case_1: State of Kerala v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Finance Commission grants under Article 275 are formula-based; discretionary grants under Article 282 should not replace them
landmark_case_2: Constitutional Reference on Federal Finance
lc2_holding_summary: Balance between statutory grants (Article 275) and discretionary grants (Article 282) is a federal fiscal issue
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

title: Expenditure defrayable by the Union or a State out of its revenues
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Union or a State may make any grants for any public purpose, notwithstanding that the purpose is not one with respect to which Parliament or the Legislature of the State, as the case may be, may make laws
simplified_explanation: Article 282 allows discretionary spending by Centre and States on any public purpose. Centrally Sponsored Schemes (CSS) like MGNREGA, PM Kisan, Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana are funded under Article 282. Critics argue excessive CSS spending erodes fiscal federalism as Centre conditions grants in State subjects.
keywords: Article 282,Centrally Sponsored Schemes,Discretionary Grants,CSS,Fiscal Federalism
landmark_case_1: State of Madhya Pradesh v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Centrally Sponsored Schemes under Article 282 must respect State legislative competence
landmark_case_2: NITI Aayog Reference
lc2_holding_summary: Shift from Planning Commission to NITI Aayog affects distribution of discretionary grants under Article 282
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Freedom of trade, commerce and intercourse
verbatim_text_excerpt: Subject to the other provisions of this Part, trade, commerce and intercourse throughout the territory of India shall be free
simplified_explanation: Freedom of inter-state trade and commerce is a constitutional right. Parliament and State Legislatures can impose reasonable restrictions. State laws restricting inter-state trade require Presidential assent under Article 304(b). This provision prevents internal trade barriers between states — creating a common Indian market.
keywords: Freedom of Trade,Inter-State Commerce,Article 301,Common Market,Trade Barriers
landmark_case_1: Atiabari Tea Co. Ltd v. State of Assam
lc1_holding_summary: Taxes that directly impede the free flow of trade violate Article 301
landmark_case_2: Automobile Transport v. State of Rajasthan
lc2_holding_summary: Regulatory measures (not taxes) that facilitate trade are valid even if they incidentally restrict trade
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Doctrine of Eclipse in Constitutional Law
verbatim_text_excerpt: A pre-constitutional law that is inconsistent with a Fundamental Right is not void but only unenforceable (eclipsed). If the Fundamental Right is subsequently removed or amended, the eclipse is lifted and the law becomes fully operative again. This doctrine applies only to pre-constitutional laws.
simplified_explanation: The doctrine of eclipse was established in Bhikaji Narain v. State of MP (1955). A pre-1950 law inconsistent with Fundamental Rights is dormant/eclipsed but not dead. If the Constitution is amended to remove the conflicting right, or if the law is amended to remove inconsistency, the eclipse is removed. Does NOT apply to post-constitutional laws which are void ab initio.
keywords: Eclipse Doctrine,Pre-Constitutional Law,Inconsistency,Void,Dormant,Fundamental Rights
landmark_case_1: Bhikaji Narain v. State of MP
lc1_holding_summary: Pre-constitutional law is eclipsed (not void) by an inconsistent Fundamental Right; if the right is removed, eclipse lifts
landmark_case_2: Keshavan Madhava Menon v. State of Bombay
lc2_holding_summary: Earlier view — pre-constitutional laws are void; Bhikaji Narain modified this to eclipse doctrine
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Doctrine of Severability (or Separability)
verbatim_text_excerpt: When a part of a statute is unconstitutional and the rest is valid, the court will sever the unconstitutional part and uphold the rest. The test is whether the legislature would have enacted the remaining part without the unconstitutional portion. If they are inseparably mixed, the whole law falls.
simplified_explanation: The severability doctrine ensures that an entire law is not struck down merely because one provision is unconstitutional. The valid portion survives. The invalid portion is severed. Test: Can the law survive without the struck-down portion? Is the valid part workable and meaningful? Used extensively in Fundamental Rights challenges.
keywords: Severability,Constitutional Validity,Partial Invalidity,Striking Down,Inseverable
landmark_case_1: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
lc1_holding_summary: Severability doctrine applied — valid and invalid parts of Preventive Detention Act separated
landmark_case_2: Romesh Thappar v. State of Madras
lc2_holding_summary: When an unconstitutional restriction is inseverable from the rest of the law, the entire law fails
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Doctrine of Waiver of Fundamental Rights
verbatim_text_excerpt: A citizen can waive a personal Fundamental Right. But a right conferred in public interest or for the benefit of the public cannot be waived. A citizen cannot waive rights that primarily benefit the public even if they individually appear to benefit him.
simplified_explanation: The waiver doctrine was discussed in Behram Khurshed Pesikaka v. State of Bombay. A person can voluntarily give up rights meant for their personal benefit (e.g., right to a specific procedure). But rights like freedom of press (Article 19) which serve public interest cannot be waived by the individual because their waiver harms others.
keywords: Waiver,Fundamental Rights,Personal Right,Public Interest,Voluntary Relinquishment
landmark_case_1: Basheshar Nath v. Commissioner of Income Tax
lc1_holding_summary: Fundamental Rights in public interest cannot be waived; right against self-incrimination in public interest cannot be waived by settlement
landmark_case_2: Behram Khurshed Pesikaka v. State of Bombay
lc2_holding_summary: A citizen may waive rights conferred primarily for personal benefit but not rights in which the public has an interest
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Doctrine of Colourable Legislation (Fraud on Constitution)
verbatim_text_excerpt: A legislature cannot do indirectly what it cannot do directly. If a legislature enacts a law which in form falls within its competence but in substance and real effect trenches upon a subject within the exclusive competence of another legislature, the law is colourable legislation and unconstitutional.
simplified_explanation: The doctrine prevents circumvention of constitutional limitations on legislative power. 'What cannot be done directly cannot be done indirectly' (K.C. Gajapati Narayan Deo). If a state labels a law as a land reform measure but actually it is confiscation without compensation, the label cannot save the law.
keywords: Colourable Legislation,Fraud on Constitution,Legislative Competence,Substance over Form,Indirect Legislation
landmark_case_1: K.C. Gajapati Narayan Deo v. State of Orissa
lc1_holding_summary: Court looks at substance not form; colourable legislation is unconstitutional
landmark_case_2: Prafulla Kumar Mukherjee v. Bank of Commerce Ltd
lc2_holding_summary: Pith and substance test: if pith and substance is within competence, incidental encroachment on other list is valid
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Pith and Substance Doctrine
verbatim_text_excerpt: The doctrine of pith and substance is used to determine the true nature and character of legislation to ascertain whether it falls within the legislative competence of the enacting legislature. Courts look at the substance of the legislation, not merely its form or the label attached to it
simplified_explanation: Used primarily to resolve Centre-State legislative competence disputes. A law may be primarily about a Union List subject even if it incidentally affects a State List subject (and vice versa). The law's dominant purpose and effect determines its classification. This prevents technical challenges from paralysing legislative activity.
keywords: Pith and Substance,Legislative Competence,Dominant Purpose,Seventh Schedule,Incidental Encroachment
landmark_case_1: State of Bombay v. F.N. Balsara
lc1_holding_summary: Bombay Prohibition Act's pith and substance is regulation of liquor (State List); its incidental effect on imports does not make it a Union List subject
landmark_case_2: Medically Ill-equipped Workers Union v. State of Tamil Nadu
lc2_holding_summary: When pith and substance of a law is within competence, incidental trenching on another legislature's domain is valid
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Doctrine of Occupied Field / Pre-emption
verbatim_text_excerpt: Where Parliament has made a comprehensive law on a Concurrent List subject, thereby occupying the field, a State cannot make a law on the same subject even if there is no direct conflict between the two laws, unless the State law receives Presidential assent under Article 254(2)
simplified_explanation: The occupied field doctrine prevents States from legislating on Concurrent List subjects already comprehensively covered by Parliament, even if there is no direct inconsistency. If Parliament has occupied the field, the state legislature is excluded from that area unless the State law gets Presidential assent.
keywords: Occupied Field,Pre-emption,Concurrent List,Article 254,Parliamentary Supremacy
landmark_case_1: M. Karunanidhi v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Four tests for repugnancy under Article 254: (1) direct conflict; (2) parliamentary intention to cover the field; (3) occupied field; (4) irreconcilability
landmark_case_2: Zaverbhai Amaidas v. State of Bombay
lc2_holding_summary: When Parliament occupies a field in the Concurrent List, State law on the same subject is repugnant unless Presidential assent obtained
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Doctrine of Territorial Nexus in Taxation
verbatim_text_excerpt: A State can tax transactions or persons outside its territory if there is a sufficient territorial nexus between the taxing State and the transaction or person being taxed. The nexus must be real and proximate, not far-fetched or illusory.
simplified_explanation: States can only make laws for their own territory but taxation is an exception — if there is a territorial nexus with the State, the State can tax persons or transactions outside its borders. E.g., a State can tax income earned outside the State by a company registered and managed there.
keywords: Territorial Nexus,State Taxation,Extra-territorial,Sales Tax,Income Tax
landmark_case_1: State of Bombay v. R.M.D. Chamarbaugwala
lc1_holding_summary: Territorial nexus doctrine: State can tax activities outside its territory if there is sufficient connection with the taxing State
landmark_case_2: Wallace Brothers & Co. Ltd v. Commissioner of Income Tax
lc2_holding_summary: Privy Council established nexus doctrine for extra-territorial taxation; adopted by Indian courts
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

title: Public Interest Litigation (PIL) — Constitutional Development
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Supreme Court has evolved a procedure by which any member of the public can seek judicial redressal for violation of public interest or constitutional rights where the aggrieved person is unable to approach the court. The court relaxes locus standi requirements in such cases.
simplified_explanation: PIL was pioneered by Justice P.N. Bhagwati and Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer in the late 1970s and 1980s. It democratized access to the Supreme Court. Key features: relaxed locus standi, letter petitions treated as writ petitions, appointment of commissioners for fact-finding, continuing mandamus. Covers bonded labour, prisoners' rights, environment, corruption, electoral reforms.
keywords: PIL,Public Interest Litigation,Locus Standi,Article 32,Judicial Activism,Letter Petitions
landmark_case_1: S.P. Gupta v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Locus standi expanded; any public-spirited citizen can move court on behalf of those unable to do so
landmark_case_2: Hussainara Khatoon v. State of Bihar
lc2_holding_summary: Foundational PIL: letter to Chief Justice on undertrial prisoners led to landmark judgments on speedy trial and bail
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

title: Absolute Liability vs Strict Liability
verbatim_text_excerpt: In M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Oleum Gas Leak case, 1987), the Supreme Court evolved the doctrine of Absolute Liability — an enterprise engaged in a hazardous or inherently dangerous activity is absolutely liable for any harm resulting from that activity. There are no exceptions, unlike in the English doctrine of Strict Liability (Rylands v. Fletcher).
simplified_explanation: Absolute Liability differs from Strict Liability: (1) No exceptions like Act of God, act of stranger, or consent of plaintiff; (2) Compensation must be commensurate with enterprise's capacity — the larger and more prosperous the enterprise, the higher the damages; (3) Applies to industries using hazardous substances or carrying on hazardous activities.
keywords: Absolute Liability,Strict Liability,Hazardous Industry,M.C. Mehta,Polluter Pays,Compensation
landmark_case_1: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Oleum Gas Leak)
lc1_holding_summary: Absolute Liability doctrine — hazardous enterprise liable for all harm without any exceptions; compensation linked to enterprise's size
landmark_case_2: Shriram Food and Fertilisers Case
lc2_holding_summary: Background case leading to Absolute Liability evolution; Bhopal Gas Tragedy context also key
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Constitutional Morality vs Social Morality
verbatim_text_excerpt: Constitutional morality refers to adherence to the norms, values, and spirit of the Constitution. It is different from popular or social morality. The State and courts must be guided by constitutional morality even when it conflicts with prevailing social morality — ensuring protection of individual rights and minorities even against majoritarian pressure.
simplified_explanation: The doctrine was invoked prominently in Navtej Singh Johar (2018 — decriminalization of homosexuality) and Joseph Shine (2018 — adultery). The Court held that constitutional morality — respect for individual dignity, liberty, and equality — must prevail over social morality (majority's disapproval of certain conduct). Dr. B.R. Ambedkar had emphasized constitutional morality in the Constituent Assembly.
keywords: Constitutional Morality,Social Morality,Individual Dignity,Minority Rights,Ambedkar,Navtej Johar
landmark_case_1: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Section 377 BNS struck down; constitutional morality protects LGBT persons even against majority disapproval
landmark_case_2: Joseph Shine v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Section 497 BNS (adultery) struck down; constitutional morality demands gender equality over patriarchal social norms
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Manifest Arbitrariness as Ground for Striking Down Legislation
verbatim_text_excerpt: Legislation can be struck down under Article 14 if it is manifestly arbitrary — that is, if it is capricious, irrational, without adequate determining principle, or enacted without reasonable basis. The court does not require proof of discrimination; irrationality itself is sufficient to invalidate the law.
simplified_explanation: The doctrine was definitively established in Shayara Bano v. Union of India (2017 — Triple Talaq case). The majority held that instantaneous triple talaq (talaq-e-biddat) was manifestly arbitrary because it gave the husband absolute, unilateral power to dissolve marriage capriciously. The test is: is the provision clearly capricious, irrational, and without adequate reason?
keywords: Manifest Arbitrariness,Article 14,Arbitrariness,Irrationality,Legislation
landmark_case_1: Shayara Bano v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Instantaneous triple talaq struck down as manifestly arbitrary; Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Marriage) Act 2019 followed
landmark_case_2: Swiss Ribbons Pvt Ltd v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: IBC's distinction between financial and operational creditors is not manifestly arbitrary; classification has rational basis
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Right to Privacy as Fundamental Right
verbatim_text_excerpt: The right to privacy is protected as an intrinsic part of the right to life and personal liberty under Article 21 and as a part of the freedoms guaranteed by Part III of the Constitution. Privacy includes at its core the preservation of personal intimacies, the sanctity of family life, marriage, procreation, the home and sexual orientation.
simplified_explanation: The 9-judge bench in K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017) unanimously declared privacy a Fundamental Right — overruling M.P. Sharma (1954) and Kharak Singh (1962). The judgment has three dimensions: (1) privacy of information/data; (2) bodily integrity/autonomy; (3) dignity and freedom in personal choices. Aadhaar's data collection was subsequently tested against this.
keywords: Right to Privacy,KS Puttaswamy,Article 21,Informational Privacy,Bodily Integrity,Dignity,Sexual Orientation
landmark_case_1: K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: 9-judge bench: Right to Privacy is a Fundamental Right under Article 21; overrules MP Sharma and Kharak Singh
landmark_case_2: K.S. Puttaswamy (Aadhaar) v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Aadhaar partially upheld; Section 57 allowing private entities to use Aadhaar struck down as privacy violation
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Scope and Limits of Judicial Review in India
verbatim_text_excerpt: Judicial review is the power of courts to examine the constitutional validity of legislative and executive actions. In India, it has three dimensions: (1) Legislations must conform to the Constitution; (2) Executive actions must have legislative backing and be within legislative limits; (3) After Kesavananda, even constitutional amendments must conform to basic structure.
simplified_explanation: India's judicial review is wider than the UK's but narrower than the USA's. Courts can examine: constitutional validity of laws, procedural propriety of Parliament's functioning (limited), whether executive action has legal authority, proportionality of rights restrictions. Courts CANNOT examine the wisdom or policy of laws — only their constitutionality.
keywords: Judicial Review,Constitutionality,Basic Structure,Executive Review,Policy Questions,Proportionality
