landmark_case_1: Marbury v. Madison (US reference)
lc1_holding_summary: American origin of judicial review — Indian SC adopted and developed it in Indian constitutional context
landmark_case_2: L. Chandra Kumar v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Power of judicial review is part of basic structure; cannot be abrogated by Parliament or constitutional amendment
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Legitimate Expectation in Administrative and Constitutional Law
verbatim_text_excerpt: If a public authority has made a promise or representation (express or implied) or has established a settled practice, a person to whom the promise was made or who relied on the practice has a 'legitimate expectation' that the authority will not act contrary to that expectation without at least affording procedural fairness.
simplified_explanation: Legitimate expectation is an evolved principle from natural justice. It bridges administrative law and constitutional law. The expectation may be either: (1) Procedural — right to be heard before the expectation is defeated; or (2) Substantive — expectation that a benefit will be conferred. Indian courts have applied this in service law, government contracts, and regulatory matters.
keywords: Legitimate Expectation,Natural Justice,Procedural Fairness,Promise,Public Authority,Administrative Law
landmark_case_1: M.P. Oil Extraction v. State of MP
lc1_holding_summary: Settled government practice creates legitimate expectation; departure requires reasons and opportunity to be heard
landmark_case_2: Food Corporation of India v. Kamdhenu Cattle Feed Industries
lc2_holding_summary: Legitimate expectation under Article 14: if government gives a representation, it cannot arbitrarily resile from it
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Code on Wages 2019
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Code on Wages, 2019 seeks to regulate wage and bonus payments in all employments where any industry, trade, business or manufacture is carried out. It subsumes four central labour laws: the Payment of Wages Act 1936, Minimum Wages Act 1948, Payment of Bonus Act 1965 and Equal Remuneration Act 1976
simplified_explanation: First of the four Labour Codes. Universal minimum wage coverage extended to all workers (previously only scheduled employments under Minimum Wages Act). Floor wage to be fixed by Centre; States cannot fix wages below floor. Equal remuneration for men and women. Bonus for employees earning up to Rs 21,000/month. Simplifies compliance with unified definitions.
keywords: Code on Wages,Minimum Wages,Floor Wage,Equal Remuneration,Bonus,Labour Code,Four Labour Laws
landmark_case_1: Workmen of Reptakos Brett v. Management
lc1_holding_summary: Minimum wage must cover basic needs, education, health, old age provision — five-component living wage test
landmark_case_2: Hindustan Antibiotics Ltd v. Workmen
lc2_holding_summary: Wage fixation must consider industry's capacity to pay, prevailing wages, and cost of living
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Industrial Relations Code 2020
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Industrial Relations Code, 2020 consolidates and amends the laws relating to Trade Unions, conditions of employment in industrial establishment or undertaking, investigation and settlement of industrial disputes. It subsumed three laws: Trade Unions Act 1926, Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act 1946, and Industrial Disputes Act 1947
simplified_explanation: Major change: threshold for prior government permission for retrenchment/closure raised from 100 to 300 workers — allowing small/medium industries more flexibility. Introduces concept of 'fixed-term employment' with same benefits as permanent workers. Negotiating Union concept for collective bargaining. Re-skilling fund for retrenched workers.
keywords: Industrial Relations Code,Trade Union,Industrial Dispute,Retrenchment,Closure,Standing Orders,300 Workers Threshold
landmark_case_1: Workmen of Dimakuchi Tea Estate v. Management
lc1_holding_summary: Industrial dispute definition is wide; includes disputes relating to employment, terms of employment, and conditions of work
landmark_case_2: Bharat Union of Journalists v. The Hindu
lc2_holding_summary: Trade union right to collective bargaining is part of Article 19(1)(c) freedom of association
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Code on Social Security 2020
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Code on Social Security, 2020 consolidates the laws relating to social security with the goal to extend social security to all employees and workers either in the organised or unorganised or any other sectors. It subsumes nine laws including EPF Act 1952, ESI Act 1948, Maternity Benefit Act 1961, Gratuity Act 1972
simplified_explanation: Extends social security to gig workers, platform workers, and unorganised sector workers for the first time — a landmark change. Key bodies: EPFO (Employees Provident Fund Organisation) and ESIC (Employees State Insurance Corporation). Aggregators (like Ola, Uber, Swiggy) must contribute to a social security fund for gig/platform workers.
keywords: Social Security Code,EPFO,ESIC,Gig Workers,Platform Workers,Maternity Benefit,Gratuity,Unorganised Sector
landmark_case_1: CITU v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Extension of social security to unorganised workers is constitutionally mandated under Articles 38, 39, 41, 42, 43
landmark_case_2: Provident Fund Commissioner v. Central Board of Trustees
lc2_holding_summary: EPFO coverage and contribution requirements are mandatory; employer cannot contract out of social security obligations
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: Occupational Safety Health and Working Conditions Code 2020
title: OSH Code 2020
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions Code, 2020 consolidates and amends the laws regulating the occupational safety, health and working conditions of persons employed in an establishment. It subsumes 13 laws including Factories Act 1948, Mines Act 1952, Plantation Labour Act 1951
simplified_explanation: Unified law for workplace safety covering factories, mines, construction, plantations, and other establishments. Key features: single registration and licence for all establishments, mandatory safety committees in establishments with 250+ workers, annual health check-ups, occupational safety standards, and extension to contract labour. Replaces fragmented sector-specific laws.
keywords: OSH Code,Workplace Safety,Factories Act,Mines Act,Occupational Health,Safety Committee,Contract Labour
landmark_case_1: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Right to health and safe working conditions is part of Article 21; employer has constitutional duty of care
landmark_case_2: Consumer Education and Research Centre v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Right to health of workers is a Fundamental Right; safety regulations at workplace are constitutionally mandated
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act 2005
verbatim_text_excerpt: There shall be a Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Scheme for providing not less than one hundred days of guaranteed wage employment in every financial year to every household in rural areas whose adult members volunteer to do unskilled manual work
simplified_explanation: Landmark rural employment law guaranteeing 100 days of unskilled work per household per year at statutory minimum wages. If work not provided within 15 days, unemployment allowance is payable. Employment is demand-driven — a legal entitlement, not a welfare scheme. Has benefited over 15 crore households annually. Also creates rural infrastructure assets.
keywords: MGNREGA,100 Days Work,Rural Employment,Guaranteed Employment,Unemployment Allowance,Unskilled Work
landmark_case_1: People's Union for Civil Liberties v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Right to food and employment is part of right to life under Article 21; SC directed implementation of rural employment schemes
landmark_case_2: Swaraj Abhiyan v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: SC monitored MGNREGA implementation during drought; directed payment of pending wages and drought relief
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) 2023
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita seeks to consolidate and amend the provisions relating to general offences applicable throughout India. It replaces the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 1860
simplified_explanation: Replaced BNS from 1 July 2024. Key changes: Section 103 (murder) replaces BNS Section 103 (formerly 302); organized crime and terrorism added as offences; sedition (Section 152 (formerly 124A) BNS) removed but replaced by Section 152 (acts endangering sovereignty/integrity); enhanced protection for women and children; community service as new punishment.
keywords: BNS,Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita,BNS Replacement,Murder,Sedition Removed,Organized Crime,Community Service,2024
landmark_case_1: Kedar Nath Singh v. State of Bihar
lc1_holding_summary: Sedition (BNS 124A) can only apply to incitement to violence or disorder, not mere criticism — now replaced by BNS Section 152
landmark_case_2: S.G. Vombatkere v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: SC stayed sedition law pending review; eventually replaced by BNS 2023 without sedition
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Home Affairs
legal_classification: Section-specific; generally cognizable / non-bailable for core offences

title: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS) 2023
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita seeks to consolidate and amend the law relating to the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS) and matters connected therewith. It replaces the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS), 1973
simplified_explanation: Replaced BNSS from 1 July 2024. Key changes: 90-day remand (extendable to 180 days for serious crimes); mandatory audio-video recording of search and seizure; time-bound trials and investigations; Zero FIR to be transferred within 15 days; victims' rights strengthened; electronic summons and processes recognized.
keywords: BNSS,BNSS Replacement,Criminal Procedure,FIR,Remand,90 Days,Zero FIR,Trial Timeline
landmark_case_1: Arnab Manoranjan Goswami v. State of Maharashtra
lc1_holding_summary: Personal liberty under Article 21 requires courts to apply bail provisions liberally
landmark_case_2: Satender Kumar Antil v. CBI
lc2_holding_summary: Default bail is a right; delay in filing charge sheet cannot be used to detain accused indefinitely
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Home Affairs

title: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 consolidates and provides the general rules and principles of evidence for fair trial, and came into force on 1 July 2024.
simplified_explanation: The Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 is the operative evidence law, replacing the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam (BSA), 1872 from 1 July 2024. It modernises proof rules for electronic records, presumptions, and examination of evidence.
keywords: BSA,Evidence Law,Electronic Evidence,Proof,Evidence Act Repeal,1 July 2024
landmark_case_1: State of Maharashtra v. Praful Desai
lc1_holding_summary: Video conferencing for recording evidence is valid; physical presence not always required
landmark_case_2: Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer
lc2_holding_summary: Electronic records require proper certification under Evidence Act; digital evidence must be authenticated
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Home Affairs
punishment_quantum: Evidence law; no standalone punishment quantum.

title: Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act 1967 as amended
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to provide for the more effective prevention of certain unlawful activities of individuals and associations, and for matters connected therewith. UAPA designates organizations AND individuals as terrorists
simplified_explanation: Major 2019 Amendment allows designation of individuals as terrorists (not just organisations). NIA can investigate UAPA cases. Bail is extremely difficult — accused must prove innocence prima facie. No time limit for investigation before charge sheet. High Courts and Supreme Court have cautioned against misuse.
keywords: UAPA,Unlawful Activities,Terrorist Designation,Individual Designation,NIA,Anti-Terror
landmark_case_1: National Investigation Agency v. Zahoor Ahmad Shah Watali
lc1_holding_summary: Bail standard under UAPA is very high — court must be satisfied that no prima facie case exists; cannot go into merits at bail stage
landmark_case_2: Union of India v. K.A. Najeeb
lc2_holding_summary: Constitutional courts can grant bail on grounds of prolonged incarceration violating Article 21 even in UAPA cases
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry
legal_classification: Section-specific; generally cognizable / non-bailable for core offences

title: Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act 1985
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to consolidate and amend the law relating to narcotic drugs, to make stringent provisions for the control and regulation of operations relating to narcotic drugs and psychotropic substances, to provide for the forfeiture of property derived from, or used in, illicit traffic in narcotic drugs and psychotropic substances
simplified_explanation: Strict anti-narcotics law with reverse burden of proof — accused must prove innocence for commercial quantity drugs. Mandatory minimum sentences for commercial quantities. NCB (Narcotics Control Bureau) is the investigating agency. The Act distinguishes small quantity (no mandatory minimum), intermediate, and commercial quantity (10+ years).
keywords: NDPS Act,Narcotic Drugs,NCB,Reverse Burden,Commercial Quantity,Mandatory Minimum Sentence
landmark_case_1: Tofan Singh v. State of Tamil Nadu
lc1_holding_summary: NCB officers are 'police officers' under NDPS; confessions before them are inadmissible as evidence
landmark_case_2: State of HP v. Pawan Kumar
lc2_holding_summary: Strict compliance with NDPS Act procedures for sampling is mandatory; violation leads to acquittal
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry
legal_classification: Section-specific; generally cognizable / non-bailable for core offences

title: Prevention of Money Laundering Act 2002 — Key Provisions
verbatim_text_excerpt: Whosoever directly or indirectly attempts to indulge or knowingly assists or knowingly is a party or is actually involved in any process or activity connected with proceeds of crime and projecting it as untainted property shall be guilty of the offence of money laundering under this Act
simplified_explanation: Three stages of money laundering: Placement (introducing dirty money into financial system), Layering (disguising the trail), Integration (bringing money back as legitimate). ED (Enforcement Directorate) investigates. Twin test for bail: accused must show no prima facie guilt AND no likelihood of reoffending. Properties can be attached before conviction.
keywords: PMLA,Money Laundering,ED,Enforcement Directorate,Attachment,Twin Test,Proceeds of Crime
landmark_case_1: Vijay Madanlal Choudhary v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: PMLA's powers of arrest, search, attachment, and twin bail test upheld as constitutionally valid
landmark_case_2: Y. Balaji v. Karthik Desari
lc2_holding_summary: PMLA cannot be invoked unless predicate offence (scheduled offence) is duly established
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

article_section: IT Act 2000 and IT Amendment Act 2008
title: Information Technology Act 2000
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to provide legal recognition for transactions carried out by means of electronic data interchange and other means of electronic communication, commonly referred to as electronic commerce, and to facilitate electronic filing of documents with Government agencies
simplified_explanation: IT Act provides legal validity for electronic documents, digital signatures, and e-contracts. Defines cybercrimes: hacking (Section 66), phishing, identity theft, cyber stalking, voyeurism, sexting. Section 69 allows government to intercept electronic communications for security. Section 66A (online offensive content) was struck down by SC as unconstitutional.
keywords: IT Act,Cybercrimes,Digital Signature,Section 66A,Electronic Commerce,Hacking,Cyber Law
landmark_case_1: Shreya Singhal v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Section 66A IT Act struck down — vague, overbroad, and violates free speech under Article 19(1)(a)
landmark_case_2: Avinash Bajaj v. State (NCT of Delhi)
lc2_holding_summary: Internet intermediaries have limited liability for user-generated content under IT Act's safe harbour provisions
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology

title: Companies Act 2013
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to consolidate and amend the law relating to companies. A company is a legal entity that is distinct from its members, with perpetual succession, common seal, and separate legal personality
simplified_explanation: Major reform of Indian corporate law. Key features: One Person Company (OPC); independent directors mandatory for listed companies; Serious Fraud Investigation Office (SFIO) for white-collar crime; mandatory 2% CSR spending for qualifying companies; National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) replaces CLB; class action suits allowed; women director mandatory.
keywords: Companies Act,NCLT,CSR,Independent Directors,One Person Company,SFIO,Women Director
landmark_case_1: Salomon v. Salomon and Co Ltd
lc1_holding_summary: Foundational case on corporate personality — company is a separate legal entity distinct from its members
landmark_case_2: State of UP v. Renusagar Power Co
lc2_holding_summary: Lifting the corporate veil justified when company is a sham or to prevent fraud
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Securities and Exchange Board of India Act 1992
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to provide for the establishment of a Board to protect the interests of investors in securities and to promote the development of, and to regulate, the securities market and for matters connected therewith or incidental thereto
simplified_explanation: SEBI is the statutory regulator for capital markets. Powers: (1) Quasi-legislative — makes regulations; (2) Quasi-executive — investigates, conducts searches; (3) Quasi-judicial — adjudicates and imposes penalties. SEBI regulates: stock exchanges, brokers, mutual funds, FIIs, insider trading, takeover code, collective investment schemes.
keywords: SEBI,Securities Market,Stock Exchange,Insider Trading,Mutual Fund,Capital Markets
landmark_case_1: SEBI v. Rajkumar Nagpal
lc1_holding_summary: SEBI has wide investigative powers; past misconduct in securities market can justify future bar from markets
landmark_case_2: NSE Co-location Case
lc2_holding_summary: SEBI's powers extend to surveillance of market manipulation including algorithmic trading irregularities
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Competition Act 2002
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to provide, keeping in view of the economic development of the country, for the establishment of a Commission to prevent practices having adverse effect on competition, to promote and sustain competition in markets, to protect the interests of consumers and to ensure freedom of trade
simplified_explanation: Competition Commission of India (CCI) established. Prohibits: (1) anti-competitive agreements (cartels, price-fixing, bid-rigging); (2) abuse of dominant position (predatory pricing, denial of market access); (3) combinations (mergers/acquisitions reducing competition). Dawn raids, leniency programme. CCI can impose penalty up to 10% of average turnover for 3 years.
keywords: Competition Act,CCI,Cartel,Dominant Position,Merger,Anti-Competitive,Predatory Pricing
landmark_case_1: CCI v. Steel Authority of India
lc1_holding_summary: CCI has wide powers of investigation; forming prima facie opinion before investigation is mandatory
landmark_case_2: Google LLC v. CCI
lc2_holding_summary: Digital platforms with dominant position cannot favour own services; CCI imposed Rs 1337 crore penalty on Google
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Corporate Affairs
punishment_quantum: Section-specific civil penalties and monetary sanctions; quantum depends on contravention and turnover/asset metrics.

title: Consumer Protection Act 2019
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to provide for protection of the interests of consumers and for the said purpose, to establish authorities for timely and effective administration and settlement of consumers disputes and for matters connected therewith or incidental thereto
simplified_explanation: Replaces Consumer Protection Act 1986. Key additions: e-commerce regulation (sellers must disclose information); product liability (manufacturer/seller liable for defective product even without negligence); mediation as alternate dispute resolution; Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA) for class actions; enhanced pecuniary jurisdiction of consumer forums.
keywords: Consumer Protection Act,CCPA,Product Liability,E-commerce,Consumer Forum,Mediation,Defective Product
landmark_case_1: Lucknow Development Authority v. M.K. Gupta
lc1_holding_summary: Consumer forums can award compensation for deficiency in service including by statutory bodies
landmark_case_2: Spring Meadows Hospital v. Harjol Ahluwalia
lc2_holding_summary: Medical negligence falls within 'deficiency in service' under Consumer Protection Act
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: Arbitration and Conciliation Act 1996 as amended
title: Arbitration and Conciliation Act 1996
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to consolidate and amend the law relating to domestic arbitration, international commercial arbitration and enforcement of foreign arbitral awards. India adopted UNCITRAL Model Law through this Act
simplified_explanation: India's principal arbitration legislation. Key amendments in 2015 and 2019: strict time limits for completing arbitration (12 months + 6 months extension); automatic stay of award on filing appeal only if court is satisfied of fraud/corruption; Arbitration Council of India for accreditation; grading of arbitrators to ensure quality.
keywords: Arbitration,ADR,UNCITRAL,International Commercial Arbitration,Foreign Award,Conciliation
landmark_case_1: BALCO v. Kaiser Aluminium Technical Services
lc1_holding_summary: Seat of arbitration determines governing law; part I of the Act does not apply to foreign-seated arbitrations
landmark_case_2: Ssangyong Engineering v. NHAI
lc2_holding_summary: Public policy review of arbitral awards is narrow; courts cannot re-examine merits of award
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Foreign Exchange Management Act 1999
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to consolidate and amend the law relating to foreign exchange with the objective of facilitating external trade and payments and for promoting the orderly development and maintenance of foreign exchange market in India
simplified_explanation: Replaced FERA (Foreign Exchange Regulation Act) 1973. Major shift: forex violations are now civil offences (not criminal). RBI regulates capital account transactions; Centre regulates current account. FDI, FPI, ECB governed by FEMA. Compounding of offences allowed. Enforcement Directorate enforces FEMA alongside PMLA.
keywords: FEMA,Foreign Exchange,RBI,FDI,FPI,Capital Account,Current Account,Enforcement Directorate
landmark_case_1: Anuj Garg v. Hotel Association of India
lc1_holding_summary: FEMA regulation must be proportionate; restrictions on foreign exchange must have reasonable nexus to monetary stability
landmark_case_2: K.P. Unnikrishnan v. Enforcement Directorate
lc2_holding_summary: FEMA proceedings are civil in nature; mens rea not required; penalties are not criminal punishment
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Securitisation and Reconstruction of Financial Assets and Enforcement of Security Interest Act 2002
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to regulate securitisation and reconstruction of financial assets and enforcement of security interest and to provide for a Central database of security interests created on property rights, and for matters connected therewith or incidental thereto
simplified_explanation: SARFAESI allows banks and financial institutions to recover secured loans (above Rs 1 lakh) without court intervention. Banks can take possession of secured assets, manage them, and sell them on default without filing a civil suit. Borrower can approach Debt Recovery Tribunal within 45 days of taking possession.
keywords: SARFAESI,NPA,Loan Recovery,Secured Asset,Bank Recovery,DRT
landmark_case_1: Mardia Chemicals Ltd v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: SARFAESI Act is constitutionally valid; borrower's right to approach DRT provides sufficient safeguard against arbitrary action
landmark_case_2: United Bank of India v. Satyawati Tondon
lc2_holding_summary: SARFAESI proceedings are summary proceedings for recovery; courts should not interfere at possession stage
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Wildlife (Protection) Act 1972
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to provide for the protection of wild animals, birds and plants and for matters connected therewith or ancillary or incidental thereto with a view to ensuring the ecological and environmental security of the country
simplified_explanation: India's comprehensive wildlife protection law. Creates Schedule I-VI for protected species (Schedule I — highest protection, no trade). Establishes National Parks (no human activity), Wildlife Sanctuaries (some activity permitted), Tiger Reserves, Community Reserves. CITES (Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species) compliance. Wildlife Crime Control Bureau.
keywords: Wildlife Protection Act,National Park,Wildlife Sanctuary,Tiger Reserve,CITES,Schedule I Species
landmark_case_1: T.N. Godavarman v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Ongoing PIL for forest and wildlife protection; SC has issued over 1000 orders on forest governance
landmark_case_2: Centre for Environment Law WWF-I v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Translocation of Asiatic Lions from Gir to Kuno Palpur directed for species survival — state's resistance overridden
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Forest (Conservation) Act 1980
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to provide for the conservation of forests and for matters connected therewith or ancillary or incidental thereto. No State Government shall make or permit any use of forest land for non-forest purpose without obtaining prior approval of Central Government
simplified_explanation: Prior approval of Central Government is mandatory for any diversion of forest land for non-forest purposes (mining, infrastructure, industry). The Forest (Conservation) Amendment Act 2023 renamed it Van Sanrakshan Evam Janpadha Parishad Adhiniyam but key approval requirement retained. Compensatory afforestation mandatory for diverted forest land.
keywords: Forest Conservation Act,Forest Diversion,Prior Approval,Non-Forest Purpose,Compensatory Afforestation,CAMPA
landmark_case_1: T.N. Godavarman v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: SC expansively defined 'forest' — any area recorded as forest in government records requires prior approval
landmark_case_2: Lafarge Umiam Mining v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Prior approval under Forest Conservation Act is a non-derogable requirement; diversion without it is void
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

title: Biological Diversity Act 2002
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to provide for conservation of biological diversity, sustainable use of its components and fair and equitable sharing of the benefits arising out of the use of biological resources, their by-products and knowledge associated therewith
simplified_explanation: Implements the Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) in India. Creates three-tier system: National Biodiversity Authority (NBA), State Biodiversity Boards (SBB), and Biodiversity Management Committees (BMC) at local levels. Regulates access to biological resources and associated traditional knowledge. Access and Benefit Sharing (ABS) — bio-resources cannot be used without approval and sharing benefits with local communities.
keywords: Biodiversity Act,NBA,Access Benefit Sharing,CBD,Traditional Knowledge,Bio-resources,BMC
landmark_case_1: Divya Pharmacy v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Commercial users of biological resources must comply with ABS requirements; traditional knowledge of communities must be protected
landmark_case_2: Union of India v. Biodiversity Management Committee
lc2_holding_summary: BMCs at local level have important role in protecting bio-diversity; their constitution is mandatory
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) Notification 2019
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change issues the Coastal Regulation Zone Notification under the Environment Protection Act 1986. It regulates activities in coastal areas to protect the fragile coastal ecosystem
simplified_explanation: CRZ classifies coastal areas into CRZ-I (ecologically sensitive), CRZ-II (developed urban areas), CRZ-III (rural areas), CRZ-IVA and IVB (water areas). Sets Hazard Line and HTL (High Tide Line)-based no-development zones. Construction, industry, and tourism activities regulated within 500 metres of HTL.
keywords: CRZ,Coastal Regulation,High Tide Line,Coastal Ecosystem,CRZ-I,CRZ-III,Hazard Line
landmark_case_1: Indian Council for Enviro-Legal Action v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Industries violating CRZ notification must pay Polluter Pays compensation; absolute liability applies
landmark_case_2: Goa Foundation v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: CRZ notification must be strictly enforced; state cannot dilute it for tourism interests
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: National Green Tribunal Act 2010 — Key Provisions
title: NGT Act 2010 — Jurisdiction and Powers
verbatim_text_excerpt: The National Green Tribunal shall have jurisdiction over all civil cases where a substantial question relating to environment (including enforcement of any legal right relating to environment) is involved and such question arises out of the implementation of the enactments specified in Schedule I
simplified_explanation: Schedule I laws under NGT jurisdiction: Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, Environment Protection Act 1986, Forest Conservation Act 1980, Biodiversity Act 2002, Wildlife Protection Act, Public Liability Insurance Act 1991. NGT can award compensation and relief. Orders are like decrees of civil courts. Appellate jurisdiction over MoEF decisions.
keywords: NGT,National Green Tribunal,Schedule I,Environmental Justice,Compensation,Appellate Jurisdiction
landmark_case_1: M.K. Ranjitsinh v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: SC recognized climate change as a fundamental rights issue; NGT to be strengthened for environmental justice
landmark_case_2: Braj Foundation v. State of UP
lc2_holding_summary: NGT has jurisdiction over all matters in Schedule I; state cannot oust NGT jurisdiction by local legislation
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

article_section: Air Prevention and Control of Pollution Act 1981
title: Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act 1981
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to provide for the prevention, control and abatement of air pollution, for the establishment, with a view to carrying out the aforesaid purposes, of Boards, for conferring on and assigning to such Boards powers and functions relating thereto and for matters connected therewith
simplified_explanation: Enacted under Article 253 after the UN Stockholm Conference resolution. Central and State Pollution Control Boards regulate air quality. Industries require consent from SPCB. National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) set by CPCB. Delhi's odd-even scheme, GRAP (Graded Response Action Plan) derive authority from this Act.
keywords: Air Pollution,CPCB,SPCB,Ambient Air Quality,NAAQS,Consent to Operate
landmark_case_1: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Vehicular Pollution)
lc1_holding_summary: Delhi vehicular pollution — SC directed conversion of public transport to CNG; courts can direct pollution control measures
landmark_case_2: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Taj Trapezium)
lc2_holding_summary: Industries emitting sulphur pollutants near Taj Mahal directed to relocate; air pollution causes heritage damage
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005
title: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to provide for more effective protection of the rights of women guaranteed under the Constitution who are victims of violence of any kind occurring within the family and for matters connected therewith or incidental thereto
simplified_explanation: Comprehensive civil law for domestic violence protection. Covers physical, sexual, emotional, verbal, and economic abuse. Provides for: Protection Orders (no-contact), Residence Orders (right to live in shared household), Monetary Relief, Custody Orders, Compensation Orders. Magistrate can pass ex parte orders. Protection Officers assist victims.
keywords: Domestic Violence,Protection Order,Residence Order,PWDVA,Shared Household,Monetary Relief
landmark_case_1: Indra Sarma v. V.K.V. Sarma
lc1_holding_summary: Domestic Violence Act applies to relationships in the nature of marriage; live-in relationships covered
landmark_case_2: S.R. Batra v. Taruna Batra
lc2_holding_summary: Shared household under PWDVA means household belonging to husband or jointly owned; wife cannot claim right in in-laws' house if husband has no interest in it
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace Act 2013
title: Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act 2013
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to provide protection against sexual harassment of women at workplace and for the prevention and redressal of complaints of sexual harassment and for matters connected therewith or incidental thereto
simplified_explanation: Enacted to give statutory effect to the Vishaka guidelines (1997). Every employer with 10+ employees must constitute an Internal Complaints Committee (ICC). Local Complaints Committee (LCC) for establishments below 10 or complaints against employer. Defines sexual harassment broadly including unwelcome physical/verbal/non-verbal conduct of sexual nature.
keywords: POSH Act,Sexual Harassment,Workplace,ICC,LCC,Vishaka Guidelines,Prevention
landmark_case_1: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
lc1_holding_summary: Sexual harassment at workplace violates Articles 14, 19, 21; Vishaka guidelines issued as interim measure pending legislation
landmark_case_2: Medha Kotwal Lele v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: SC monitored implementation of Vishaka guidelines in all sectors; POSH Act followed in 2013
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: Scheduled Caste and Scheduled Tribe Prevention of Atrocities Act 1989
title: SC and ST (Prevention of Atrocities) Act 1989 as amended 2015 and 2018
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to prevent the commission of offences of atrocities against the members of the Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes, to provide for Special Courts for the trial of such offences and for the relief and rehabilitation of the victims of such offences
simplified_explanation: Defines specific atrocities against SC/ST members. Special Courts for speedy trial. No anticipatory bail for accused (upheld by SC). Exclusive special courts in all districts with 10+ SC/ST population. 2015 Amendment added new offences including garlanding with footwear, preventing SC/ST from voting, imposing social/economic boycott.
keywords: SC ST Atrocities Act,Prevention of Atrocities,Special Court,No Anticipatory Bail,Social Boycott,Caste Discrimination
landmark_case_1: Subhash Kashinath Mahajan v. State of Maharashtra
lc1_holding_summary: SC wrongly diluted the Act by adding safeguards — quickly nullified by 2018 Amendment reversing this decision
landmark_case_2: Union of India v. State of Maharashtra
lc2_holding_summary: No anticipatory bail under SC/ST Atrocities Act; Act must be enforced strictly without procedural dilution
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: Right to Fair Compensation and Transparency in Land Acquisition Act 2013
title: Right to Fair Compensation and Transparency in Land Acquisition, Rehabilitation and Resettlement Act 2013
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to ensure, in consultation with institutions of local self-government and Gram Sabhas established under the Constitution, a humane, participatory, informed and transparent process for land acquisition for industrialisation, development of essential infrastructural facilities and urbanisation with the least disturbance to the owners of the land and other affected families and provide just and fair compensation to the affected families
simplified_explanation: Replaced the colonial Land Acquisition Act 1894. Key changes: mandatory Social Impact Assessment (SIA) before acquisition; consent of 70% (PPP projects) and 80% (private projects) affected families; market value × 2 (rural) or market value (urban) as minimum compensation; mandatory R&R provisions; multi-crop irrigated land generally not to be acquired.
keywords: Land Acquisition,LARR Act,Fair Compensation,SIA,Consent,Rehabilitation Resettlement,Multi-Crop Land
landmark_case_1: Indore Development Authority v. Manoharlal
lc1_holding_summary: Lapse of land acquisition proceedings under old Act cannot be revived; compensation must be paid
landmark_case_2: Pune Municipal Corporation v. Harakchand Solanki
lc2_holding_summary: Delay in compensation payment does not invalidate acquisition but entitled to interest
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act 2015
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to consolidate and amend the law relating to children alleged and found to be in conflict with law and children in need of care and protection by catering to their basic needs through proper care, protection, development, treatment and social re-integration
simplified_explanation: Replaced JJ Act 2000. Key change: juveniles aged 16-18 charged with heinous offences (7+ years) can be tried as adults by Juvenile Justice Board. Foster care introduced. Adoption simplified under CARA (Central Adoption Resource Authority). Child Welfare Committee for CNCP (Children in Need of Care and Protection). Mandatory reporting of missing children.
keywords: Juvenile Justice,JJ Act,CARA,Adoption,Child Welfare Committee,Heinous Offence,CNCP
landmark_case_1: Salil Bali v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Age of juvenility remains 18 years; Nirbhaya case juvenile controversy led to 2015 Act amendment
landmark_case_2: Bachpan Bachao Andolan v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Children's rights require protective legislation; JJ framework must ensure rehabilitation not punishment
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: National Food Security Act 2013
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to provide for food and nutritional security in human life cycle approach, by ensuring access to adequate quantity of quality food at affordable prices to people to live a life with dignity and for matters connected therewith or incidental thereto
simplified_explanation: 75% rural and 50% urban population covered. Priority Households: 5 kg food grains/person/month at subsidised prices (Rs 3/2/1 per kg for rice/wheat/coarse grain). Antyodaya Anna Yojana households: 35 kg/family/month. Pregnant women and lactating mothers entitled to meals during pregnancy. Children 6 months to 14 years entitled to meals under ICDS and MDM.
keywords: NFSA,Food Security,PDS,Priority Households,AAY,Mid Day Meal,Ration Card
landmark_case_1: People's Union for Civil Liberties v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Right to food is part of right to life under Article 21; SC directed implementation of food schemes
landmark_case_2: Swaraj Abhiyan v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: During drought, NFSA entitlements must be provided without conditions; state cannot deny food security
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: Transgender Persons Protection of Rights Act 2019
title: Transgender Persons (Protection of Rights) Act 2019 — Key Provisions
verbatim_text_excerpt: Every transgender person shall have a right to be recognised as such and shall have the right to self-perceived gender identity. Every transgender person who has been recognised shall have the right to a self-perceived gender identity. No establishment shall discriminate against any transgender person in matters relating to employment
simplified_explanation: Prohibits discrimination in: education, employment, healthcare, public accommodation, housing. Welfare measures: educational facilities, non-discrimination in healthcare, social security, rehabilitation. Criticisms: mandatory medical certificate for recognition as 'male'/'female' (not self-declaration); inadequate penalty provisions; no reservation for transgender persons in education/employment.
keywords: Transgender,Gender Identity,Non-Discrimination,NALSA,LGBTQ,Welfare
landmark_case_1: National Legal Services Authority v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Transgender persons are third gender; entitled to all constitutional rights and protections; self-identification of gender recognised
landmark_case_2: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Section 377 decriminalized; LGBTQ+ persons have full constitutional protection of dignity, autonomy, and identity
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Protection of Human Rights Act 1993 — National Human Rights Commission
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to provide for the constitution of a National Human Rights Commission, State Human Rights Commissions in States and Human Rights Courts for better protection of human rights and for matters connected therewith or incidental thereto
simplified_explanation: NHRC is India's apex human rights body. Chairperson must be a former Chief Justice of India. Can investigate human rights violations by State, take suo motu cognizance, visit any institution, review laws and recommend changes. Cannot investigate violations by Armed Forces directly (can seek reports from Defence Ministry). Recommendations are not binding but carry strong persuasive value.
keywords: NHRC,Human Rights,Protection of Human Rights Act,State Human Rights Commission,Former CJI
landmark_case_1: NHRC v. State of Arunachal Pradesh
lc1_holding_summary: NHRC has jurisdiction over all state human rights violations; recommendations must be complied with
landmark_case_2: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal
lc2_holding_summary: NHRC guidelines on custodial violence must be followed; compensation for custodial death is a right
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Home Affairs

title: Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act 2013
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to provide for the establishment of a body of Lokpal for the Union and Lokayuktas for States to inquire into allegations of corruption against certain public functionaries and for matters connected therewith or incidental thereto
simplified_explanation: Lokpal (for Centre) and Lokayuktas (for States) are ombudsman institutions. Lokpal's jurisdiction covers the PM (with restrictions), Ministers, MPs, Group A-D officers. Chairperson must be a former CJI or SC judge. Cannot inquire into PM's actions relating to national security, international relations, and public order. Complaints must be disposed of within 60 days.
keywords: Lokpal,Lokayukta,Anti-Corruption,Ombudsman,PM,Ministers,Group A-D
landmark_case_1: Anna Hazare Movement Reference
lc1_holding_summary: Civil society campaign that led to Lokpal Act; demonstrating constitutional mechanism for anti-corruption accountability
landmark_case_2: Vineet Narain v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: CBI must be independent; political interference in corruption investigations violates rule of law — context for Lokpal creation
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Personnel, Public Grievances and Pensions
punishment_quantum: No standalone punishment quantum at the body level; offence consequences arise through linked anti-corruption laws.
exceptions_and_limitations: Subject to jurisdictional carve-outs, prescribed complaint procedure, and linked anti-corruption statutes.
cross_references: Central Vigilance Commission Act 2003; Prevention of Corruption Act 1988

title: Central Vigilance Commission Act 2003
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to provide for the constitution of a Central Vigilance Commission to inquire into offences alleged to have been committed under the Prevention of Corruption Act 1988 by certain categories of public servants
simplified_explanation: CVC is a statutory body (constitutionally recognised since 2003) supervising vigilance in Central government. Headed by Central Vigilance Commissioner (CVC). Two Vigilance Commissioners. Superintendence over CBI for PC Act cases. Can recommend disciplinary action but cannot impose it. Department of Personnel and Training implements recommendations.
keywords: CVC,Central Vigilance Commission,Anti-Corruption,CBI,Prevention of Corruption,Vigilance
landmark_case_1: Vineet Narain v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: CVC must superintend CBI without political interference; hawala case led to CVC's statutory strengthening
landmark_case_2: State of West Bengal v. Committee for Protection of Democratic Rights
lc2_holding_summary: CBI investigation under CVC superintendence must maintain independence; courts can direct CBI to investigate
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Comptroller and Auditor General's (Duties, Powers and Conditions of Service) Act 1971
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to determine and specify the duties and powers of the Comptroller and Auditor General of India in relation to accounts of the Union and of the States and of other authorities or bodies
simplified_explanation: CAG audits: Union government accounts, State government accounts, government companies (partial), autonomous bodies receiving substantial grants, and defence accounts. Reports placed before Parliament/State Legislature through Public Accounts Committee (PAC). Performance audit (value for money), compliance audit, and financial audit are three types.
keywords: CAG,Comptroller Auditor General,Public Accounts,Financial Accountability,PAC,Performance Audit
landmark_case_1: Comptroller and Auditor General v. K.S. Jagannathan
lc1_holding_summary: CAG's constitutional mandate covers all government expenditure; it is the Parliament's watchdog
landmark_case_2: Subramanian Swamy v. Director CBI
lc2_holding_summary: CAG reports are authoritative findings of financial irregularity; they can form basis for CBI investigation
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

article_section: Prevention of Corruption Act 1988 as amended 2018
title: Prevention of Corruption Act 1988
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to consolidate and amend the law relating to the prevention of corruption and for matters connected therewith. A public servant who commits or abets the commission of an offence of criminal misconduct shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term which shall be not less than four years but which may extend to ten years
simplified_explanation: Key 2018 Amendment: bribe-givers are also criminally liable (previously only public servants); prior sanction required to prosecute public servants; time-bound completion of trial within 2 years (extendable); attachment and forfeiture of proceeds of corruption. CBI and Lokpal/Lokayukta are designated investigating agencies.
keywords: Prevention of Corruption,PC Act,Public Servant,Bribery,Prior Sanction,CBI,Lokpal
landmark_case_1: Subramanian Swamy v. Director CBI
lc1_holding_summary: Prior sanction requirement for prosecution of public servants protects them from harassment but must not be abused to shield corrupt officers
landmark_case_2: P.V. Narasimha Rao v. State
lc2_holding_summary: MPs enjoy constitutional protection under Article 105 for votes in Parliament but not for bribery
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry
legal_classification: Section-specific; generally cognizable / non-bailable for core offences

title: National Education Policy 2020
verbatim_text_excerpt: The National Education Policy 2020 envisions an India-centred education system that contributes directly to transforming our nation sustainably into an equitable and vibrant knowledge society, by providing high quality education to all
simplified_explanation: NEP 2020 (not a statute but a policy implemented through legislative and executive actions): 5+3+3+4 curriculum structure; emphasis on mother tongue/regional language instruction up to Grade 5; FLN (Foundational Literacy and Numeracy) by Grade 3; multidisciplinary higher education; vocational education integration; 50% GER (Gross Enrolment Ratio) in higher education by 2035; ITE (Integrated Teacher Education Programme).
keywords: NEP 2020,5+3+3+4,Mother Tongue,FLN,Multidisciplinary,GER,Vocational Education
landmark_case_1: Society for Unaided Private Schools v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Educational policy must conform to constitutional provisions of Articles 21A, 29, 30
landmark_case_2: Pai Foundation v. State of Karnataka
lc2_holding_summary: Education policy must balance institutional autonomy with right to education and anti-discrimination
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Citizenship Amendment Act 2019
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act further to amend the Citizenship Act, 1955. The First Schedule to the Citizenship Act, 1955 shall be amended. The proviso to sub-section (1) of section 2 of the principal Act shall be omitted. In section 6B of the principal Act — persons belonging to Hindu, Sikh, Buddhist, Jain, Parsi, or Christian community from Afghanistan, Bangladesh and Pakistan shall not be treated as illegal migrants
simplified_explanation: CAA grants accelerated citizenship (5 years instead of 11) to non-Muslim persecuted minorities from Afghanistan, Bangladesh, and Pakistan. Controversial because it excludes Muslims. Challenge pending in SC: Article 14 equal protection, secularism as basic structure, and discrimination on religious grounds. CAA Rules notified in March 2024.
keywords: CAA,Citizenship Amendment,Hindu Sikh Buddhist Jain Parsi Christian,Afghanistan Bangladesh Pakistan,Secularism,Article 14
landmark_case_1: Indian Union Muslim League v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: CAA challenge pending before SC; substantial questions on Article 14 and secularism must be examined
landmark_case_2: Sarbananda Sonowal v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Citizenship law must conform to constitutional provisions; NRC was upheld in this Assam context
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Three Farm Laws 2020 (Repealed 2021)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Farmers' Produce Trade and Commerce (Promotion and Facilitation) Act, 2020; Farmers (Empowerment and Protection) Agreement on Price Assurance and Farm Services Act, 2020; Essential Commodities (Amendment) Act, 2020 — all repealed in November 2021
simplified_explanation: The three farm laws were enacted in September 2020 as agricultural market reforms: (1) FPTC Act — trade outside APMC mandis; (2) FAPAFS Act — contract farming; (3) EC Amendment — deregulation of commodities. The SC stayed implementation and appointed a committee. Massive farmer protests led to repeal in November 2021 before SC could rule on validity.
keywords: Farm Laws,APMC,Contract Farming,MSP,Agricultural Reform,Repeal 2021,Farmer Protest
landmark_case_1: In Re Farm Laws
lc1_holding_summary: SC stayed three farm laws; appointed expert committee; laws eventually repealed before SC pronouncement
landmark_case_2: Essential Commodities Act v. Agricultural Marketing Reference
lc2_holding_summary: Balance between free market and farmers' protection is a constitutional question under Articles 19 and 39
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Waqf (Amendment) Act 2025
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act further to amend the Waqf Act, 1995. The Amendment removes the power of Waqf Boards to unilaterally declare any property as Waqf property, mandates inclusion of non-Muslim members in Central Waqf Council and State Waqf Boards, and changes the dispute resolution mechanism
simplified_explanation: Controversial amendment to the Waqf Act 1995 (governing Muslim religious endowments). Key changes: (1) Removes 'waqf by user' concept — long-use cannot create waqf property; (2) Non-Muslim members in Waqf bodies; (3) Collector (district authority) to determine if disputed land is government or waqf; (4) Removal of survey commissioner powers. Challenged before SC as violating Articles 25, 26, and 30.
keywords: Waqf Amendment,Waqf Board,Muslim Religious Endowment,Non-Muslim Members,Waqf by User
landmark_case_1: In Re Waqf Amendment Act
lc1_holding_summary: Multiple petitions challenging Waqf Amendment Act; SC issued notice; interim orders on certain provisions
landmark_case_2: Waqf Board v. State of Karnataka
lc2_holding_summary: Waqf property rights are protected under Articles 25, 26, and 30; state cannot arbitrarily interfere
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Forest (Conservation) Amendment Act 2023 (Van Sanrakshan Evam Janpadha Parishad Adhiniyam)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Amendment Act exempts certain categories of land from the Forest (Conservation) Act 1980 including: land within 100km of international borders; survey/reconnaissance for national security; roadside amenities on existing roads; zoos; safari parks; eco-tourism facilities
simplified_explanation: Significant dilution of forest protection: exemptions for defence and strategic projects near borders, plantation forestry on degraded land without prior approval, and certain infrastructure near existing roads. Critics argue the amendment weakens the T.N. Godavarman PIL protections. Tribal forest dwellers' rights under Forest Rights Act may be affected.
keywords: Forest Conservation Amendment 2023,Border Areas,Defence Exemption,Plantation Forestry,VAN SANRAKSHAN
landmark_case_1: T.N. Godavarman v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: SC's broad definition of 'forest' now subject to statutory exceptions under 2023 Amendment
landmark_case_2: Wildlife First v. Ministry of Forest
lc2_holding_summary: Forest diversion for development projects must strictly follow FCA; exceptions must be narrowly construed
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Defence

title: Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 — Detailed Provisions
verbatim_text_excerpt: Every Data Fiduciary shall: take reasonable security safeguards to prevent personal data breach; inform the Board and each affected Data Principal in the event of a personal data breach; erase personal data as soon as the purpose for processing is over; publish the contact details of a Data Protection Officer
simplified_explanation: Key framework: Data Principal rights include right to information, right to correction, right to erasure, right to grievance redressal, right to nominate. Data Fiduciary obligations: purpose limitation, storage limitation, security safeguards, breach notification. Significant Data Fiduciaries (large platforms): additional obligations. Data Protection Board adjudicates complaints.
keywords: DPDP Act,Data Protection Board,Data Principal Rights,Data Fiduciary,Breach Notification,Consent,Purpose Limitation
landmark_case_1: KS Puttaswamy v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Privacy as Fundamental Right mandated data protection legislation — DPDP Act 2023 is the legislative response
landmark_case_2: Justice Srikrishna Committee Report
lc2_holding_summary: DPDP Act 2023 is the second iteration; first Personal Data Protection Bill 2019 was withdrawn and redrafted
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology

title: High Level Committee Report on One Nation One Election 2024
verbatim_text_excerpt: The High Level Committee (Kovind Committee) recommended simultaneous elections to Lok Sabha and all State Assemblies in the first phase, followed by simultaneous elections to urban and rural local bodies within 100 days. Recommended constitutional amendments to Articles 83, 172, 327 and new Article 82A
simplified_explanation: ONOE would require constitutional amendments to synchronize all elections. Committee submitted report in March 2024. The Constitution (129th Amendment) Bill 2024 was introduced in Parliament in December 2024. Issues include: hung Parliaments and state assemblies, federal concerns, Article 356 implications, frequent by-elections for vacated seats.
keywords: One Nation One Election,ONOE,Kovind Committee,Simultaneous Elections,129th Amendment Bill
landmark_case_1: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: ONOE concerns federalism; frequent elections have democratic accountability value — context for debate
landmark_case_2: Kuldip Nayar v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Electoral processes must be democratic; changes to election cycles require careful constitutional scrutiny
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice

title: National Legal Services Authority — Legal Aid Schemes
verbatim_text_excerpt: The National Legal Services Authority (NALSA) constituted under Legal Services Authorities Act 1987 provides free legal aid to: members of SC/ST, victims of human trafficking, women, children, persons with disability, industrial workers, persons in custody, and persons with annual income below Rs 3 lakh
simplified_explanation: NALSA implements Article 39A. Key schemes: Victim Compensation Scheme (for crime victims), Legal Services Clinics in colleges and prisons, Lok Adalats (settlement of disputes), Permanent Lok Adalats for public utility services, Mobile Legal Services Vans, PLVs (Para Legal Volunteers) in villages. NALSA has settled over 1 crore cases through Lok Adalats.
keywords: NALSA,Legal Aid,Lok Adalat,Victim Compensation,Para Legal Volunteers,Article 39A,Free Legal Services
landmark_case_1: Hussainara Khatoon v. State of Bihar
lc1_holding_summary: Free legal aid is a Fundamental Right; NALSA operationalises this constitutional mandate
landmark_case_2: Suk Das v. Union Territory of Arunachal Pradesh
lc2_holding_summary: Conviction without legal aid is unconstitutional; right to legal aid is part of fair trial under Article 21
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: PM WANI Scheme and Telecom Act 2023
title: Telecommunications Act 2023
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to consolidate and amend the laws governing telecommunications services, telecommunication networks and infrastructure, spectrum management and assignment, and matters connected therewith. It replaces the Indian Telegraph Act 1885, Wireless Telegraphy Act 1933, and Telegraph Wires (Unlawful Possession) Act 1950
simplified_explanation: Replaced 138-year-old colonial telecom law. Key changes: biometric-based SIM verification; government can take over telecom services during national security emergencies; appointment of Telecom Regulatory Authority of India (TRAI) streamlined; spectrum assigned by auction (default) or administrative process; right of way for telecom infrastructure simplified.
keywords: Telecom Act 2023,TRAI,Spectrum,SIM Verification,National Security,Telecom Infrastructure
landmark_case_1: TRAI v. Reliance Industries Limited
lc1_holding_summary: TRAI's regulatory powers over telecom tariffs are statutory and binding on all operators
landmark_case_2: Supreme Court Spectrum Allocation Case (2G)
lc2_holding_summary: Spectrum is a national resource; allocation must be through transparent auction process
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: POCSO (Amendment) Act 2019
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (Amendment) Act 2019 enhanced punishments for sexual offences against children, including death penalty for penetrative sexual assault on a child below 12 years and a minimum punishment of 20 years for aggravated penetrative sexual assault
simplified_explanation: Enhanced deterrence: death penalty for penetrative sexual assault on children below 12 years. Storage and distribution of child pornography (CSAM) now explicitly an offence. Child-friendly court procedures strengthened. Fast-track special courts for POCSO cases under fast-track courts scheme. False complaint provisions added to prevent misuse.
keywords: POCSO Amendment 2019,Child Sexual Abuse,Death Penalty,CSAM,Fast Track Courts,Child Protection
landmark_case_1: Alakh Alok Srivastava v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: SC directed creation of fast-track courts for POCSO cases; monitoring mechanism established
landmark_case_2: State of Karnataka v. Krishnappa
lc2_holding_summary: Sexual violence against children is a serious crime; courts must award exemplary punishment to deter
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry
legal_classification: Section-specific; generally cognizable / non-bailable for core offences

title: Surrogacy (Regulation) Act 2021
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to constitute a National Surrogacy Board, State Surrogacy Boards and appointment of appropriate authorities for regulation of the practice and process of surrogacy in India and to prohibit commercial surrogacy and to allow ethical surrogacy to needy infertile couples and for matters connected therewith
simplified_explanation: Prohibits commercial surrogacy — only altruistic surrogacy (by close relative) allowed. Surrogate mother must be a married woman between 25-35 years with own child. Surrogate cannot donate her own gametes. Insurance coverage for surrogate for 36 months. National Surrogacy Board for oversight. Penalties for commercial surrogacy.
keywords: Surrogacy Act,Altruistic Surrogacy,Commercial Surrogacy Banned,Surrogate Mother,National Surrogacy Board
landmark_case_1: Baby Manji Yamada v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Courts recognized the lacuna in surrogacy law; directed government to enact regulatory framework
landmark_case_2: Jan Balaz v. Anand Municipality
lc2_holding_summary: Citizenship and legal parentage issues in surrogacy require clear statutory framework — led to Surrogacy Act
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Assisted Reproductive Technology (Regulation) Act 2021
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to provide for the regulation and supervision of the assisted reproductive technology clinics and assisted reproductive technology banks, prevention of misuse, safe and ethical practice of assisted reproductive technology services and for matters connected therewith or incidental thereto
simplified_explanation: Regulates IVF clinics, sperm/egg banks, and ART procedures. National and State ART Boards and Registries. Mandatory registration of clinics and banks. Pre-implantation genetic testing regulated. Donor age limits: egg donors 23-35 years, sperm donors 21-55 years. Maximum 5 donations per donor. Prohibition on sex selection.
keywords: ART Act,IVF,Sperm Bank,Egg Donation,Assisted Reproduction,Pre-implantation Genetic Testing
landmark_case_1: Baby Manji Yamada v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: ART requires regulatory framework; rights of child born through ART must be protected
landmark_case_2: K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Reproductive autonomy is part of right to privacy and personal liberty under Article 21
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice

title: Ancient Monuments and Archaeological Sites and Remains (Amendment and Validation) Act 2010
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Amendment Act prohibits all construction activity in 'prohibited area' (within 100 metres of a protected monument) and restricts construction in the 'regulated area' (100-300 metres from a monument)
simplified_explanation: Strengthened protection of ASI-protected monuments after controversy over commercial construction near protected sites. The National Monuments Authority (NMA) constituted to grant permissions in regulated areas. Heritage Bye-laws for each monument. Challenged constructions near Qutub Minar, Taj Mahal, and Red Fort brought protection gaps to light.
keywords: Ancient Monuments,ASI,Prohibited Area,Regulated Area,NMA,Heritage Protection,100 Metres
landmark_case_1: M.I. Builders Pvt Ltd v. Radhey Shyam Sahu
lc1_holding_summary: No construction permitted that damages protected monuments; courts have wide power to order demolition
landmark_case_2: Tarun Bharat Sangh v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Heritage sites must be protected from all encroachments; NMA must actively enforce monument protection zones
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Specific Relief (Amendment) Act 2018
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Amendment Act to the Specific Relief Act 1963 makes specific performance of contracts the rule (not the exception) and damages the exception for infrastructure contracts. It substituted substituted performance, limited defences, and introduced provisions for infrastructure projects
simplified_explanation: Major change: Courts can now enforce specific performance of contracts in most cases (earlier discretionary in India unlike UK/USA). For infrastructure projects, courts cannot grant injunctions that would obstruct completion. Substituted performance — if party defaults, other party can get work done through third party at defaulter's cost.
keywords: Specific Relief,Contract Enforcement,Infrastructure,Injunction,Specific Performance Rule,Substituted Performance
landmark_case_1: Adcon Electronics v. Axxon Bharat
lc1_holding_summary: Specific performance is discretionary remedy; amended Act makes it standard for contracts except personal service
landmark_case_2: NHAI v. Progressive MVP Joint Venture
lc2_holding_summary: Post-amendment, courts are reluctant to grant injunctions in infrastructure projects; specific performance is preferred
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Prafulla Kumar Mukherjee v. Bank of Commerce Ltd (Privy Council)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The true nature and character of the legislation must be examined and the dominant purpose identified. The mere fact that a law incidentally encroaches upon subjects in another list does not render it invalid — Privy Council 1946
simplified_explanation: Established the foundational Pith and Substance doctrine for resolving Centre-State legislative conflicts. A Bengal moneylending law was challenged as trenching on the Union List promissory notes subject. Privy Council held that pith and substance was moneylending (State List); encroachment on promissory notes was only incidental.
keywords: Pith and Substance,Privy Council,Bengal Moneylending,Legislative Competence,Seventh Schedule
landmark_case_1: Prafulla Kumar Mukherjee v. Bank of Commerce Ltd
lc1_holding_summary: Pith and substance doctrine established; incidental encroachment on another list does not invalidate a law
landmark_case_2: State of Bombay v. F.N. Balsara
lc2_holding_summary: Pith and substance of Bombay Prohibition Act is regulation of liquor — State List subject
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

title: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
verbatim_text_excerpt: The word 'law' in Article 21 means state-made law or enacted law, not the principles of natural justice or due process — Supreme Court 1950
simplified_explanation: First major constitutional case. 6-judge bench held Articles 19, 21, and 22 are independent and do not overlap. Procedure established by law need not be fair or just — any procedure enacted by legislature is sufficient. This narrow view was later completely overruled by Maneka Gandhi (1978) which held procedure must be fair, just, and reasonable.
keywords: A.K. Gopalan,Article 21,Procedure Established by Law,Preventive Detention,Overruled
landmark_case_1: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
lc1_holding_summary: Narrow view: Article 21 satisfied by any procedure established by law; Articles 19, 21, 22 are not interconnected — overruled by Maneka Gandhi
landmark_case_2: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Overruled AK Gopalan: Articles 14, 19, 21 form a golden triangle; procedure must be fair, just and reasonable
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: State of Madras v. Champakam Dorairajan
verbatim_text_excerpt: The order of the State of Madras reserving seats in medical and engineering colleges on the basis of religion, race, and caste is void as it is repugnant to Article 15(1) of the Constitution
simplified_explanation: First major case on reservations in education. SC held that state communal GO (reservation of seats by religion/community) violated Article 15(1) which prohibits discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth. This led to the First Constitutional Amendment (1951) adding Article 15(4) to permit special provisions for backward classes.
keywords: Article 15,Reservation,Education,Communal GO,First Amendment
landmark_case_1: State of Madras v. Champakam Dorairajan
lc1_holding_summary: Communal GO violates Article 15(1); led to 1st Constitutional Amendment adding Article 15(4)
landmark_case_2: M.R. Balaji v. State of Mysore
lc2_holding_summary: Reservation exceeding 50% is arbitrary; backward class definition must not be based solely on caste
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: State of West Bengal v. Subodh Gopal Bose
verbatim_text_excerpt: The right to property under Article 19(1)(f) and Article 31 must be read together. The State can acquire property but must pay adequate compensation — the amount fixed by law must not be illusory
simplified_explanation: Early property rights case that pushed Parliament to pass successive amendments (4th, 17th, 44th) to restrict judicial review of property laws. The tension between property rights and social reform (land redistribution) defined constitutional law for decades until the 44th Amendment removed property from Fundamental Rights in 1978.
keywords: Right to Property,Article 31,Compensation,Land Acquisition,Social Reform,Zamindari Abolition
landmark_case_1: State of West Bengal v. Subodh Gopal Bose
lc1_holding_summary: Compensation must be adequate; nominal or illusory amounts violate Article 31 — led to 4th Amendment
landmark_case_2: State of Bihar v. Kameshwar Singh
lc2_holding_summary: Zamindari Abolition Act protected under Article 31A; but compensation must not be illusory
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: In re the Berubari Union and Exchange of Enclaves
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Preamble alone cannot be used to cut down the plenary terms of the Articles of the Constitution. The cession of a part of India's territory requires an amendment under Article 368 and cannot be done by executive action or ordinary legislation alone
simplified_explanation: The President sought SC's advisory opinion on whether the India-Pakistan agreement on Berubari (Bengal territory) required constitutional amendment. SC held that cession of territory to a foreign country is not within Parliament's ordinary legislative power and requires constitutional amendment. This principle was applied in the Bangladesh LBA case (100th Amendment 2015).
keywords: Berubari,Territory Cession,Constitutional Amendment,Article 3,Article 368,Bangladesh
landmark_case_1: In Re Berubari Union
lc1_holding_summary: Territory cession requires constitutional amendment; Parliament cannot cede territory by ordinary legislation
landmark_case_2: 100th Constitutional Amendment
lc2_holding_summary: India-Bangladesh LBA implemented through constitutional amendment following Berubari principle
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: I.C. Golak Nath v. State of Punjab
verbatim_text_excerpt: Parliament has no power to abridge or take away any of the Fundamental Rights. The word 'amendment' in Article 368 cannot be interpreted to authorize Parliament to make a law in violation of Part III — Subba Rao CJ
simplified_explanation: 11-judge bench by 6:5 majority held that Parliament cannot amend Fundamental Rights. Constitutional amendments are 'laws' within Article 13. This created a major constitutional crisis. Parliament responded with the 24th Amendment (1971) which explicitly overruled Golak Nath. But the basic structure doctrine evolved from Kesavananda Bharati (1973) provides a permanent limit.
keywords: Golak Nath,Fundamental Rights,Constitutional Amendment,Article 13,Subba Rao,24th Amendment
landmark_case_1: I.C. Golak Nath v. State of Punjab
lc1_holding_summary: Parliament cannot amend Fundamental Rights; constitutional amendments are 'law' under Article 13 — overruled by 24th Amendment and Kesavananda
landmark_case_2: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
lc2_holding_summary: Parliament can amend any provision including Fundamental Rights but cannot destroy basic structure
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Indira Nehru Gandhi v. Raj Narain
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Allahabad High Court correctly set aside the Prime Minister's election on the ground of corrupt practices. However, the retrospective validation of the PM's election by the 39th Amendment violates the basic structure — the doctrine of free and fair elections and the rule of law
simplified_explanation: Allahabad HC had set aside PM Indira Gandhi's 1971 election on grounds of using government machinery for election campaign and corrupt practices. Emergency was declared before SC could hear appeal. The 39th Amendment validated her election retroactively. SC struck down the retroactive validation as destroying basic structure.
keywords: Indira Gandhi Election,Allahabad HC,39th Amendment,Free and Fair Elections,Basic Structure,Emergency
landmark_case_1: Indira Nehru Gandhi v. Raj Narain
lc1_holding_summary: 39th Amendment struck down — retroactive validation of PM's election destroys basic structure of free and fair elections
landmark_case_2: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
lc2_holding_summary: Basic structure doctrine applied — free and fair elections is a basic feature of the Constitution
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice

title: ADM Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla (Habeas Corpus Case)
verbatim_text_excerpt: During a Proclamation of Emergency, no person has any locus standi to move any writ petition before a High Court for habeas corpus — Majority
simplified_explanation: The most criticised Supreme Court judgment. During Internal Emergency (1975-77), government detainees filed habeas corpus petitions. SC by 4:1 majority (only Justice H.R. Khanna dissenting) held that even the right to life under Article 21 can be suspended during Emergency. Overruled by K.S. Puttaswamy (2017) which held Article 21 is non-derogable.
keywords: ADM Jabalpur,Habeas Corpus,Emergency,Article 21,H.R. Khanna,Overruled
landmark_case_1: ADM Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla
lc1_holding_summary: Worst SC judgment: Right to life suspended during Emergency; 4:1 majority — only Khanna J dissented; effectively overruled
landmark_case_2: K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: ADM Jabalpur overruled; right to life and liberty cannot be suspended even during Emergency
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India (Mandal Case)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The total reservations including those already provided under Article 16(4) and those to be provided under Article 16(4) shall in no case exceed 50% of appointments in any year — Nine judge bench
simplified_explanation: The most comprehensive judgment on reservations. 9-judge bench upheld 27% OBC reservation; imposed 50% cap on total reservations; held creamy layer must be excluded from OBC benefits; barred reservation in promotions; allowed separate consideration for most backward among OBCs. Shaped reservation law for three decades.
keywords: Mandal Commission,OBC Reservation,50% Cap,Creamy Layer,Indra Sawhney,27% Reservation
landmark_case_1: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: 27% OBC reservation upheld; 50% cap; creamy layer excluded; no reservation in promotion for OBCs
landmark_case_2: Jarnail Singh v. Lachhmi Narain Gupta
lc2_holding_summary: Creamy layer applies to SC/ST promotions; quantifiable data requirement for promotion reservation relaxed
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
verbatim_text_excerpt: The contents of the fundamental rights guaranteed in our Constitution, guarantee the gender equality and an injury to gender equality is an injury to the human rights of women. The employer is under a duty to protect the women employees from sexual harassment — Verma CJ
simplified_explanation: Bhanwari Devi, a social worker in Rajasthan, was gang-raped for preventing a child marriage. Her perpetrators were acquitted. Vishaka NGO filed PIL. SC issued detailed guidelines making sexual harassment at workplace a Fundamental Rights violation under Articles 14, 15, 19, 21. Vishaka Guidelines had force of law until POSH Act 2013.
keywords: Vishaka,Sexual Harassment,Workplace,Bhanwari Devi,POSH Act,Gender Equality,Article 21
landmark_case_1: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
lc1_holding_summary: Sexual harassment at workplace violates Articles 14, 19, 21; Vishaka Guidelines issued as binding interim law
landmark_case_2: Medha Kotwal Lele v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: SC monitored compliance with Vishaka Guidelines; poor implementation led to enactment of POSH Act 2013
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. Union of India
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Precautionary Principle and the Polluter Pays Principle are essential features of Sustainable Development. The burden of proof is on the industry to show that its action is environmentally benign — Supreme Court 1996
simplified_explanation: PIL against tanneries in Tamil Nadu polluting rivers and soil. SC held that the Precautionary Principle (prevent potential harm even under uncertainty) and Polluter Pays Principle are part of Indian environmental law through Article 21 and Article 48A, and are consistent with international environmental law under Article 51(c).
keywords: Precautionary Principle,Polluter Pays,Sustainable Development,Tanneries,Article 48A,Environmental Law
landmark_case_1: Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Precautionary Principle and Polluter Pays are Indian constitutional law; burden of proof shifts to industry
