landmark_case_2: Indian Council for Enviro-Legal Action v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Absolute liability for industrial pollution; Polluter Pays includes cost of restoring damaged environment
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

title: Bijoe Emmanuel v. State of Kerala (National Anthem Case)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The right to freedom of conscience and religious belief guaranteed by Article 25 includes the right to remain silent when the National Anthem is sung if the person's religion forbids them from singing it — Supreme Court 1986
simplified_explanation: Three children of Jehovah's Witnesses stood up respectfully when the National Anthem was played in school but did not sing it because their religion prohibited them from singing to anyone other than God. SC held that standing up (showing respect) was sufficient — they could not be expelled for not singing.
keywords: National Anthem,Freedom of Conscience,Article 25,Jehovah's Witnesses,Right to Silence
landmark_case_1: Bijoe Emmanuel v. State of Kerala
lc1_holding_summary: Standing during National Anthem is sufficient; Article 25 protects right not to sing the anthem on religious grounds
landmark_case_2: Shyam Narayan Chouksey v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: National Anthem must be shown respect but coercive measures (like mandatory standing in cinemas) are arbitrary

title: T.N. Seshan v. Union of India
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Election Commission has the plenary power under Article 324 to ensure free and fair elections. It can issue directions and enforce them without specific statutory authority. The power of superintendence, direction and control is complete in itself — Supreme Court 1995
simplified_explanation: T.N. Seshan, as CEC, asserted ECI's independence and wide powers under Article 324. SC confirmed that Article 324 gives ECI all necessary powers for free and fair elections including enforcement of Model Code of Conduct, power to postpone elections, and power to cancel elections if vitiating.
keywords: Article 324,Election Commission,T.N. Seshan,Free and Fair Elections,Model Code of Conduct,Plenary Powers
landmark_case_1: T.N. Seshan v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Article 324 gives ECI complete and plenary power over elections; power is not confined by existing laws
landmark_case_2: Union of India v. Association for Democratic Reforms
lc2_holding_summary: ECI can direct disclosure of criminal antecedents and assets by candidates under Article 324
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice

title: Olga Tellis v. Bombay Municipal Corporation
verbatim_text_excerpt: The right to livelihood is an integral component of the right to life under Article 21. A person who is deprived of their livelihood is deprived of life itself. Pavement dwellers cannot be evicted without providing alternative accommodation — Chandrachud CJ
simplified_explanation: Pavement dwellers in Mumbai filed PILs challenging eviction orders. SC held right to livelihood is part of right to life under Article 21. State cannot evict the poor without providing alternative accommodation. However, the state's obligation to clear pavements for public use was also recognized — balancing two constitutional concerns.
keywords: Right to Livelihood,Article 21,Pavement Dwellers,Eviction,Alternative Accommodation,Urban Poor
landmark_case_1: Olga Tellis v. Bombay Municipal Corporation
lc1_holding_summary: Right to livelihood is part of Article 21; pavement dwellers cannot be evicted without rehabilitation
landmark_case_2: Chameli Singh v. State of UP
lc2_holding_summary: Right to shelter is a Fundamental Right; right to life includes right to decent housing
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Unni Krishnan v. State of Andhra Pradesh
verbatim_text_excerpt: The right to education up to the age of 14 years is a Fundamental Right under Article 21 read with Article 45 of the Constitution. Beyond 14 years, it is subject to the limits of State's economic capacity — Supreme Court 1993
simplified_explanation: Before the 86th Amendment (2002), SC derived right to education from Article 21. Held that charging capitation fees for admission to professional colleges was unconstitutional as it denied equal access to education. The 86th Amendment later gave explicit constitutional status to elementary education as a Fundamental Right under Article 21A.
keywords: Right to Education,Article 21,Article 45,Capitation Fee,86th Amendment,Unni Krishnan
landmark_case_1: Unni Krishnan v. State of AP
lc1_holding_summary: Right to education up to 14 years is a Fundamental Right derived from Article 21; capitation fees are unconstitutional
landmark_case_2: Miss Mohini Jain v. State of Karnataka
lc2_holding_summary: Capitation fees in educational institutions are unconstitutional; right to education is a Fundamental Right

title: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
verbatim_text_excerpt: Federalism is a basic feature of our Constitution. The floor of the House is the only forum for testing majority. The President's satisfaction for invoking Article 356 must be based on objective materials, not subjective satisfaction alone — Nine judge bench
simplified_explanation: President's Rule imposed in several states (Karnataka, Meghalaya, Mizoram, Nagaland, Rajasthan, Himachal Pradesh) was challenged. SC's landmark 9-judge decision: federalism is basic structure; President's Rule is subject to judicial review; government cannot be dismissed merely to serve the ruling party's interests; floor test is the only way to test majority.
keywords: SR Bommai,President's Rule,Article 356,Federalism,Basic Structure,Floor Test,Judicial Review
landmark_case_1: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Federalism is basic structure; President's Rule subject to judicial review; floor test mandatory before dismissal of government
landmark_case_2: Rameshwar Prasad v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Bihar President's Rule struck down as imposed to prevent majority party from forming government — applying SR Bommai

title: Supreme Court Advocates on Record Association v. Union of India (Second Judges Case)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The opinion of the Chief Justice of India formed after consultation with a collegium of judges should have primacy. The executive cannot override the judicial recommendation in matters of appointment and transfer of judges — Nine judge bench
simplified_explanation: Overruled S.P. Gupta (1981 — First Judges Case) where government's view prevailed in judicial appointments. Established the collegium system: for SC appointments, CJI must consult 2 senior-most judges; for HC appointments, CJI must consult 2 senior-most SC judges. Collegium's view is binding on the executive.
keywords: Second Judges Case,Collegium System,Judicial Appointments,CJI Primacy,Independence of Judiciary,NJAC
landmark_case_1: SCAORA v. Union of India (Second Judges Case)
lc1_holding_summary: Collegium system established; CJI's view on judicial appointments is binding on government
landmark_case_2: SCAORA v. Union of India (NJAC Case)
lc2_holding_summary: National Judicial Appointments Commission (99th Amendment) struck down; collegium system reaffirmed as basic structure

title: Francis Coralie Mullin v. Administrator
verbatim_text_excerpt: The right to live with human dignity, enshrined in Article 21 derives its life breath from the Directive Principles of State Policy. It includes the bare necessities of life such as adequate nutrition, clothing and shelter and facilities for reading, writing and expressing oneself in diverse forms — Bhagwati J
simplified_explanation: Prisoner filed petition challenging prison conditions. SC expansively interpreted Article 21 to include human dignity, bare necessities, and facilities for development. This case marked the beginning of systematic expansion of Article 21 to include socio-economic rights through judicial interpretation.
keywords: Francis Coralie,Human Dignity,Article 21,Prison Conditions,Bare Necessities,Socio-Economic Rights
landmark_case_1: Francis Coralie Mullin v. Administrator
lc1_holding_summary: Article 21 includes right to live with human dignity; prison conditions must respect this

lc2_holding_summary: Right to emergency medical treatment is a Fundamental Right under Article 21

title: Vineet Narain v. Union of India (Hawala Case)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The CBI and other investigative agencies must be free from political interference. No one, howsoever high he may be, is above the law — Supreme Court 1997
simplified_explanation: Investigations into hawala transactions implicating top politicians and bureaucrats were being suppressed. SC took suo motu cognizance based on journalist's petition. Monitored CBI investigation for 2 years. Directed CBI to be insulated from political pressure. Led to creation of CVC as statutory body and increased accountability of CBI.
keywords: Vineet Narain,Hawala Case,CBI Independence,Political Interference,Rule of Law,CVC
landmark_case_1: Vineet Narain v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: CBI must act without fear or favour; political executive cannot interfere in criminal investigation; CVC must be independent
landmark_case_2: Subramanian Swamy v. Director CBI
lc2_holding_summary: Right to investigate corruption must be free from political influence; prior sanction for prosecution cannot be used to shield corrupt officers

title: Dinesh Trivedi MP v. Union of India
verbatim_text_excerpt: The citizens of this country have a right to know about the affairs of the government. The concept of an open government is the direct emanation from the right to know — Supreme Court 2003
simplified_explanation: Pre-RTI Act case establishing right to information from constitutional sources. SC held that Article 19(1)(a) includes the right of citizens to know about government affairs. This judicial foundation paved the way for the Right to Information Act 2005.

lc1_holding_summary: Right to know is part of Article 19(1)(a); citizens have right to information about government affairs

title: Aruna Shanbaug v. Union of India
verbatim_text_excerpt: A person in a persistent vegetative state (PVS) can be allowed to die with dignity by withdrawing life-sustaining treatment. The concept of passive euthanasia is recognized. Active euthanasia remains impermissible — Supreme Court 2011
simplified_explanation: Aruna Shanbaug had been in PVS for 37 years after a brutal assault. Court recognized passive euthanasia (withdrawal of treatment) as legal under Article 21's right to die with dignity. Active euthanasia (administering lethal injection) remains illegal. Advanced directives ('living wills') were later fully recognized in Common Cause v. Union of India (2018).

lc1_holding_summary: Passive euthanasia is legal; guidelines for withdrawal of life support for PVS patients laid down
landmark_case_2: Common Cause v. Union of India

title: Government of NCT Delhi v. Union of India
verbatim_text_excerpt: The elected government of Delhi represents the will of the people. The Lieutenant Governor is bound by the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers except in matters specifically provided in the Constitution — Constitutional Bench 2018
simplified_explanation: Five-judge Constitution Bench resolved the LG-CM conflict in Delhi. Held that democratic governance demands that elected government has real power. LG cannot act contrary to Cabinet's advice in all matters (except Public Order, Police, Land). The principle of collective responsibility and constitutional trust must guide LG-CM relationship.

title: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India
verbatim_text_excerpt: Section 377 of BNS in so far as it criminalises consensual sexual conduct between adults of the same sex is unconstitutional as it violates the rights to liberty, dignity, autonomy, identity and equality guaranteed under Articles 14, 15, 19 and 21 — Five judge bench
simplified_explanation: Unanimously overruled Suresh Kumar Koushal (2013) which had reinstated Section 377. SC held that sexual orientation is a ground protected under Article 15; consensual adult relationships fall within the private sphere protected by Article 21; constitutional morality must prevail over social morality; history must be made to apologize to LGBTQ+ community.

title: K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (Privacy Case)
verbatim_text_excerpt: Privacy is a constitutionally protected right which emerges primarily from the guarantee of life and personal liberty in Article 21 of the Constitution. Privacy includes at its core the preservation of personal intimacies — Nine judge bench
simplified_explanation: Most comprehensive constitutional judgment of recent decades. 9-judge bench unanimously overruled M.P. Sharma (1954) and Kharak Singh (1962). Privacy recognized as a Fundamental Right. Covers informational privacy, bodily integrity, dignity, and personal autonomy. Justice D.Y. Chandrachud's concurrence provided the most expansive analysis including sexual orientation.

title: Joseph Shine v. Union of India

simplified_explanation: Section 497 BNS punished a man for having sex with another man's wife — only the man could complain, not the wife, and the woman was never punished. SC struck it down as patriarchal, violating dignity, equality (Articles 14, 15, 21), and the woman's sexual autonomy. Marriage does not merge woman's identity with husband's.

title: Lafarge Umiam Mining v. Union of India
verbatim_text_excerpt: Prior approval under Section 2 of the Forest Conservation Act 1980 is a mandatory condition precedent for any diversion of forest land. No authority can waive this requirement — Supreme Court 2011
simplified_explanation: Mining in Meghalaya was proceeding on what was claimed to be non-forest land. SC held that the land was forest, the company had violated FCA 1980 by not obtaining prior approval, and the entire process of mining permission was vitiated. title: Anuradha Bhasin v. Union of India
verbatim_text_excerpt: An order suspending internet access must satisfy the tests of necessity and proportionality. There can be no indefinite internet shutdown. Such orders must be reviewed periodically — Supreme Court 2020
simplified_explanation: Government had shut down internet in J&K for over 100 days following Article 370 abrogation. SC held that freedom of speech via internet is protected under Article 19(1)(a); internet shutdowns must be temporary, necessary, and proportionate; orders must be published; review mechanism mandatory. title: In Re: Cognizance for Extension of Limitation (COVID-19)
verbatim_text_excerpt: In extraordinary situations, the Supreme Court exercising its powers under Article 142 can extend the period of limitation to do complete justice — Supreme Court 2020
simplified_explanation: During COVID-19 pandemic, SC suo motu extended all statutes of limitation for legal proceedings from March 2020 until further orders, to ensure that no party loses rights due to the pandemic. This demonstrated the SC's extraordinary power under Article 142 (complete justice) and its role as guardian of rights in national emergencies.

title: Vikas Dubey Case — Encounter Deaths
verbatim_text_excerpt: The right to life under Article 21 applies even to persons accused of heinous crimes. Extrajudicial executions (fake encounters) violate Article 21 and the rule of law — Supreme Court 2021
simplified_explanation: SC took suo motu cognizance of the Vikas Dubey encounter case where a gangster and his associates were killed by police in allegedly staged encounters. Appointed SIT for investigation. Reaffirmed that even the most dangerous criminal is entitled to the right to life and a fair trial; police cannot be judge, jury, and executioner.
keywords: Encounter Deaths,Fake Encounter,Article 21,Extrajudicial Execution,Rule of Law,SIT Inquiry
landmark_case_1: People's Union for Civil Liberties v. State of Maharashtra
lc1_holding_summary: Guidelines for encounter killings — mandatory FIR, magisterial inquiry, and suspension of involved officers
landmark_case_2: PUCL v. State of UP
lc2_holding_summary: Vikas Dubey case SIT — encounter deaths require independent inquiry; Article 21 protects even accused

title: Janhit Abhiyan v. Union of India (EWS Reservation)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The 103rd Constitutional Amendment providing 10% reservation for Economically Weaker Sections does not violate the basic structure of the Constitution. It does not violate the equality code or the 50% ceiling rule — Three judge majority 2022
simplified_explanation: 5-judge bench upheld EWS reservation by 3:2 majority. Majority held: economic criterion alone can be the basis for classification; exclusion of SC/ST/OBC from EWS is not discrimination but recognition that they have their own reservation; 50% cap is a rule not an inviolable ceiling (dissent by Justices Trivedi and Bhat disagreed strongly).
keywords: EWS Reservation,103rd Amendment,Janhit Abhiyan,10% Reservation,Basic Structure,50% Cap
landmark_case_1: Janhit Abhiyan v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: EWS 10% reservation upheld 3:2; economic criterion valid for reservation; 50% cap can be crossed in exceptional circumstances
landmark_case_2: M. Nagaraj v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Reservation provisions must satisfy test of backwardness, inadequacy, and overall administrative efficiency
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Union of India v. Mohit Mineral Pvt Ltd (GST Council)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The recommendations of the GST Council are not binding on the Parliament of the Union or the State Legislatures. They are persuasive in nature. Both Parliament and State Legislatures have independent power to legislate under Article 246A — Supreme Court 2022
simplified_explanation: A critical ruling on cooperative federalism in GST. Centre had argued GST Council recommendations were binding on States. SC held otherwise — both Centre and States have independent taxing power under Article 246A (inserted by 101st Amendment); GST Council is a recommendatory body, not a mandatory one. This preserved fiscal federalism within the GST framework.
keywords: GST Council,Article 246A,Fiscal Federalism,Cooperative Federalism,Binding Recommendations,101st Amendment
landmark_case_1: Union of India v. Mohit Mineral Pvt Ltd
lc1_holding_summary: GST Council recommendations are persuasive not binding; Centre and States have equal and independent taxing power under Article 246A
landmark_case_2: State of Kerala v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: States can challenge Centre's fiscal decisions in SC under Article 131; fiscal federalism is justiciable
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

title: In Re Article 370 of the Constitution
verbatim_text_excerpt: Article 370 was a temporary provision. Jammu & Kashmir did not retain any element of sovereignty when it acceded to India. The President acting under Article 370(3) could abrogate the entire Article without the concurrence of the Constituent Assembly of J&K which had ceased to exist — Five judge bench 2023
simplified_explanation: SC upheld abrogation of Article 370 by a unanimous 5-judge bench. Key holdings: J&K's accession was complete and full; Article 370 was a transitional provision; after J&K Constituent Assembly dissolved, the President could abrogate Article 370; however, the downgrade of J&K to UT and separation of Ladakh as another UT were also upheld. Statehood for J&K to be restored at earliest.
keywords: Article 370,J&K,Abrogation,Temporary Provision,Sovereignty,Statehood Restoration
landmark_case_1: In Re Article 370
lc1_holding_summary: Article 370 abrogation upheld; J&K had no internal sovereignty; statehood to be restored at appropriate time; elections by September 2024 directed
landmark_case_2: Prem Nath Kaul v. State of J&K
lc2_holding_summary: Article 370 is transitional; J&K Constituent Assembly had limited power to determine extent of constitutional application
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Subhash Desai v. Principal Secretary Governor of Maharashtra (Shinde-Thackeray)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Speaker cannot decide disqualification petitions filed against members who have supported the removal of the Speaker. The constitutional scheme envisages a fair hearing — Five judge bench 2023
simplified_explanation: The Maharashtra political crisis saw rebel Shiv Sena MLAs led by Eknath Shinde claim the real Shiv Sena. SC held: Governor was wrong to call floor test; Speaker cannot hear disqualification petitions against members who moved his removal; issue of 'real party' must be decided by EC; but original government could not be restored as trust vote was held.
keywords: Maharashtra Political Crisis,10th Schedule,Anti-Defection,Speaker Bias,Floor Test,Eknath Shinde,Uddhav Thackeray
landmark_case_1: Subhash Desai v. Principal Secretary Governor of Maharashtra
lc1_holding_summary: Speaker cannot decide disqualification when facing removal; Governor cannot call floor test arbitrarily; EC decides party symbol disputes
landmark_case_2: Nabam Rebia v. Deputy Speaker
lc2_holding_summary: Speaker cannot hear disqualification petitions when a notice for their own removal is pending
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: State of Punjab v. Principal Secretary to Governor of Punjab
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Governor cannot indefinitely withhold assent to Bills passed by the State Legislature. The Governor's role is constitutional; they are not a free agent. Acting contrary to the constitutional scheme and withholding assent without any explanation is unconstitutional — Supreme Court 2023
simplified_explanation: Punjab Governor had withheld assent to 7 Bills passed by the state legislature for an indefinite period. SC held that under Article 200, the Governor cannot create a 'pocket veto' — indefinitely withholding assent without action is unconstitutional. Governor must either assent, withhold (with reasons once), or reserve for President's consideration within a reasonable time.
keywords: Governor Pocket Veto,Article 200,Assent,State Legislation,Governor vs State Government
landmark_case_1: State of Punjab v. Principal Secretary to Governor
lc1_holding_summary: Governor's indefinite withholding of assent is unconstitutional; must act within reasonable time under Article 200
landmark_case_2: In Re Article 200 of Constitution
lc2_holding_summary: SC issued comprehensive guidelines for Governor's exercise of powers under Article 200; pocket veto is impermissible
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Anoop Baranwal v. Union of India (Election Commissioner Appointment)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The appointment of the Chief Election Commissioner and Election Commissioners shall be made by the President on the advice of a Committee consisting of the Prime Minister, the Leader of Opposition, and the Chief Justice of India until Parliament enacts a law — Supreme Court 2023
simplified_explanation: SC 5-judge bench held that absence of a law governing ECI appointments created a constitutional vacuum. Invoked Articles 142 and 324 to fill this vacuum by directing that appointments be made by a 3-member committee until Parliament legislates. Parliament responded by passing the Chief Election Commissioner and Other Election Commissioners Act 2023 excluding the CJI from the committee.
keywords: Election Commissioner Appointment,Anoop Baranwal,Article 324,Committee,CJI,PM,Leader of Opposition,Independence of ECI
landmark_case_1: Anoop Baranwal v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: 3-member committee (PM, LOP, CJI) for ECI appointments directed; Parliament subsequently excluded CJI by legislation
landmark_case_2: T.N. Seshan v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Independence of ECI is essential for free and fair elections; institutional design must protect it

title: Sukanya Shantha v. Union of India (Prison Caste Discrimination)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Caste-based discrimination in prisons through classification of prison work, segregation of barracks, and differential treatment of prisoners based on caste, as found in various Prison Manuals, is unconstitutional and violates Articles 14, 15, 17, 21, and 23 of the Constitution — Supreme Court 2024
simplified_explanation: A journalist's article exposed caste discrimination in Rajasthan's prison manual (specifying that Balmikis should clean toilets; caste determines prison work allocation; separate eating areas). SC struck down these provisions in all state prison manuals. Directed states to revise prison manuals within 3 months to eliminate caste-based discrimination.
keywords: Prison Caste Discrimination,Article 17,Untouchability,Prison Manual,Caste Classification,Article 15
landmark_case_1: Sukanya Shantha v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Caste discrimination in prison manuals is unconstitutional; violates Articles 14, 15, 17, 21, 23; states directed to amend all prison manuals
landmark_case_2: Sunil Batra v. Delhi Administration
lc2_holding_summary: Prisoners retain their Fundamental Rights; barbaric prison conditions violate Article 21

title: Abhijit Iyer-Mitra v. Union of India (Same-Sex Marriage)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution does not recognise a fundamental right to marry. The right to choose a partner is a fundamental right but the right to have that relationship legally recognised as marriage is not — Supreme Court 2023
simplified_explanation: 5-judge bench by 3:2 majority refused to read a right to same-sex marriage into the Constitution. Held that marriage is a statutory/social institution; right to marry is not a Fundamental Right; legislature must decide on same-sex marriage recognition. However, SC directed government to constitute a committee to examine rights of same-sex couples (joint accounts, adoption, etc.).
keywords: Same-Sex Marriage,LGBTQ,Right to Marry,Fundamental Rights,Special Marriage Act,Parliament
landmark_case_1: Supriyo v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: No fundamental right to same-sex marriage; legislature must decide; government committee to examine rights of same-sex couples
landmark_case_2: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Decriminalization of homosexuality; dignity and autonomy of LGBTQ+ persons are Fundamental Rights
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: M.K. Ranjitsinh v. Union of India (Climate Change)
verbatim_text_excerpt: Every person has a right against the adverse effects of climate change. The State has an obligation to protect this right. The right to a clean environment and the right to be free from the adverse effects of climate change are part of the right to life and equality — Supreme Court 2024
simplified_explanation: A case on protection of the Great Indian Bustard (GIB) in Rajasthan — underground power cables were being directed to protect birds from overhead lines. SC made a landmark declaration that the right to be free from adverse effects of climate change is a Fundamental Right under Articles 14 and 21. First explicit recognition of climate rights in Indian constitutional law.
keywords: Climate Change,Fundamental Right,Article 21,Great Indian Bustard,Clean Environment,Climate Justice
landmark_case_1: M.K. Ranjitsinh v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Right against adverse effects of climate change is a Fundamental Right under Articles 14 and 21 — first express recognition
landmark_case_2: Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Sustainable development and environmental rights derived from Article 21 — precursor to climate rights

title: Babu v. State of Kerala (Caste Certificate Misuse)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The purpose of the reservation policy is to uplift the marginalized; it is not to perpetuate benefits for those who have crossed the threshold of backwardness. Fraudulent caste certificates destroy the constitutionally mandated purpose of reservations — Supreme Court 2024
simplified_explanation: A systematic examination of caste certificate fraud where persons from non-SC/ST communities obtained SC/ST certificates to avail reservation benefits. SC directed states to have robust verification mechanisms; directed cancellation of fraudulent appointments; held that obtaining fake caste certificates is a fraud on the Constitution.
keywords: Caste Certificate,Reservation Fraud,SC/ST,Verification Mechanism,Constitutional Purpose
landmark_case_1: Madhuri Patil v. Additional Commissioner
lc1_holding_summary: Caste certificate scrutiny committees are mandatory; Vigilance Cell must investigate false certificates
landmark_case_2: Kavita Solunke v. State of Maharashtra
lc2_holding_summary: Obtaining false caste certificate for reservation is a fraud on Constitution; employment must be cancelled

title: Saurav Yadav v. State of UP (Women Reservation in Police)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The State can provide horizontal reservation for women across all verticals (SC, ST, OBC, General) in police recruitment. This ensures that women from all social categories are represented — Supreme Court 2021
simplified_explanation: Clarified the concept of horizontal and vertical reservations in public employment. Vertical reservations are for SC/ST/OBC. Horizontal reservations (like for women, ex-servicemen, disabled) cut across all verticals. Proper application: first fill vertical quota seats, then apply horizontal within each vertical, not across the total.
keywords: Horizontal Reservation,Vertical Reservation,Women Reservation,Police Recruitment,Article 16
landmark_case_1: Saurav Yadav v. State of UP
lc1_holding_summary: Women's horizontal reservation must be applied within each vertical category; not across overall seats
landmark_case_2: Anil Kumar Gupta v. State of UP
lc2_holding_summary: Horizontal reservations apply across categories; must be applied carefully to avoid violating either category
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Prabha Tyagi v. Kamlesh Devi (Domestic Violence)
verbatim_text_excerpt: A woman who has been in a 'domestic relationship' as defined under Section 2(f) of the PWDVA can seek protection under the Act even if she is no longer living in the shared household — Supreme Court 2022
simplified_explanation: Resolved a critical gap in domestic violence law: women who had left the shared household due to abuse could still seek remedies under PWDVA. SC held that the Act's protection is not lost by leaving the abusive situation. Also held that a woman who has never lived with a man but is in an intimate relationship of sufficient permanence can access the Act.

landmark_case_1: Aruna Parmod Shah v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: PWDVA's provisions must be liberally interpreted to protect women from all forms of violence
landmark_case_2: Indra Sarma v. V.K.V. Sarma
lc2_holding_summary: Live-in relationships of sufficient duration and permanence covered by PWDVA; children of such relationships are legitimate

title: In Re Illegal Mining Chhattisgarh
verbatim_text_excerpt: The right to a healthy environment is a Fundamental Right under Articles 14, 21 and 48A. Illegal mining not only destroys the environment but also deprives local communities, especially tribals, of their constitutional rights — Supreme Court 2024
simplified_explanation: SC took suo motu cognizance of rampant illegal mining in Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, and other states. Constituted Central Empowered Committee (CEC) for monitoring. Directed environmental restoration fund. Held that revenue-sharing cannot justify illegal extraction; states cannot waive environmental standards for royalty revenues.
keywords: Illegal Mining,Environmental Rights,Tribal Rights,CEC,Environmental Restoration,Article 21
landmark_case_1: Lafarge Umiam Mining v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Mining in forest areas requires stringent compliance with FCA and environmental laws
landmark_case_2: Samatha v. State of AP

title: Vijaya Surendra Choudhary v. Union of India (Marital Rape)
verbatim_text_excerpt: Whether the exception in Section 375 BNS (now Section 63 BNS) that exempts a husband from rape charges for sexual intercourse with his wife is constitutional — Issue under consideration in Delhi HC and SC
simplified_explanation: Delhi HC split verdict (2022): Justice Rajiv Shakdher held marital rape exception unconstitutional (violates Articles 14, 15, 21); Justice Hari Shankar upheld it (legislative domain, societal values). SC is hearing the matter. Critical question: Does marriage extinguish a woman's sexual autonomy? Constitutional morality demands affirmative answer.
keywords: Marital Rape,Section 375 BNS,Section 63 BNS,Sexual Autonomy,Article 21,Marriage Exception

lc1_holding_summary: HC split on marital rape exception — SC to decide; strong constitutional argument that exception violates bodily autonomy

lc2_holding_summary: Marriage does not deprive women of autonomy; constitutional morality requires gender equality in personal law
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Aligarh Muslim University Minority Status Case
verbatim_text_excerpt: Whether the Aligarh Muslim University (AMU), established by Parliament under the AMU Act 1920, qualifies as a minority educational institution under Article 30(1) — Seven judge bench overruling S. Azeez Basha (1967)
simplified_explanation: 7-judge bench by 4:3 majority overruled Azeez Basha (1967 — which had held AMU is not a minority institution as it was established by Parliamentary legislation). New test: the character of an institution is determined by who established it — if a minority community established it and gave it to the State for administration, it retains minority character.
keywords: AMU,Minority Institution,Article 30,Aligarh Muslim University,Azeez Basha,Minority Character
landmark_case_1: Aligarh Muslim University v. Naresh Agarwal
lc1_holding_summary: AMU is a minority institution; Azeez Basha overruled; test for minority institution is who established it, not whether it has statutory backing
landmark_case_2: T.M.A. Pai Foundation v. State of Karnataka
lc2_holding_summary: Minority status determined by religion or language; administrative structure does not affect minority character

title: Re: Welfare of Persons with Disabilities
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act 2016 must be read with Article 21 to ensure substantive equality for persons with disabilities. Reasonable accommodation is a constitutional requirement, not merely statutory — Supreme Court 2024
simplified_explanation: A series of SC orders directing implementation of RPwD Act 2016 (which replaced PWD Act 1995, implementing UNCRPD). Key rulings: barrier-free access is a Fundamental Right; elections must be accessible to all including PWDs; public examinations must provide reasonable accommodation; government schemes must include PWD-specific components.
keywords: Disability Rights,RPwD Act,Reasonable Accommodation,UNCRPD,Substantive Equality,Barrier-Free Access
landmark_case_1: Jeeja Ghosh v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: PWD has right to travel with dignity; forcible deboarding from flight is unconstitutional; disability is a diversity characteristic
landmark_case_2: Vikash Kumar v. UPSC
lc2_holding_summary: UPSC must provide scribes and compensatory time for candidates with disabilities; denial violates Article 21

title: State of Tamil Nadu v. Governor of Tamil Nadu
verbatim_text_excerpt: A Governor who has withheld assent to Bills without communicating reasons and without returning them for reconsideration has acted unconstitutionally. Article 200 does not permit indefinite inaction — Supreme Court 2024
simplified_explanation: Tamil Nadu Governor had withheld assent to 10 Bills including higher education Bills for months/years. SC held: (1) Governor must act with dispatch; (2) withholding without reasons is unconstitutional; (3) the Bills are deemed assented if Governor acts contrary to constitutional mandate; (4) Governor's role is constitutional, not partisan. 10 Bills deemed to have received assent.
keywords: Tamil Nadu Governor,Article 200,Bills Assent,Constitutional Role,Governor vs CM,Deemed Assent

lc1_holding_summary: SC deemed 10 Bills to have received Presidential assent; Governor cannot act as partisan political agent
landmark_case_2: State of Punjab v. Principal Secretary to Governor

title: Quo Warranto — Leading Cases
verbatim_text_excerpt: By what authority? A writ of quo warranto is issued to inquire into the authority by which a person claims to hold a public office. If the person is found to be holding the office without lawful authority, they can be removed
simplified_explanation: Quo Warranto (by what authority) can be issued against a person holding a public office to show by what authority they hold it. Conditions: (1) The office must be a public office; (2) The office must be created by statute or Constitution; (3) The person must be holding it without legal authority. Cannot be issued against private offices.
keywords: Quo Warranto,Public Office,Statutory Office,Ouster,Writ
landmark_case_1: University of Mysore v. C.D. Govinda Rao
lc1_holding_summary: Quo warranto lies against appointment to public office made in violation of statutory qualifications
landmark_case_2: G.D. Karkare v. T.L. Shevde
lc2_holding_summary: Quo warranto requires that the office is a public one of a substantive character created by Constitution or statute
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Prohibition and Certiorari — Distinguished
verbatim_text_excerpt: A writ of prohibition prevents an inferior court or tribunal from exceeding its jurisdiction or acting in excess of jurisdiction. A writ of certiorari removes the record of a decided case to quash a decision already made by an inferior court or tribunal
simplified_explanation: Prohibition = prevents (before decision); Certiorari = quashes (after decision). Both lie for excess of jurisdiction, error of law apparent on face of record, violation of natural justice. Cannot be issued against legislative bodies. High Courts under Article 226 have wider certiorari jurisdiction than traditional common law.
keywords: Prohibition,Certiorari,Inferior Court,Jurisdiction,Natural Justice,Judicial Review
landmark_case_1: T.C. Basappa v. T. Nagappa
lc1_holding_summary: Certiorari under Article 226 is wider than common law certiorari; lies for any error of law apparent on record
landmark_case_2: Hari Vishnu Kamath v. Ahmad Ishaque
lc2_holding_summary: Certiorari lies for jurisdictional errors; error must appear on the face of the record to be quashed
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: National Commission for Women — Leading Cases
verbatim_text_excerpt: The National Commission for Women Act 1990 establishes NCW to review constitutional and legal safeguards for women, recommend remedial legislative measures, facilitate redressal of grievances, and advise the Government on all policy matters affecting women
simplified_explanation: NCW is a statutory body (not constitutional like NHRC). Key functions: take suo motu notice of cases of atrocity against women, advise on legislative matters, conduct special studies on constitutional safeguards. Has examined cases of rape, trafficking, divorce law, and workplace harassment. Its recommendations are persuasive on government.
keywords: NCW,National Commission for Women,Women Rights,Statutory Body,Policy Recommendations
landmark_case_1: Medha Kotwal Lele v. Union of India

title: Central Information Commission — Leading Cases
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Central Information Commission is established under Section 12 of the RTI Act 2005 to adjudicate appeals and complaints against refusal of information by public authorities. The CIC's orders are binding on public authorities.
simplified_explanation: CIC is the apex appellate body under RTI Act. Chief Information Commissioner (now limited to 3-year non-renewable term after 2019 amendment) leads it. Key RTI precedents: Cabinet notes are disclosable after final decision; file notings disclosable; personal information of public servants related to their public duties is disclosable; political parties are debated to be public authorities.

title: Ram Kishore Arora v. Union of India (Enforcement Directorate)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Directorate of Enforcement is a specialized investigation agency. The Director's tenure protection is necessary for ensuring institutional independence and effective investigation of money laundering and foreign exchange violations — Supreme Court 2023
simplified_explanation: Petitions challenged repeated extensions of ED Director's tenure beyond 2 years by the Central government. SC upheld fixed-term tenure extensions in exceptional circumstances under statutory provisions but emphasized that this should not become a norm. ED Director's independence is essential for effective anti-money laundering enforcement.

title: Re: Sedition Law (S.G. Vombatkere)
verbatim_text_excerpt: Section 152 (formerly 124A) BNS (Sedition) was challenged as unconstitutional for being vague, overbroad, and chilling free speech. The SC stayed all sedition proceedings in May 2022 pending constitutional review — SC 2022-2024
simplified_explanation: The Supreme Court in May 2022 stayed all sedition cases across India pending review of Section 152 (formerly 124A) BNS. The government indicated willingness to re-examine the law. The new BNS 2023 deleted Section 152 (formerly 124A) and replaced it with Section 152 (acts endangering sovereignty, unity, integrity) which is arguably broader. The original challenge became infructuous.

title: Kaushal Kishor v. State of Uttar Pradesh (Ministers' Free Speech)
verbatim_text_excerpt: A Minister's statement which is not made in their official capacity or on behalf of the government does not attract vicarious liability of the State. However, such statements may violate constitutional obligations of holders of high constitutional offices — Supreme Court 2023
simplified_explanation: A UP Minister made statements blaming gang-rape victim for the crime. Court held: State is not vicariously liable for a minister's personal statements; BUT ministers hold constitutional offices and have enhanced obligations not to make statements that violate Fundamental Rights; statements hostile to constitutional values by ministers are a constitutional concern.

landmark_case_2: Pravasi Bhalai Sangathan v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Hate speech by public officials is a serious constitutional concern requiring regulatory response

title: Re: Validity of Electoral Bonds Scheme
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Electoral Bonds Scheme of 2018 violates the right to information under Article 19(1)(a) and the principle of free and fair elections under Article 324. Voters have a right to know the source of political party funding — Supreme Court 2024
simplified_explanation: SC unanimously struck down the Electoral Bonds Scheme. Key findings: anonymous political funding violates voters' right to information about the source of political funds; it creates quid pro quo possibilities (donating companies getting government contracts/licenses); the scheme enables unlimited corporate funding to political parties which distorts elections.

title: Bilkis Bano v. Union of India (Remission of Convicts)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The exercise of remission powers by the State must be in accordance with law. Remission cannot be granted by a State which is not the appropriate government for the case. Remission of sentence of persons convicted of gang-rape and murder requires consideration of all statutory factors — Supreme Court 2023
simplified_explanation: Gujarat government prematurely released 11 convicts in the 2002 Bilkis Bano gang-rape and murder case through remission. SC quashed the remission: Gujarat was not the 'appropriate government' (trial was in Maharashtra); the remission committee did not follow the guidelines; serious offences against women cannot be treated leniently.

landmark_case_1: Bilkis Yakub Rasool v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Remission quashed — Gujarat had no jurisdiction; remission committee did not apply correct criteria; gravity of offence must be considered
landmark_case_2: Maru Ram v. Union of India
