title: Doctrine of Constitutional Trust and Dhruv Rathee Principle
verbatim_text_excerpt: Constitutional functionaries — Governors, Presidents, Ministers — hold their office in trust for the people and the Constitution. They must exercise their powers in furtherance of constitutional values and not for partisan purposes. Abuse of constitutional office betrays this trust
simplified_explanation: Constitutional Trust is not an explicit doctrine but is implicit throughout constitutional jurisprudence. The Governor's discretionary powers, the President's constitutional role, and the Speaker's quasi-judicial functions are all fiduciary responsibilities. Abuse of these powers — like indefinitely withholding assent, calling a floor test to topple a government, or using legislative privileges arbitrarily — are betrayals of constitutional trust.
keywords: Constitutional Trust,Governor Powers,President Powers,Speaker Role,Fiduciary Duty,Constitutional Office
landmark_case_1: Nabam Rebia v. Deputy Speaker
lc1_holding_summary: Governor cannot act as partisan agent; constitutional offices carry fiduciary obligations to Constitution
landmark_case_2: Rameshwar Prasad v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Governor's imposition of President's Rule without objective material is a betrayal of constitutional trust
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: All India Services and Central Services
verbatim_text_excerpt: Parliament may by law regulate the recruitment, and the conditions of service of persons appointed, to public services and posts in connection with the affairs of the Union or of any State. There shall be constituted the following All India Services, namely the Indian Administrative Service, the Indian Police Service
simplified_explanation: All India Services (IAS, IPS, IFS) are jointly controlled by Centre and States. Centre controls recruitment (through UPSC), training, and service rules; States control posting and day-to-day work. Article 312 enables Parliament to create new All India Services if Rajya Sabha passes a resolution by 2/3 majority. Central Services (IRS, IDAS, etc.) are exclusively under Centre.
keywords: All India Services,IAS,IPS,IFS,Article 312,Central Services,UPSC,Recruitment,Joint Control
landmark_case_1: State of UP v. Chandra Mohan Nigam
lc1_holding_summary: All India Services officers serve both Centre and States; their service conditions governed by Central rules
landmark_case_2: P. Sirajuddin v. State of Madras
lc2_holding_summary: IAS officer's posting is state subject but disciplinary proceedings governed by Central rules; dual control principle
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Dismissal, removal or reduction in rank of persons employed in civil capacities
verbatim_text_excerpt: No person who is a member of a civil service of the Union or an all-India service or a civil service of a State or holds a civil post under the Union or a State shall be dismissed or removed by an authority subordinate to that by which he was appointed
simplified_explanation: Article 311 provides two constitutional safeguards to civil servants against arbitrary dismissal: (1) Cannot be dismissed by authority subordinate to appointing authority; (2) Must be given reasonable opportunity to show cause before dismissal, removal, or reduction in rank. Exceptions: national security, impracticability, conviction in criminal court.
keywords: Article 311,Civil Servants,Dismissal,Removal,Reasonable Opportunity,Constitutional Safeguard,National Security Exception
landmark_case_1: State of Orissa v. Ram Narayan Das
lc1_holding_summary: Article 311 safeguards apply to all civil servants; inquiry must be fair and opportunity genuine
landmark_case_2: Union of India v. Tulsiram Patel
lc2_holding_summary: Exceptions to Article 311(2) must be narrowly construed; national security exception cannot be used routinely
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Reservation of seats for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in the House of the People
verbatim_text_excerpt: Seats shall be reserved in the House of the People for the Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes. The number of seats reserved for the Scheduled Castes or the Scheduled Tribes in the House of the People shall bear, as nearly as may be, the same proportion to the total number of seats in the House as the population of the Scheduled Castes in the Union or of the Scheduled Tribes in the Union bears to the total population of the Union
simplified_explanation: SC/ST reservation in Lok Sabha is proportional to their population. Reserved seats are spread across states proportionally. Rotation of reserved constituencies happens after every delimitation to ensure geographic distribution. The 104th Amendment extended this reservation for 10 more years (2020-2030). Anglo-Indian nomination (2 seats) was abolished.
keywords: Article 330,SC ST Reservation,Lok Sabha,Proportional Representation,Delimitation,104th Amendment
landmark_case_1: Ashok Kumar Gupta v. State of UP
lc1_holding_summary: Reservation in legislative bodies is a constitutional mandate reflecting historical injustice; must be implemented faithfully
landmark_case_2: Meghraj Kothari v. Delimitation Commission
lc2_holding_summary: Delimitation Commission's decisions on reserved constituencies are final and not subject to court review
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Reservation of seats for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in Legislative Assemblies
verbatim_text_excerpt: Seats shall be reserved in the Legislative Assembly of every State for the Scheduled Castes and for the Scheduled Tribes, except the Scheduled Tribes in the tribal areas of Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram
simplified_explanation: Same principle as Article 330 for State Assemblies. States with significant ST populations (Madhya Pradesh, Odisha, Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Gujarat) have substantial reserved constituencies. Northeastern states with tribal-majority areas have special provisions. Reserved constituencies must be spread across the state.
keywords: Article 332,State Assembly,SC ST Reservation,Legislative Representation,Delimitation
landmark_case_1: Krishnamurthy v. Bharath Janata Party
lc1_holding_summary: Reservation of Assembly seats must be in proportion to SC/ST population; delimitation must be done correctly
landmark_case_2: State of MP v. Baldeo Prasad
lc2_holding_summary: Reservation of assembly seats is a constitutional requirement; failure to reserve is unconstitutional
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Claims of Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes to services and posts
verbatim_text_excerpt: The claims of the members of the Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes shall be taken into consideration, consistently with the maintenance of efficiency of administration, in the making of appointments to services and posts in connection with the affairs of the Union or of a State
simplified_explanation: Article 335 requires that SC/ST claims in public employment must be considered while also maintaining administrative efficiency. The efficiency caveat was used to resist reservation in promotions. After the M. Nagaraj case, reservation in promotion requires quantifiable data showing that reservations will not adversely affect efficiency.
keywords: Article 335,SC ST Employment,Administrative Efficiency,Reservation,Services and Posts
landmark_case_1: M. Nagaraj v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Article 335's efficiency requirement must be demonstrated by quantifiable data before granting reservation in promotions
landmark_case_2: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Efficiency and social justice can coexist; reservations are consistent with meritocracy in the long run
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Appointment of Commission — OBC
verbatim_text_excerpt: The President may by order appoint a Commission consisting of such persons as he thinks fit to investigate the conditions of socially and educationally backward classes within the territory of India and the difficulties under which they labour and to make recommendations as to the steps that should be taken by the Union or any State to remove such difficulties and to improve their condition
simplified_explanation: Two landmark commissions: Kaka Kalelkar Commission (1955) and Mandal Commission (1979-80). Mandal recommended 27% OBC reservation implemented in 1990 by V.P. Singh government. Supreme Court upheld this in Indra Sawhney (1992) with 50% total cap. Concept of creamy layer for OBCs established. State OBC commissions formed under similar provisions.
keywords: Article 340,OBC Commission,Kaka Kalelkar,Mandal Commission,Backward Classes,27% OBC Reservation
landmark_case_1: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Mandal Commission's 27% OBC reservation upheld; creamy layer must be excluded; 50% total cap applies
landmark_case_2: M.R. Balaji v. State of Mysore
lc2_holding_summary: More than 50% reservation is arbitrary; caste alone cannot be the criterion for backward class determination
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Language Provisions — Overview
verbatim_text_excerpt: Hindi in Devanagari script shall be the official language of the Union. The form of numerals to be used for the official purposes of the Union shall be the international form of Indian numerals. The Parliament may by law provide for the use of the Hindi language and the Devanagari script for such of the official purposes of the Union as may be specified in the law
simplified_explanation: The language provisions (Articles 343-351) navigate a complex compromise: Hindi is official for Union purposes; English continues as associate official language under Official Languages Act 1963; Eighth Schedule lists 22 scheduled languages; States can adopt their own official languages; Three-Language Formula recommends teaching regional language, Hindi, and English in schools.
keywords: Official Language,Hindi,8th Schedule,22 Languages,Three Language Formula,Article 343,Regional Languages
landmark_case_1: Tamilnadu v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Hindi cannot be imposed on non-Hindi states; three-language formula must respect regional autonomy
landmark_case_2: State of Madras v. Champakam Dorairajan
lc2_holding_summary: Language rights are fundamental; medium of instruction cannot be imposed against minority rights
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Suspension of provisions of Article 19 during emergencies
verbatim_text_excerpt: While a Proclamation of Emergency is in operation, nothing in Article 19 shall restrict the power of the State to make any law or to take any executive action which the State would but for the provisions contained in that Article be competent to make or to take
simplified_explanation: During National Emergency (Article 352), Article 19 freedoms are automatically suspended — no court proceedings for enforcement. After 44th Amendment, this suspension is limited to war/external aggression emergencies, not armed rebellion emergencies. Article 19 is NOT suspended during President's Rule (Article 356) or Financial Emergency (Article 360).
keywords: Article 358,Emergency,Article 19 Suspension,National Emergency,44th Amendment,Armed Rebellion
landmark_case_1: A.K. Roy v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Article 358 suspension of Article 19 is automatic during external emergency; armed rebellion emergency does not attract Article 358
landmark_case_2: Minerva Mills v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Emergency provisions must not be used to destroy basic structure; rights cannot be permanently suspended
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Suspension of the enforcement of the rights conferred by Part III during emergencies
verbatim_text_excerpt: Where a Proclamation of Emergency is in operation, the President may by order declare that the right to move any court for the enforcement of such of the rights conferred by Part III as may be mentioned in the order shall remain suspended for the period during which the Proclamation is in force
simplified_explanation: Unlike Article 358 (automatic suspension of Article 19), Article 359 requires a Presidential Order to suspend enforcement of specified Fundamental Rights during emergency. After 44th Amendment, Articles 20 and 21 can NEVER be suspended even during emergency. This directly overrules the infamous ADM Jabalpur judgment.
keywords: Article 359,Emergency,Presidential Order,Enforcement Suspension,Articles 20 21 Non-Derogable
landmark_case_1: K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: ADM Jabalpur overruled; Articles 20 and 21 cannot be suspended even during emergency under Article 359
landmark_case_2: ADM Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla
lc2_holding_summary: (Overruled) Article 359 allows suspension of Article 21 during emergency — now constitutionally impossible after 44th Amendment
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Protection of President and Governors and Rajpramukhs
verbatim_text_excerpt: The President, or the Governor or Rajpramukh of a State, shall not be answerable to any court for the exercise and performance of the powers and duties of his office or for any act done or purporting to be done by him in the exercise and performance of those powers and duties
simplified_explanation: The President and Governors enjoy constitutional immunity from court proceedings for official acts during their tenure. However, this immunity is not absolute — after leaving office, they can face proceedings for acts done before or after the protected period. The immunity does not extend to executive actions done in personal capacity.
keywords: Article 361,President Immunity,Governor Immunity,Official Acts,Constitutional Protection
landmark_case_1: Rameshwar Prasad v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Article 361 immunity covers official acts; Governor's recommendation for President's Rule is an official act but can be reviewed by court post-facto
landmark_case_2: B.P. Singhal v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Article 361 immunity does not make the Governor unaccountable; removal without cause can be challenged
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Effect of failure to comply with, or to give effect to, directions given by the Union
verbatim_text_excerpt: Where any State has failed to comply with, or to give effect to, any directions given in the exercise of the executive power of the Union under any of the provisions of this Constitution, it shall be lawful for the President to hold that a situation has arisen in which the Government of that State cannot be carried on in accordance with the provisions of this Constitution
simplified_explanation: Article 365 provides a ground for invoking President's Rule (Article 356) when a state fails to comply with Central directions. However, after S.R. Bommai (1994), this too is subject to judicial review. Non-compliance must be serious and go to the root of constitutional governance, not mere policy disagreement.
keywords: Article 365,President's Rule,Central Directions,State Non-Compliance,Article 356
landmark_case_1: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Article 365 cannot be invoked for trivial non-compliance; must be serious failure threatening constitutional governance
landmark_case_2: State of Rajasthan v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Centre's power to give directions and invoke Article 365 is constitutional but subject to judicial review
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Kerala Land Reforms Act 1963
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to give effect to the policy of the State towards securing the principle specified in clause (b) of Article 39 of the Constitution and for that purpose to place ceiling on land-holdings, provide for the equitable distribution of land amongst those who work on the land, to confer ownership rights on cultivating tenants and to make provision for certain other matters
simplified_explanation: Kerala's landmark land reform legislation that redistributed land from large landowners (jenmis) to agricultural workers. Protected under the Ninth Schedule. Ceiling on land holdings imposed. Cultivating tenants given ownership rights. Led to dramatic reduction in rural inequality. Often cited as a model for successful land reform without violent conflict.
keywords: Kerala Land Reforms,Ceiling on Land,Cultivating Tenants,Ninth Schedule,Land Redistribution
landmark_case_1: State of Kerala v. Thomas
lc1_holding_summary: Land reform laws protected by Ninth Schedule; cannot be challenged under Fundamental Rights
landmark_case_2: Nair Service Society v. State of Kerala
lc2_holding_summary: Land reform must be genuine — cannot be used selectively against specific communities
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Maharashtra Rent Control Act 1999
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to consolidate and amend the law relating to the control of rents and repairs of certain premises, of evictions and for encouraging the construction of new houses by assuring a fair return on the investment and for matters connected therewith
simplified_explanation: Regulates rent and eviction in Maharashtra. Tenants in rent-controlled premises enjoy strong protection against eviction. Landlords can seek eviction only on specified grounds (personal use, non-payment of rent, sub-letting). This has led to frozen rentals in old buildings and disincentivized maintenance — a social problem widely studied.
keywords: Maharashtra Rent Control,Rent Regulation,Tenant Protection,Eviction,Fair Return
landmark_case_1: Saul Bhatt v. State of Maharashtra
lc1_holding_summary: Rent control laws that deny reasonable return to landlords may violate Article 19(1)(f) property rights
landmark_case_2: Atma Ram Properties v. Federal Motors
lc2_holding_summary: Rent Control Acts must balance tenant protection with landlord's right to fair return
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: Goa Land Acquisition Regulation Act and Common Civil Code
title: Goa Civil Code — Portuguese Civil Code in Goa
verbatim_text_excerpt: Goa is the only Indian state with a Uniform Civil Code. The Portuguese Civil Code 1867 continues to apply in Goa after its liberation in 1961. It governs marriage, divorce, inheritance, and property rights uniformly for all citizens of Goa regardless of religion
simplified_explanation: Goa's Civil Code is often cited in UCC debates as proof of concept. Key features: mandatory civil registration of all marriages (Hindu, Muslim, Christian); equal inheritance rights for all children; spouse entitled to half the matrimonial property; divorce requires court proceedings. The SC in several cases has referred to Goa as an example of UCC in practice.
keywords: Goa Civil Code,Uniform Civil Code,Portuguese Civil Code,Marriage Registration,Inheritance,UCC
landmark_case_1: Sarla Mudgal v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Goa's UCC cited as model; SC urged Parliament to enact UCC under Article 44
landmark_case_2: John Vallamattom v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Discriminatory provisions in personal law must yield to constitutional equality; Goa UCC is constitutionally valid
category: State Laws - Uniform Civil Code Goa
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: Tamil Nadu Prohibition of Online Gambling Act 2022
title: Tamil Nadu Prohibition of Online Gambling and Regulation of Online Games Act 2022
verbatim_text_excerpt: An Act to prohibit online gambling and online games of chance and to provide for regulation of online games of skill played for stakes within the State of Tamil Nadu, to curb the menace of online gambling, addiction and related ills
simplified_explanation: A contentious state law attempting to regulate online gaming — a subject of both State and Union List. Struck down by Madras HC as unconstitutional (violating Article 19(1)(g) — right to carry on business; games of skill like rummy, poker are not gambling). The Centre subsequently enacted the Online Gaming Rules 2023 under IT Act.
keywords: Online Gaming,Online Gambling,Games of Skill,Tamil Nadu,IT Act,Article 19(1)(g)
landmark_case_1: Junglee Games India v. State of Tamil Nadu
lc1_holding_summary: Games of skill — rummy, fantasy sports — are protected under Article 19(1)(g); blanket prohibition is unconstitutional
landmark_case_2: K.R. Lakshmanan v. State of Tamil Nadu
lc2_holding_summary: Horse racing is a game of skill; activity involving skill cannot be prohibited as gambling
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Rajasthan Panchayati Raj Act 1994 and Two-Child Norm
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Rajasthan Panchayati Raj Act 1994 provides for disqualification from contesting panchayat elections for persons having more than two children after a specified date
simplified_explanation: Several states (Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh, Haryana) have enacted laws disqualifying persons with more than two children from contesting panchayat elections. The Supreme Court upheld this in Javed v. State of Haryana (2003) holding it promotes population control and is not violative of fundamental rights as it is a voluntary choice.
keywords: Two-Child Norm,Panchayat Disqualification,Population Control,Article 14,Family Planning
landmark_case_1: Javed v. State of Haryana
lc1_holding_summary: Two-child norm disqualification for panchayat elections is valid; does not violate Articles 14, 21; it is an incentive for family planning
landmark_case_2: Shailendra Kumar Tripathi v. State of UP
lc2_holding_summary: Two-child norm must be applied uniformly; exceptions for adoption must be carefully considered
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice

title: Universal Declaration of Human Rights 1948
verbatim_text_excerpt: All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. Everyone has the right to life, liberty and security of person. Everyone has the right to freedom of thought, conscience and religion — Articles 1, 3, 18 UDHR
simplified_explanation: UDHR was adopted by UN General Assembly on 10 December 1948 (Human Rights Day). India voted in favour. While not legally binding, it has been used by Indian courts to interpret Fundamental Rights under Part III. Key UDHR rights that parallel Indian constitutional rights: right to life (Article 3), equal protection (Article 7), fair trial (Article 10), freedom of movement (Article 13), right to education (Article 26).
keywords: UDHR,Universal Declaration of Human Rights,Human Rights Day,Article 21,Fundamental Rights,International Norms
landmark_case_1: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
lc1_holding_summary: International conventions like CEDAW and UDHR used to interpret Indian Fundamental Rights; Article 51(c) provides constitutional basis
landmark_case_2: Francis Coralie Mullin v. Administrator
lc2_holding_summary: Right to live with dignity in Article 21 interpreted in light of UDHR and ICCPR norms
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights 1966
verbatim_text_excerpt: Each State Party to the present Covenant undertakes to respect and to ensure to all individuals within its territory and subject to its jurisdiction the rights recognized in the present Covenant, without distinction of any kind — Article 2 ICCPR
simplified_explanation: India ratified ICCPR in 1979. It provides binding international norms on: right to life, prohibition of torture, fair trial rights, freedom of speech, freedom of religion, right to privacy. Indian courts use ICCPR to interpret Articles 14, 19, 21. The Human Rights Committee monitors compliance. India has not ratified the Optional Protocol allowing individual petitions.
keywords: ICCPR,Civil and Political Rights,India Ratification 1979,Human Rights Committee,Right to Life,Fair Trial
landmark_case_1: People's Union for Civil Liberties v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: ICCPR norms on privacy and surveillance used to interpret Article 21; phone tapping must be regulated
landmark_case_2: Bachan Singh v. State of Punjab
lc2_holding_summary: Death penalty — ICCPR's position on right to life considered while upholding constitutional validity of death penalty
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights 1966
verbatim_text_excerpt: The States Parties to the present Covenant recognize the right of everyone to work, which includes the right of everyone to the opportunity to gain his living by work which he freely chooses or accepts — Article 6 ICESCR
simplified_explanation: India ratified ICESCR in 1979. It covers socio-economic rights: right to work, right to social security, right to an adequate standard of living, right to education, right to health. These correspond to DPSPs in Part IV. Indian courts have used ICESCR to give content to Article 21 (right to health, food, livelihood).
keywords: ICESCR,Economic Social Cultural Rights,India Ratification 1979,Right to Work,Right to Health,Right to Education
landmark_case_1: Paschim Banga Khet Mazdoor Samity v. State of West Bengal
lc1_holding_summary: Right to emergency health care as part of Article 21 — ICESCR's right to health used to support this
landmark_case_2: Consumer Education and Research Centre v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Right to health is a Fundamental Right under Article 21; ICESCR standards used to define scope
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women 1979
verbatim_text_excerpt: States Parties condemn discrimination against women in all its forms, agree to pursue by all appropriate means and without delay a policy of eliminating discrimination against women — Article 2 CEDAW
simplified_explanation: India ratified CEDAW in 1993 with reservations on Articles 5(a) and 16(1). CEDAW requires equal treatment for women in education, employment, health, political participation, and family law. Used by Indian courts in sexual harassment cases, maternal health cases, and personal law reforms. CEDAW Committee reviews India's periodic reports.
keywords: CEDAW,Women Discrimination,India Ratification 1993,Gender Equality,Sexual Harassment,Personal Law
landmark_case_1: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
lc1_holding_summary: CEDAW used to interpret gender equality under Articles 14 and 15; sexual harassment at workplace violates CEDAW
landmark_case_2: Laxmi v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Acid attack regulation — CEDAW's mandate to eliminate gender-based violence cited by SC
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child 1989
verbatim_text_excerpt: Every child has the inherent right to life. States Parties shall ensure to the maximum extent possible the survival and development of the child — Article 6 UNCRC
simplified_explanation: India ratified UNCRC in 1992. Provides comprehensive rights framework for children: survival rights, development rights, protection rights, participation rights. Key implementation: RTE Act 2009, POCSO Act 2012, JJ Act 2015, Child Labour (Prohibition) Amendment Act 2016. Best interests of the child is the primary consideration.
keywords: UNCRC,Children Rights,India Ratification 1992,Best Interests of Child,Child Protection,Survival Rights
landmark_case_1: Bachpan Bachao Andolan v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: UNCRC's best interests of the child principle guides interpretation of all child-related legislation
landmark_case_2: Gaurav Jain v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Children of sex workers have right to education and dignified life; UNCRC standards applied
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Paris Agreement on Climate Change 2015
verbatim_text_excerpt: Each Party shall prepare, communicate and maintain successive nationally determined contributions that it intends to achieve. Parties shall pursue domestic mitigation measures, with the aim of achieving the objectives of such contributions — Article 4 Paris Agreement
simplified_explanation: India ratified the Paris Agreement in 2016. India's NDC (Nationally Determined Contribution) commits to: 50% electricity from non-fossil fuels by 2030, reducing emission intensity of GDP by 45% by 2030, creating carbon sink of 2.5-3 billion tonnes through forests. India achieved its 40% non-fossil fuel target 9 years early.
keywords: Paris Agreement,Climate Change,NDC,India Ratification 2016,Net Zero,Emission Intensity,Renewable Energy
landmark_case_1: M.K. Ranjitsinh v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Right to be free from climate change adverse effects is a Fundamental Right; India's Paris Agreement commitments inform this right
landmark_case_2: Ridhima Pandey v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Child activist's petition on climate change — SC observed India has statutory and international obligations to address climate change
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

title: Agreement on Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights (TRIPS) 1994
verbatim_text_excerpt: Members shall give effect to the provisions of this Agreement. Members may, in formulating or implementing their laws and regulations, adopt measures necessary to protect public health and nutrition, and to promote the public interest in sectors of vital importance — Articles 1 and 8 TRIPS
simplified_explanation: India implemented TRIPS through Patents (Amendment) Act 2005 introducing product patents in pharmaceuticals. Controversially, Section 3(d) of the Patents Act bars ever-greening of patents (minor modifications to get new patent). Novartis case (2013) upheld Section 3(d) as TRIPS-compliant. India is the 'pharmacy of the world' supplying 40% of generic medicines to the US and 25% to the UK.
keywords: TRIPS,Patent,Section 3(d),Ever-greening,Generic Medicine,Novartis,Pharmaceutical
landmark_case_1: Novartis AG v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Section 3(d) Patents Act is TRIPS-compliant; Gleevec (Imatinib) patent refused as it does not demonstrate enhanced efficacy
landmark_case_2: Bayer Corporation v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Compulsory licence for Nexavar cancer drug granted; TRIPS Article 31 compulsory licensing provisions applied
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities 2006
verbatim_text_excerpt: Persons with disabilities include those who have long-term physical, mental, intellectual or sensory impairments which in interaction with various barriers may hinder their full and effective participation in society on an equal basis with others — Article 1 UNCRPD
simplified_explanation: India ratified UNCRPD in 2007. Implemented through Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act 2016 (RPwD Act) which expanded disability categories to 21 types, mandated 5% reservation in government jobs, introduced 'reasonable accommodation' as a legal right, and created Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities.
keywords: UNCRPD,Disability Rights,India Ratification 2007,RPwD Act,Reasonable Accommodation,21 Disability Types
landmark_case_1: Vikash Kumar v. UPSC
lc1_holding_summary: UPSC must provide reasonable accommodation (scribe, compensatory time) for candidates with disabilities; denial violates RPwD Act and Article 21
landmark_case_2: Jeeja Ghosh v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Disability is a diversity characteristic; persons with disabilities cannot be treated as incapable of independent decisions
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Doctrine of Harmonious Construction
verbatim_text_excerpt: When there is an apparent conflict between two or more provisions of the Constitution or a statute, the court must try to construe them harmoniously so as to give effect to all provisions. No provision should be rendered meaningless or otiose if it can be given a harmonious interpretation
simplified_explanation: The doctrine prevents courts from striking down one provision when it conflicts with another. Instead, they try to reconcile both. In constitutional law — used when Fundamental Rights conflict with DPSPs (both given effect), when different entries in the Seventh Schedule appear to overlap, and when constitutional provisions seem contradictory.
keywords: Harmonious Construction,Constitutional Interpretation,Conflict of Provisions,Seventh Schedule,Fundamental Rights,DPSPs
landmark_case_1: CIT v. Hindustan Bulk Carriers
lc1_holding_summary: All provisions of a statute must be read as a whole; harmonious construction avoids conflict between provisions
landmark_case_2: Sri Ram Ram Narain Medhi v. State of Bombay
lc2_holding_summary: Seventh Schedule entries must be read harmoniously; overlap is permitted if both legislative competencies are respected
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Rule of Liberal Construction of Fundamental Rights
verbatim_text_excerpt: Fundamental Rights must be construed broadly and liberally. Where a law restricts a Fundamental Right, the restriction must be construed strictly. The right-conferring provision must be given the widest possible interpretation; the restriction-enabling provision must be given the narrowest possible interpretation
simplified_explanation: The liberal construction rule reflects the constitutional design: rights are primary, restrictions are secondary. Courts must not cut down rights through restrictive interpretation. Article 13(1) and (2) reflect this by making all inconsistent laws void. The 'golden triangle' of Articles 14, 19, 21 must be broadly read as an integrated right.
keywords: Liberal Construction,Fundamental Rights,Restrictive Interpretation,Golden Triangle,Articles 14 19 21
landmark_case_1: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Articles 14, 19, 21 must be read together liberally; any law restricting liberty must pass all three tests
landmark_case_2: State of Madras v. V.G. Row
lc2_holding_summary: Constitutional freedoms must be construed liberally; restrictions on them must be interpreted strictly
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: Doctrine of Double Effect and Reading Down
title: Doctrine of Reading Down — Saving a Law by Limiting its Application
verbatim_text_excerpt: Where a law is capable of two interpretations — one constitutional and one unconstitutional — the court must adopt the constitutional interpretation. Where a law has an unconstitutional sweep, the court may 'read it down' to limit its application to constitutional situations only
simplified_explanation: Reading down saves a law from being struck down entirely. The court reads the law as if it has only the constitutional scope. Difference from severability: reading down limits the operation of the provision without striking any words; severability removes the unconstitutional portion. Used frequently in free speech cases.
keywords: Reading Down,Constitutional Interpretation,Saving Legislation,Unconstitutional Sweep,Severability Compared
landmark_case_1: Kedar Nath Singh v. State of Bihar
lc1_holding_summary: Sedition law read down to apply only to incitement to violence; otherwise the law would be unconstitutional
landmark_case_2: Shreya Singhal v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Section 66A could not be read down as its vagueness was intrinsic; had to be struck down entirely unlike sedition
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Doctrine of Non-Delegation of Essential Legislative Functions
verbatim_text_excerpt: The legislature cannot delegate its essential legislative functions to the executive. The legislature must itself determine the policy, lay down the standard, and delegate only the task of carrying out the details. Excessive delegation of legislative power is unconstitutional
simplified_explanation: Parliament can delegate subordinate legislation (rules, regulations, notifications) to the executive but cannot abdicate its essential legislative function. Courts use the 'policy and guidelines' test — if Parliament has laid down a policy and guidelines, delegation of details to executive is valid. Delegated legislation can be challenged as ultra vires if it exceeds the parent law.
keywords: Non-Delegation,Essential Legislative Function,Delegated Legislation,Ultra Vires,Subordinate Legislation
landmark_case_1: Harishankar Bagla v. State of MP
lc1_holding_summary: Parliament can delegate subordinate legislation with policy and guidelines; if guideline exists, delegation is valid
landmark_case_2: Delhi Laws Act Reference
lc2_holding_summary: Essential legislative functions cannot be delegated; policy must be determined by legislature
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Doctrine of Implied Powers in Indian Constitution
verbatim_text_excerpt: Where a power is conferred, all incidental or ancillary powers necessary for the exercise of that power are also impliedly conferred. An authority empowered to do something is also impliedly empowered to do whatever is reasonably necessary to do that thing
simplified_explanation: The doctrine prevents strict literal construction from defeating the constitutional purpose. If the Constitution or a law confers a power, incidental powers necessary for its exercise are implied. Applied to legislative powers (incidental legislative powers), executive powers (implied executive powers), and judicial powers.
keywords: Implied Powers,Incidental Powers,Ancillary Powers,Constitutional Interpretation,Legislative Competence
landmark_case_1: Jagannath v. Authorised Controller
lc1_holding_summary: Legislature can by implication grant all powers incidentally necessary to give effect to the main statutory purpose
landmark_case_2: Attorney General for India v. Amratlal Prajivandas
lc2_holding_summary: Implied powers doctrine applies to constitutional bodies; SC can take all steps necessary to enforce its orders
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Doctrine of Prospective Overruling
verbatim_text_excerpt: When the Supreme Court overrules a previous decision, it may declare that the new rule shall operate only prospectively — from the date of the new decision — and shall not affect past transactions or decisions that relied on the old rule. This prevents undue disruption of settled affairs
simplified_explanation: The doctrine was introduced in India in Golak Nath v. State of Punjab (1967). SC held its ruling (Parliament cannot amend Fundamental Rights) would operate prospectively — past amendments would not be invalidated. Also applied in I.C. Golak Nath to prevent chaos from invalidating past constitutional amendments.
keywords: Prospective Overruling,Overruling,Retroactivity,Past Transactions,Legal Certainty,Golak Nath
landmark_case_1: I.C. Golak Nath v. State of Punjab
lc1_holding_summary: Doctrine of prospective overruling first applied in India; new ruling to operate only from date of judgment
landmark_case_2: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Creamy layer requirement applied prospectively; past OBC beneficiaries not affected by new rule
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: Rule of Law — A.V. Dicey's Principles in India
title: Rule of Law in Indian Constitutional Context
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Rule of Law has three meanings: (1) Supremacy of law — no man is punishable except for a breach of law established in the ordinary legal manner; (2) Equality before law — no man is above the law; (3) Predominance of legal spirit — the rights of individuals are products of judicial decisions — A.V. Dicey
simplified_explanation: India's Constitution embeds all three aspects of Dicey's Rule of Law: Article 13 (supremacy of Constitution), Article 14 (equality before law), and Articles 32 and 226 (judicial protection of rights). The Supreme Court has elevated the Rule of Law to a basic structure of the Constitution — Parliament cannot abrogate it even through constitutional amendment.
keywords: Rule of Law,Dicey,Supremacy of Law,Equality,Article 14,Basic Structure
landmark_case_1: ADM Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla
lc1_holding_summary: H.R. Khanna J dissented: Rule of Law is not abrogated even during Emergency; it is the constitutional foundation
landmark_case_2: Indira Nehru Gandhi v. Raj Narain
lc2_holding_summary: Rule of law is a basic feature of the Constitution; retroactive validation of election violates it
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: Concept of Social Justice in Indian Constitution
title: Social Justice as a Constitutional Mandate
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Preamble's commitment to social justice runs through the entire constitutional framework — Fundamental Rights, Directive Principles, Fundamental Duties, Reservation provisions, and SC/ST protective legislation. Social justice requires not just formal equality but substantive equality — treating unequals unequally to bring about real equality
simplified_explanation: Social justice in the Indian Constitution goes beyond formal equality (treating everyone the same) to substantive equality (giving more to those with less to bring about real equality). This justifies affirmative action, protective discrimination, progressive taxation, land reform, and welfare rights. The SC has consistently held that social justice is a constitutional goal that must guide all legislation.
keywords: Social Justice,Substantive Equality,Affirmative Action,Protective Discrimination,Preamble,Constitutional Goal
landmark_case_1: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Social justice requires upliftment of historically disadvantaged groups; reservations are tools of social justice
landmark_case_2: Air India v. Nargesh Meerza
lc2_holding_summary: Social justice demands that women not be discriminated against in employment; gender justice is part of social justice
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Finance

title: Doctrine of State Action and Horizontal Application of Fundamental Rights
verbatim_text_excerpt: Fundamental Rights are primarily available against the State (defined under Article 12). However, courts have extended their protection against private parties in certain circumstances — especially when the private party is discharging a public function, has a monopoly, or is substantially funded/controlled by the State
simplified_explanation: Articles 17 (Untouchability) and 23 (Forced Labour) explicitly apply to private parties. Courts have also applied Article 21 against private hospitals, Article 14 against private employers in dominant positions, and Articles 15/16 against private educational institutions backed by state funding. The trend is toward broader horizontal application.
keywords: State Action,Horizontal Application,Private Parties,Article 12,Fundamental Rights,Public Function
landmark_case_1: People's Union for Democratic Rights v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Articles 17 and 23 apply directly against private parties; the State has a duty to enforce these rights even against private actors
landmark_case_2: MC Mehta v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Absolute liability doctrine applies to private industries; right to life in Article 21 creates obligations on private actors
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: Doctrine of Adequate Representation and Creamy Layer
title: Creamy Layer Doctrine in Reservation Jurisprudence
verbatim_text_excerpt: The creamy layer refers to the more advanced members of a backward class who are socially, economically and educationally at par with the forward classes. Such persons should not be given the benefit of reservation as they have ceased to be backward — Indra Sawhney 1992
simplified_explanation: The creamy layer concept ensures that reservation benefits reach genuinely backward persons, not the already-advanced among OBCs. Income ceiling (currently Rs 8 lakh/year per Jarnail Singh 2018, revised to higher by government), constitutional posts held by parents, and educational attainment are markers of creamy layer. Applies to OBCs; debated for SC/ST promotions.
keywords: Creamy Layer,OBC Reservation,Backward Class,Income Ceiling,Genuinely Backward,Article 16(4)
landmark_case_1: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Creamy layer must be excluded from OBC reservation; more advanced among backward class are not backward for reservation purposes
landmark_case_2: Jarnail Singh v. Lachhmi Narain Gupta
lc2_holding_summary: Creamy layer concept applicable to SC/ST reservation in promotions; income-based exclusion must be applied
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: Separation of Powers — Judiciary vs Legislature
title: Contempt of Court and Parliamentary Privilege — Conflict
verbatim_text_excerpt: A conflict arises when Parliament seeks to punish a judge for contempt of its privilege, or a court seeks to punish a Member of Parliament for contempt of court for statements made in Parliament. Article 105 gives immunity to MPs for statements in Parliament; Article 129 gives SC power to punish for contempt
simplified_explanation: The MP's speech in Parliament attacking a court judgment is protected by Article 105 (Parliamentary privilege) and cannot be prosecuted in court. But if an MP, outside Parliament, scans a court, the court's contempt jurisdiction applies. The immunity under Article 105 is absolute for in-Parliament statements.
keywords: Contempt of Court,Parliamentary Privilege,Article 105,Article 129,Separation of Powers,Conflict
landmark_case_1: Searchlight Case (In Re Harijai Singh)
lc1_holding_summary: MP's statements in Parliament about court proceedings are protected by Article 105 privilege; court cannot punish
landmark_case_2: In Re Arundhati Roy
lc2_holding_summary: Contempt of court power must be used sparingly; right to criticise courts peacefully is protected by Article 19
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Constitutional Implications of Coalition Governments and Floor Tests
verbatim_text_excerpt: In a coalition government, the question of confidence is tested on the floor of the House. The Governor cannot dismiss a coalition government on personal satisfaction without calling for a floor test. The President/Governor's discretion is extremely limited when a party/coalition commands majority
simplified_explanation: Post-SR Bommai, the Governor cannot dismiss a government on the basis of a subjective assessment of loss of majority. A floor test is the only constitutional method. Coalition arrangements, including pre-poll and post-poll alliances, have given rise to complex constitutional questions on anti-defection law, Speaker's role, and Governor's discretion.
keywords: Coalition Government,Floor Test,Anti-Defection,Governor Discretion,Majority Testing,Constitutional Convention
landmark_case_1: Nabam Rebia v. Deputy Speaker
lc1_holding_summary: Governor cannot call session of House to test majority without CM's advice; floor test is the correct mechanism
landmark_case_2: Subhash Desai v. Principal Secretary Governor of Maharashtra
lc2_holding_summary: Governor cannot call floor test based on rebel MLAs' petition alone; constitutional convention requires CM to advise
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: National Judicial Appointments Commission vs Collegium — Constitutional Debate
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution (Ninety-Ninth Amendment) Act 2015 and the National Judicial Appointments Commission Act 2014 sought to replace the collegium system with a 6-member NJAC (CJI + 2 senior SC judges + Union Law Minister + 2 eminent persons). The SC struck down the 99th Amendment and NJAC Act as unconstitutional in October 2015
simplified_explanation: NJAC was struck down 4:1 (Justice Chelameshwar dissenting) in SCAORA v. Union of India (NJAC Case 2015). Reasons: inclusion of Law Minister gives executive a veto over judicial appointments, violating independence of judiciary as basic structure; eminent person provision gives undue power to non-judicial actors. Post-NJAC, memorandum of procedure (MoP) reform remains pending.
keywords: NJAC,99th Amendment,Collegium System,Judicial Independence,Basic Structure,Appointment of Judges
landmark_case_1: SCAORA v. Union of India (NJAC Case)
lc1_holding_summary: NJAC struck down; collegium system reaffirmed as constitutionally mandated; judicial independence is basic structure
landmark_case_2: SCAORA v. Union of India (Second Judges Case)
lc2_holding_summary: Collegium established; CJI's primacy in judicial appointments is constitutional requirement
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: National Security Laws and Civil Liberties — Balancing
title: National Security Laws vs Fundamental Rights — Constitutional Balance
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Supreme Court has consistently held that even in national security matters, Fundamental Rights cannot be completely suspended. Procedural safeguards must be maintained, habeas corpus lies (post-44th Amendment), and the proportionality test applies to security-related restrictions
simplified_explanation: India's security laws (UAPA, NSA, AFSPA, PSA in J&K) must coexist with constitutional guarantees. Post-44th Amendment, Article 21 cannot be suspended. SC has directed that preventive detention must be reviewed; AFSPA areas must have human rights safeguards; UAPA bail conditions are very strict but prolonged imprisonment without trial violates Article 21.
keywords: AFSPA,National Security Act,Preventive Detention,Article 21,Security Laws,Civil Liberties
landmark_case_1: Extra Judicial Execution Victim Families Association v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: AFSPA does not confer absolute immunity; security forces can be prosecuted for fake encounters even in disturbed areas
landmark_case_2: Union of India v. K.A. Najeeb
lc2_holding_summary: Prolonged incarceration without trial violates Article 21 even in UAPA cases; constitutional courts can grant bail
category: Special Topics - National Security vs Civil Liberties
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Electoral Reforms — Constitutional and Statutory Framework
verbatim_text_excerpt: Free and fair elections are part of the basic structure of the Constitution. The election system is governed by: the Constitution (Articles 324-329), Representation of People Act 1950 (electoral rolls) and 1951 (conduct of elections), Model Code of Conduct, and Supreme Court directions under Article 142
simplified_explanation: Key electoral reforms: NOTA option (2013); compulsory disclosure of criminal antecedents and assets; EVM and VVPAT; State Funding of Elections debate; criminalization of politics (Public Interest Foundation v. Union of India 2018 — SC directed Parliament to act); One Nation One Election debate; electoral bonds struck down (2024); ECI appointment reform.
keywords: Electoral Reforms,NOTA,EVM,VVPAT,Criminalization of Politics,ECI Independence,Electoral Bonds
landmark_case_1: Public Interest Foundation v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: SC directed Parliament to enact law barring persons with serious criminal cases from elections; Parliament must act
landmark_case_2: Association for Democratic Reforms v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Electoral Bonds Scheme struck down; anonymous political funding violates voters' right to information
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice

title: Cooperative Federalism vs Competitive Federalism in India
verbatim_text_excerpt: Cooperative federalism involves Centre and States working together on shared goals (GST Council, NITI Aayog, Inter-State Council). Competitive federalism involves States competing to attract investment and improve governance metrics (Ease of Doing Business rankings, Aspirational Districts). Both forms coexist in India's constitutional framework
simplified_explanation: Post-1991 liberalisation, both cooperative and competitive federalism became prominent. GST Council (Article 279A) is the best example of cooperative federalism — unanimous decisions preferred, States have 2/3 weightage. NITI Aayog (replaced Planning Commission 2015) uses competitive federalism benchmarks. COVID-19 highlighted both — Centre-State coordination needs and inter-State competition for vaccines.
keywords: Cooperative Federalism,Competitive Federalism,GST Council,NITI Aayog,Planning Commission,Centre-State
landmark_case_1: Union of India v. Mohit Mineral
lc1_holding_summary: GST Council represents cooperative federalism; recommendations persuasive not binding — balancing cooperation and autonomy
landmark_case_2: State of Kerala v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: State can challenge Centre's fiscal decisions violating federal principles; cooperative federalism has constitutional backing
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Gender Justice under the Indian Constitution
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Constitution guarantees gender equality through Articles 14 (equality before law), 15 (non-discrimination), 16 (equal opportunity), 19 (equal freedoms), 21 (dignity and autonomy), 39(d) (equal pay) and 42 (maternity protection). Courts have evolved gender justice through constitutional interpretation
simplified_explanation: Constitutional gender justice has expanded through: Vishaka (workplace harassment), Navtej Johar (sexual orientation), Joseph Shine (adultery), Shayara Bano (triple talaq), Mary Roy (inheritance), Delhi Domestic Violence judgment, maternity benefit cases, and right to safe abortion. Pending: marital rape criminalization, UCC, gender-neutral laws.
keywords: Gender Justice,Article 15,Women Rights,Constitutional Guarantee,Sexual Autonomy,Equality
landmark_case_1: Mary Roy v. State of Kerala
lc1_holding_summary: Intestate succession among Indian Christians governed by Indian Succession Act; daughters have equal rights — gender justice applied to personal law
landmark_case_2: Lt. Col. Nitisha v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Women army officers entitled to permanent commission; indirect discrimination violates gender equality under Articles 14 and 15
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Right to Health as a Fundamental Right under Article 21
verbatim_text_excerpt: The right to health is an integral facet of a meaningful right to life under Article 21. Denial of timely medical treatment is a violation of the right to life. The State has an obligation to provide emergency medical treatment regardless of financial capacity
simplified_explanation: The SC has expanded Article 21 to include: right to emergency medical treatment (Paschim Banga case 1996), right to a pollution-free environment (Subhash Kumar 1991), right to safe food (Consumer Education case 1995), right to reproductive health (Suchita Srivastava case 2009), and right to mental health (The Mental Healthcare Act 2017 — only country with a statutory right to mental health care).
keywords: Right to Health,Article 21,Emergency Treatment,Mental Health,Reproductive Health,Clean Environment
landmark_case_1: Paschim Banga Khet Mazdoor Samity v. State of West Bengal
lc1_holding_summary: Right to emergency medical treatment is a Fundamental Right; State must ensure emergency healthcare at all public hospitals
landmark_case_2: Suchita Srivastava v. Chandigarh Administration
lc2_holding_summary: Right to reproductive choice including abortion is a Fundamental Right under Article 21; mental health patient's choice must be respected
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change

article_section: Death Penalty — Constitutional Validity and Rarest of Rare
title: Death Penalty — Constitutional Framework and Rarest of Rare Doctrine
verbatim_text_excerpt: The death penalty is constitutionally valid when imposed for the most heinous crimes under the 'rarest of rare' doctrine. The sentencing court must consider both aggravating and mitigating circumstances. Life imprisonment is the rule; death penalty the exception — applied only when the alternative of life imprisonment is unquestionably foreclosed
simplified_explanation: The Bachan Singh case (1980) — 4:1 majority — upheld death penalty as constitutional but restricted it to 'rarest of rare' cases. Machhi Singh (1983) laid down criteria: manner of commission, motive, anti-social nature, extent of crime, victim's vulnerability. Wrongful imposition of death penalty is judicially reviewed. Mercy petition procedure under Articles 72 and 161.
keywords: Death Penalty,Rarest of Rare,Bachan Singh,Capital Punishment,Aggravating Mitigating Factors,Article 21
landmark_case_1: Bachan Singh v. State of Punjab
lc1_holding_summary: Death penalty upheld as constitutional 4:1; restricted to rarest of rare cases where life imprisonment is unquestionably foreclosed
landmark_case_2: Shatrughan Chauhan v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Death row prisoners have rights; inordinate delay in execution is a mitigating factor; mercy petitions must be decided expeditiously
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Judicial Accountability — Impeachment, Contempt, and Transparency
verbatim_text_excerpt: Judges are accountable through: (1) Impeachment procedure under Articles 124(4) and 218 — has never succeeded in India; (2) Contempt powers as a tool of accountability for litigants; (3) Asset disclosure — SC judges disclose assets voluntarily; (4) Collegium resolutions published since 2017; (5) RTI application to SC — SC held it is a public authority but certain information is exempt
simplified_explanation: No SC or HC judge has ever been successfully impeached. Justice Ramaswami faced impeachment proceedings in 1993 but was not removed as Congress MPs walked out. Justice Soumitra Sen and Justice P.D. Dinakaran resigned before impeachment. The Judges (Inquiry) Act 1968 governs impeachment procedure for judges.
keywords: Judicial Accountability,Impeachment,Contempt of Court,Asset Disclosure,Collegium,Transparency
landmark_case_1: Supreme Court v. Subramanian Swamy
lc1_holding_summary: SC is a public authority under RTI Act; certain judicial communications protected but basic transparency required
landmark_case_2: Campaign for Judicial Accountability v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Collegium recommendations must be published; judicial transparency is a constitutional requirement
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

title: Uniform Civil Code — Article 44 and the Constitutional Debate
verbatim_text_excerpt: Article 44 directs the State to endeavour to secure a Uniform Civil Code for all citizens. Currently different personal laws govern marriage, divorce, inheritance, and adoption for Hindus, Muslims, Christians, and Parsis. The UCC debate involves balancing national unity, gender equality, and religious freedom under Articles 25-26
simplified_explanation: Arguments for UCC: gender justice (Muslim women's divorce rights), national integration, simplification of personal law. Arguments against: Minority rights under Article 30, religious freedom under Articles 25-26, diversity as constitutional value. Several SC judgments have called on Parliament to enact UCC. Uttarakhand enacted a state UCC in 2024 — first state to do so after Goa.
keywords: Uniform Civil Code,UCC,Article 44,Personal Law,Muslim Law,Hindu Law,Uttarakhand UCC 2024
landmark_case_1: Sarla Mudgal v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: SC strongly urged Parliament to enact UCC; conversion to Islam for second marriage without divorce from first is void
landmark_case_2: Shayara Bano v. Union of India
lc2_holding_summary: Instant triple talaq struck down — incremental personal law reform even without full UCC; gender justice takes priority
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: Freedom of Religion — Essential Religious Practices
title: Essential Religious Practices Test
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Supreme Court applies the 'Essential Religious Practices' (ERP) test to determine which religious practices are protected under Articles 25 and 26. Only practices essential to a religion are protected — not all practices claimed by followers. The court decides what is 'essential' — a controversial approach
simplified_explanation: The ERP test has been used to: allow entry of women of menstruating age into Sabarimala temple (Indu Malhotra J dissented); allow entry of women into Haji Ali dargah; restrict 'animal sacrifice' claims; determine whether triple talaq is an essential Islamic practice (SC said no). Critics argue that courts should not determine what is essential to a religion.
keywords: Essential Religious Practices,ERP Test,Article 25,Article 26,Sabarimala,Religious Freedom
landmark_case_1: Indian Young Lawyers Association v. State of Kerala (Sabarimala)
lc1_holding_summary: Women's right to equality under Article 14 and 15 prevails over claimed religious custom; exclusion of women is not an essential religious practice — 4:1 majority
landmark_case_2: Durgah Committee Ajmer v. Syed Hussain Ali
lc2_holding_summary: Practices essential to religion are protected; but superstitious or non-essential practices can be regulated by State
nodal_ministry: Ministry of Law and Justice / Respective Ministry

article_section: Media Law and Freedom of Press
title: Freedom of Press — Constitutional Framework and Limits
verbatim_text_excerpt: Freedom of the press flows from Article 19(1)(a) — freedom of speech and expression. Press is not explicitly mentioned but SC has consistently held that press freedom is a fundamental freedom. Reasonable restrictions under Article 19(2) apply to press just as to individual speech — sovereignty, public order, decency, defamation, contempt of court
simplified_explanation: India has no separate Press Act. Key press-related laws: Press and Registration of Periodicals Act 2023 (replaced 1867 Act), Cable TV Networks Regulation Act, IT Rules 2021 (Digital Media), Contempt of Courts Act 1971, Defamation (BNS Sections 499-500, now BNS). India ranks poorly in World Press Freedom Index — multiple journalist arrests under UAPA, IT Act, and defamation.
keywords: Freedom of Press,Article 19(1)(a),Media Law,Pre-censorship,Sedition,UAPA,Journalist Arrest
landmark_case_1: Indian Express Newspapers v. Union of India
lc1_holding_summary: Press freedom includes right to import newsprint; customs duties that undermine press viability violate Article 19(1)(a)
landmark_case_2: Romesh Thappar v. State of Madras
lc2_holding_summary: Freedom of press is integral to democracy; pre-censorship is unconstitutional without specific constitutional authority

verbatim_text_excerpt: Key UPSC Polity topics: Basic Structure Doctrine (Kesavananda Bharati 1973), Preamble (Socialist, Secular, Democratic, Republic, Sovereign), Parts of Constitution (22 Parts, 448 Articles, 12 Schedules), Writs (5 types), Emergency (352, 356, 360), Fundamental Rights (Articles 12-35), DPSPs (Articles 36-51), Fundamental Duties (Article 51A — 11 duties), Parliamentary Procedures (Money Bill, Budget, Joint Sitting), Constitutional Amendments (368 procedure), Federal Features vs Unitary Features

verbatim_text_excerpt: The Election Commission of India is the constitutional authority entrusted with the superintendence, direction and control of elections to Parliament, State Legislatures, and the offices of President and Vice President.
simplified_explanation: Central constitutional authority for free and fair elections. It administers electoral rolls, schedules, polling, counting, model code enforcement, party registration, symbols, and election disputes within the constitutional framework.

title: Central Bureau of Investigation
verbatim_text_excerpt: The Central Bureau of Investigation is the premier investigative agency functioning under the Department of Personnel and the Ministry of Personnel, Public Grievances and Pensions; it derives core powers through the Delhi Special Police Establishment Act, 1946.

title: Sita Soren v. Union of India (2024)
verbatim_text_excerpt: Members of Parliament and State Legislatures cannot claim immunity under Articles 105(2) and 194(2) of the Constitution for taking bribes in connection with their votes or speeches in the House.
simplified_explanation: Overruled the 1998 PV Narasimha Rao judgment. A historic 7-Judge bench held that bribery destroys the foundation of parliamentary democracy and MPs/MLAs are not immune from criminal prosecution if they take bribes.

title: Mineral Area Development Authority v. Steel Authority of India (2024)
verbatim_text_excerpt: The power to tax mineral rights rests with the States under Entry 50 of List II. Royalty paid to the Centre under the MMDR Act is not a tax.
simplified_explanation: A massive 9-Judge bench ruling affirming that State Governments have the constitutional authority to levy taxes on mineral-rich lands, and the Centre's MMDR Act does not strip them of this power.

title: Sub-classification of SC/STs (2024)
verbatim_text_excerpt: State legislatures have the legislative competence to sub-classify Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes to grant separate quotas for more marginalized groups within the SC/ST categories.
